name: Ethics Dilemma

rationale: A genuinely excellent staff CPD tool: four fully-authored branching de-escalation scenarios with a live escalation meter, layered "why this matters" feedback grounded in accurate practice (affect labelling, window of tolerance, co-regulation, trauma- and attachment-aware approaches), an expert-path debrief, and a custom canvas escalation graph with screen-reader aria descriptions. Interactivity is real and consequential, the design is clean and responsive, and there are no obvious bugs; alignment is strong professional fitness rather than 5 because outcomes hinge on a single escalation score and scenarios converge regardless of early choices, slightly flattening cause-and-effect.
subject: Behaviour & Pastoral
name: De-escalation Trainer

rationale: An exceptional CPD branching simulator: three realistic, distinct scenarios map cleanly onto the six-stage restorative framework and the standard restorative questions, with quality-tiered choices and genuinely instructive, misconception-aware coach notes (penalising "why" questions, shaming-by-numbers, imposed apologies and threats). Immediate meaningful feedback, trust/quality/emotion tracking, randomised options and a rich diagnostic summary make it a polished, professionally credible staff-training tool; only modest technical limits (mobile sidebar hidden, fixed timings, no keyboard nav, cosmetic trust meter) hold it back from a perfect score.
subject: Behaviour & Pastoral
name: Restorative Conversation

rationale: A genuinely strong pastoral/RSHE tool: the CBT cognitive-triangle model is accurately implemented as an interactive trigger-thought-feeling-behaviour-consequence cycle with clickable SVG arcs, drag-and-drop, custom input, a working cascade engine (thought to feeling to behaviour), body sensations, feeling-specific coping strategies, reframes and reflection. Robust, responsive, keyboard-accessible and clean, with the demo-data modal correctly present; alignment is to RSHE rather than a core NC subject so pitched at one point below full.
subject: Behaviour & Pastoral
name: Self-Regulation Cycle Visualiser

rationale: An exceptionally well-built EYFS rhyme-detection game: spoken words via SpeechSynthesis (essential for pre-readers), and feedback that genuinely models the shared rime on a match and contrasts the differing endings on an error, making it misconception-aware. Accessible (ARIA, keyboard, aria-live), responsive, and robust; the only weakness is that rime labels and matching are spelling-based rather than truly phonemic for what is an oracy skill.
subject: English
name: Rhyming Pairs Match

rationale: A genuinely excellent A-Level MFL tool: students self-annotate authentic literary extracts (Camus, Lorca) for grammar, style and theme, then compare their classified analysis against accurate, exam-pitched examiner model notes, with a separate model-annotation mode, keyboard-accessible tooltips and correctly language-tagged read-aloud. Pitch, accuracy and cognitive demand are all spot-on for KS5; only minor limits (two languages, shallow auto-scoring, substring-only matching) hold technical short of a 5.
subject: Modern Foreign Languages
name: Literary Text Analysis

rationale: A genuinely strong KS2 reasoning tool: a structured table workspace with live valid/duplicate/incomplete status, exhaustive solution sets, "Am I Done?" checking and a reveal of missed possibilities directly teaches systematic enumeration across five well-pitched contexts (numbers, coins, arrangements, area/perimeter, magic shapes). Maths is accurate and the enumeration logic is sound; the logic-grid category is weaker because it only marks one arrangement right or wrong rather than supporting the "find all" enumeration the rest of the app models so well, and inputs lack proper labels.
subject: Maths
name: Working Systematically

rationale: A genuinely excellent KS1 reading game: predict-then-justify with clickable inference clues, partner-talk oracy prompts and a retry-on-wrong loop map precisely onto KS1 comprehension (prediction and inference) at the right pitch. Robust, accessible (ARIA, live regions, keyboard support) and cleanly designed; only minor blemish is the generic data-acknowledgement modal that talks about "grades and attendance figures" irrelevant to a fictional story.
subject: English
name: What Happens Next?

rationale: Excellent, expertly designed KS2 reading tool: dragging an inference onto the exact proving words then rating evidence strength directly targets the National Curriculum "justify inferences with evidence" skill, with multi-span cards, misconception-aware feedback and a genuine reflection step. Robust drag/tap/keyboard interaction and strong accessibility; only minor weakness is that the strength-rating rewards every answer unconditionally so it does not correct a poor judgement.
subject: English
name: Evidence Highlighter

rationale: A genuinely excellent KS2 spoken-language tool: pupils build a claim/evidence/rebuttal map by drag or keyboard, with misconception-aware distractors (bandwagon, unsupported assertion, ad hominem) and differentiated "right type but weak" vs "strong" feedback, culminating in an auto-generated speech and courteous-challenge stems. Technically robust and accessible, marked down only one point for a vestigial mislabelled motion id and the absence of any persistence/print-out.
subject: English
name: Debate Builder

rationale: A genuinely strong KS3 reading task: the three-layer design (form, period, then a misconception-aware convention check with deliberate distractors and a ballad-vs-sonnet trap) demands real discrimination rather than label-matching, and it maps cleanly onto the KS3 programme of study for reading across genres and periods. Feedback is granular and accurate, the build is robust, responsive and notably accessible (skip link, aria-live, radiogroup roles).
subject: English
name: Genre and Period Map

rationale: A polished, accessible drag-and-drop (plus full keyboard path) that builds a live viewpoint/method comparison grid precisely targeting KS4 Paper 2 AO1 synthesis and AO3 comparison, with theme-specific feedback and an exam-style closing stem. Pedagogy is strong but slightly capped because pupils match pre-written ideas and methods rather than generating synthesis or identifying methods themselves, so it scaffolds toward AO3 rather than fully training it.
subject: English
name: Viewpoint Comparator

rationale: A genuinely excellent KS5 English Language tool: pupils tag authentic spoken/written/historic data by the five A-level frameworks and get layered, misconception-aware feedback that distinguishes wrong framework from right-framework-but-weak-effect, mirroring real mark-scheme priorities. Accurate linguistics, strong accessibility (keyboard tagging and keyboard-driven drag), and a complementary language-change timeline; only minor flaws (substring keyword matching can be gamed, a thin 4-item timeline, no persistence) keep technical short of full marks.
subject: English
name: Framework Annotator (English KS5)

rationale: A genuinely excellent KS5 English Language CLA tool: drag-drop placement gates theory reveals behind correct answers and gives each theory's reading of the specific utterance, supporting argument-from-data rather than rote labelling, with accurate stages, ages and theorists and a working keyboard-accessible path. Technical is strong but not flawless: the generic demo-data modal copy ("student records, grades, attendance") is mismatched to this app and the pointer drag lacks a native DnD fallback.
subject: English
name: Acquisition Stages Explorer

rationale: A genuinely excellent, misconception-aware A-level NEA planning tool: two modes with weighted live diagnostics, an honest criteria explainer, an ethics planner and a gated sign-off that accurately reflect real NEA conventions (word count, comparability, language frameworks, data ethics). Only minor flaws: the analytical-framing check is semi-tautological because the tool auto-injects "Compare how...present", and the score thresholds are a touch lenient.
subject: English
name: NEA Question Forge

rationale: A genuinely strong KS2 place value tool: four distinct, hands-on activities (digit-by-digit building with x10/÷10 shifts, a snapping rounding number line, a counting-across-zero thermometer, and a generated quiz) that map accurately onto Y5/Y6 number and place value objectives, with misconception-aware feedback (digit cap of 9 and carrying, ÷10 requiring a zero ones digit, the half-way rounding rule, Roman numeral limits) and solid accessibility. Marked down a point only on technical polish: the x10/÷10 shift is re-rendered rather than animated so the digit movement is told more than shown, and a couple of distractor-generation edge cases are loosely guarded.
subject: Maths
name: Place Value Slider

rationale: Three coherent, misconception-aware activities (logarithmic zoom linking ordinary/standard form, an index-law comparison drill with well-chosen distractors and worked feedback, and a percentage-multiplier machine) that map precisely onto KS3 number expectations and are mathematically accurate. Build quality is strong (responsive grids, roving-tabindex tab nav, aria-live, focus-visible), with only minor rough edges in large-number rendering and the brittle aria-label-based answer matching.
subject: Maths
name: Standard Form Zoom

rationale: A polished, genuinely misconception-aware KS3 ratio tool: bidirectionally synced ratio table, scale-factor slider and draggable/keyboard double number line, with targeted diagnostics that name specific errors (additive thinking, wrong-direction scaling, treating inverse as direct). Accurate maths, strong NC alignment to the ratio/proportion strand, accessible and responsive; the only weaknesses are minor (mismatched boilerplate demo-modal text and a coarse 0.25 slider step).
subject: Maths
name: Ratio Table Tool

rationale: Excellent KS4 tool that links a repeated-multiplier chain to its exponential curve with a live draggable rate, growth/decay toggle and hover readouts, and a genuinely misconception-aware challenge engine that distinguishes simple-interest and single-year errors. Maths is accurate and the build is robust and accessible; the only weakness is the bolted-on compound-measures converter, which shares only the word "compound" and slightly dilutes the otherwise tight focus on the ratio/proportion/rates-of-change strand.
subject: Maths
name: Compound Growth Plotter

rationale: A genuinely strong KS5 Core Maths tool: live sliders driving a take-home-pay breakdown and an SVG nominal-vs-real chart, a working-shown scenario quiz, and statistical-literacy tasks (Fermi estimation, misleading-claim spotting), all with accurate 2024-25 tax/NI/Plan 5 bands and pointed misconception feedback. Clean, accessible (ARIA tabs, keyboard nav, aria-live), robust code with no obvious bugs.
subject: Maths
name: Personal Finance Modeller

rationale: A genuinely strong, misconception-aware tool: the live formality dial concretely shows how register shifts vocabulary, contractions, greetings and sign-offs, with labelled change-mappings and a graded match-the-register check that teaches the formality gradient directionally. Maps squarely onto the KS3 National Curriculum requirement to write for varied purposes and audiences using Standard English, and accessibility is well handled (aria-live, aria-valuetext, focus styles).
subject: English
name: Register Dial

rationale: An excellent, misconception-aware KS2 Earth and Space model: drag/play/keyboard-controlled canvases for day-night, an accurate eight-phase Moon maker, and a quiz whose feedback explains the science, all mapping cleanly onto the Year 5 National Curriculum. Marked down only on technical polish: the boilerplate "student records, names, grades, attendance" safeguarding modal is contextually nonsensical for an astronomy sim with no child data.
subject: Science
name: Spin the Earth

rationale: A genuinely strong working-scientifically planner: pupils sort factors into change/measure/control, get precise misconception-aware feedback (e.g. flags more than one variable changed, explains why controls matter), then read an auto-plotted chart, complete a data table and answer an evidence quiz including a negative-relationship scenario (sugar) that pre-empts the "bigger bar = better" error. Maps cleanly onto the KS2 working-scientifically programme at the right pitch with accurate, internally consistent data.
subject: Science
name: Fair Test Designer

rationale: A genuinely strong KS3 chemistry simulator covering states of matter, gas pressure and diffusion with live particle animation, multiple input methods (buttons, sliders, canvas drag, keyboard), misconception-aware explanatory feedback and per-mode quizzes that map accurately onto the England KS3 particle-model curriculum. Loses one technical point for minor model-fidelity quibbles (solid lattice vibration uses per-frame random jitter rather than smooth oscillation; gas does not fully fill the container) plus the boilerplate "student records/grades/attendance" demo modal that is irrelevant to a science tool.
subject: Science
name: Particle Theatre

rationale: Excellent KS3 physics tool that targets the core mass-vs-weight misconception head-on: a live W=m×g readout, gravity-proportional drop animation across five worlds, and a self-marking challenge with misconception-aware feedback (detects writing the mass, dividing, or wrongly using Earth's g). Accurate values, strong NC alignment, and a genuinely accessible, responsive build.
subject: Science
name: Weight on Other Worlds

rationale: A genuinely excellent KS3 "working scientifically" enquiry tool: it sequences plan, apparatus, data with real random-error simulation and anomaly handling, plotting with line of best fit and error bars, interpolation and reliability/precision evaluation, all misconception-aware and accurately pitched to the England KS3 curriculum. Marked down one point on technical only because the anomaly threshold rarely fires against the modelled error spread (weakening that learning moment) and the injected demo-data modal references "names, grades, attendance" that do not match a science app.
subject: Science
name: Investigation Studio

rationale: A genuinely model-driven KS4 homeostasis simulator: a working negative-feedback loop generates accurate, distinct glucose curves for healthy, Type 1 and Type 2 bodies, with live graph, hormone bars, pathway narration and a time-in-range challenge that all map cleanly onto the GCSE "Homeostasis and response" content. Technical quality is high but not flawless: the live readout and graph are aria-hidden so screen-reader users miss the changing glucose value, and the injected-insulin mechanism relies on a slightly hacky step-wrapper.
subject: Science
name: Blood Sugar Balance

rationale: An excellent, accurate KS4 quantitative-chemistry tool: three linked panels (mole converter, live equation balancer with atom-by-atom feedback and simplest-ratio GCD check, reacting-mass/percentage-yield with full shown working) that target real misconceptions (over-100% yield as impurity, non-lowest coefficients, yield losses). Correct Mr values, correctly balanced reactions, good ARIA/keyboard tab handling; only minor technical nitpicks keep it off a perfect score.
subject: Science
name: Mole Calculator Lab

rationale: A genuinely excellent KS4 electrolysis simulator with accurate chemistry, full spec coverage (molten and aqueous, cathode reactivity rule, anode halide rule, correct half-equations), predict-before-reveal scoring, responsive ion animation and strong canvas accessibility. Marked down slightly on alignment because the energy-profile section frames electrolysis as simply endothermic with products higher in energy, a defensible but somewhat conflated GCSE simplification that sits loosely beside the core cell activity.
subject: Science
name: Electrolysis Cell

rationale: An excellent, accurate KS4 Chemistry lab that links chromatography/Rf, flame tests, gas tests and atmospheric change with genuine canvas manipulation (animated capillary run, snap-to-feature measurement, draggable/keyboard line) and misconception-aware feedback (Rf cannot exceed 1, spot cannot pass the solvent front). Only a minor scoring bug holds back full marks: the "/ 8" denominator is wrong since there are 9 correct identifications (rf + 4 flames + 4 gases) plus a trivially-awarded "atmo" point, so the counter can display values like "9 / 8".
subject: Science
name: Chromatography and Tests

rationale: An excellent, misconception-aware A-level Biology simulator: the slider lets pupils prove the all-or-nothing principle, and differentiated feedback for sub-threshold, full-spike and refractory firing turns abstract electrophysiology into genuine inquiry, with accurate, appropriately-simplified KS5 content and strong accessibility. Marked down only on technical for the incongruous boilerplate demo-data modal (about student grades/attendance) that does not fit a physiology tool.
subject: Science
name: Action Potential Lab

rationale: An accurate, misconception-aware KS5 kinetics tool: correct MB form (sqrt(E)exp(-E/RT)), area conserved on heating, peak at RT/2, catalyst lowering Ea while leaving the curve unchanged, plus a draggable Ea line with keyboard/ARIA support, live readouts, contextual feedback and a graded rate challenge. The only blemish is that the displayed "fraction able to react" (numeric integral) and "relative rate" (pure Boltzmann factor) are computed by different methods and so are not strictly self-consistent.
subject: Science
name: Maxwell-Boltzmann Explorer

rationale: A polished, genuinely interactive EYFS history app: children sort six story clues into "Long ago" vs "Today" rooms and get child-friendly explanations of why each shows the past, mapping precisely onto the EYFS Understanding the World "Past and Present" ELG and the KS1 history foundations. Three robust input modes (drag, tap, keyboard) with aria-live feedback, sound technical handling, and clean responsive design; only the single fixed story and modest cognitive ceiling hold pedagogy short of a 5.
subject: History
name: Story Time Traveller

rationale: A genuinely strong KS1 local-history enquiry: the before/after wipe slider and five tappable pins deliver real manipulation with focused "then/now/changed-and-stayed-the-same" feedback that hits the very heart of the KS1 history aim, with a misconception-aware emphasis on continuity as well as change. Technically robust and accessible (pointer + range + keyboard control, ARIA slider, aria-live feedback), losing one mark only for fixed pin positions that overlap (cobbles/transport at 86/88%) and sit on whichever scene the slider happens to reveal.
subject: History
name: Our Place Then and Now

rationale: A genuinely strong KS2 change-and-continuity tool: real drag-and-drop plus a full keyboard move mode, sorting-versus-verdict feedback that names misplaced cards, and a deliberate continuity card to surface the "everything changed" misconception. Accessibility (live regions, aria updates, focus management) is unusually thorough; the only soft spot is a slightly prescriptive single "historian's verdict" that narrows an interpretive judgement.
subject: History
name: Turning Point Investigator

rationale: A genuinely excellent KS3 History resource: four distinct interaction modes (accessible drag-and-drop pyramid build with targeted misconception feedback, an obligations ripple explorer, an animated Black Death scarcity simulation tying labour shortage to wages, the Statute of Labourers and the 1381 revolt, and an explained quiz) all accurately pitched to the "Church, state and society 1066-1509" strand. Technical execution is robust and accessible, with only minor blemishes (the generic demo banner mentions grades/attendance irrelevant to this app, and "barons & bishops"/"king owned all the land" are light simplifications).
subject: History
name: The Feudal Pyramid Builder

rationale: A historically rich, accurate KS3 Reformation decision simulation that genuinely models the multi-causal drivers (money, heirs, faith, power) with stat meters, an animated monastery-dissolution SVG, misconception-correcting historian notes and a branching verdict. Polished, accessible and well-aligned to the England NC Early Modern unit; only minor blemishes (an irrelevant "demonstration data" student-records modal and a quasi-linear rather than fully branching structure) keep it just short of perfect.
subject: History
name: Break with Rome: Henry VIII's Dilemma

rationale: A genuinely excellent KS3 sandbox that models cause/consequence, change over time and significance: pupils build a town, watch pollution and child-labour dials respond, then pass historically accurate reforms (1832/1833/1842/1847/1848) gated by crisis conditions, with a separate triangular-trade task tying industry to empire and abolition. Robust pointer-and-keyboard interaction, ARIA live feedback, and substantive teacher notes; only minor blemish is the population gauge bar capping visually at 100% while the displayed value keeps climbing.
subject: History
name: Industrial Town Machine

rationale: An exceptionally well-designed KS4 GCSE period-study tool: the synchronised economy/votes/violence chart makes the "prosperity kills extremism, collapse feeds it" pattern visible, and the causal-weighting tester with tailored evidence feedback directly targets the AQA/Edexcel "how did Hitler come to power" question while pre-empting the classic monocausal-Depression misconception. Historically sound, genuinely manipulable, with strong accessibility (ARIA live regions, keyboard chart navigation, focus styling).
subject: History
name: Weimar to Nazi Germany: Power Tracker

rationale: A genuinely strong KS4 thematic-study tool: chronological sequencing of eight accurately-dated developments is layered with a "factor lens" that surfaces the driver/brake analytical thinking GCSE rewards, with specific directional feedback, full drag-and-keyboard parity, ARIA support and clean editorial design. Falls just short of perfect on technical only because of necessary simplifications (single driver/brake per development, rounded dates), which the footer honestly flags.
subject: History
name: Medicine Through Time Timeline

rationale: A genuinely strong KS5 tool that scaffolds the A-level History NEA accurately: four gatekeeper diagnostics (debate, source availability, 100-year context, judgement framing) map onto real coursework requirements, with live re-running feedback, an editable source basket enforcing primary breadth and historiographical engagement, and a sensible word budget plus exportable brief. The keyword/regex heuristics occasionally misfire or can be gamed (e.g. crude capitalised-word "named focus" detection), slightly capping technical robustness, but design, accessibility and responsiveness are solid.
subject: History
name: NEA Question Forge

rationale: Genuinely strong KS1 place-knowledge app: a same/different/both Venn sort with three input modalities (drag, tap, full keyboard), per-card misconception-aware feedback, and accessible markup. The only weakness is alignment: the contrasting locality is a generic fictional "hot place far away in Africa", which flattens an entire continent into a single hot-and-sandy stereotype, a recognised KS1 misconception the NC asks teachers to avoid.
subject: Geography
name: Here and There Compare

rationale: A genuinely strong KS2 human-geography app: pupils place houses, shops, farms and a river bridge on a hand-authored terrain grid, and a live model converts the real "why settlements grow near water, food, flat land and crossing points" reasoning into per-placement feedback, happiness/trade scores and a full diagnostic on Check, with misconception-aware steers (e.g. farms on hills, houses far from water). Maps cleanly onto the NC human-geography strand at the right pitch; interaction is robust (pointer place/drag plus full keyboard grid navigation, aria-live feedback), with only minor accessibility gaps (emoji-only building markers, no SVG scaling cap on very small screens) holding technical short of a 5.
subject: Geography
name: Build a Settlement

rationale: A genuinely strong KS4 resource-management sim: a coherent supply/demand-ratio model drives scarcity and conflict indices, with drought lowering reservoir/aquifer reliable yield and transfers/over-abstraction raising tension, plus a budget cap that forces real trade-offs and contextual, misconception-aware feedback (physical vs economic scarcity, sustainability). Accurate and well-pitched to AQA/Edexcel water-security specs, accessible and robust; only minor blemish is the generic acknowledgement modal text referencing irrelevant "grades/attendance" boilerplate.
subject: Geography
name: Water Security Simulator

rationale: An exceptionally well-aligned KS5 Changing Places tool whose five sequenced steps (layered data, perspective lens, misconception-aware insider/outsider sort with targeted feedback, live-marked synthesis) deliver genuine cognitive demand against the exact A-level specification content. Technically robust and accessible (keyboard/touch drag fallback, aria-live, focus styles), with only minor blemishes: keyword-regex synthesis marking is gameable and the boilerplate demo modal text references children's records that this app never shows.
subject: Geography
name: Place Profiler

rationale: Genuinely strong KS1 RE app: question-composition, adaptive sorting feedback, and multi-tradition exploration map cleanly onto enquiry-based RE (big questions, learning from religion) at the right pitch, with thoughtful handling that big questions have no single answer and user-composed notes are accepted with reasoning prompts rather than marked wrong. Three modes of real manipulation with meaningful immediate feedback, plus keyboard-accessible drag-and-drop, ARIA live regions and HTML escaping; loses a point on technical polish only because pointer-drag relies on elementFromPoint/setPointerCapture which can be fiddly on some touch devices and there is no visible focus path into newly posted notes.
subject: Religious Education
name: Wonder Wall

rationale: A theologically accurate, misconception-aware KS2 RE matching game whose per-option "why" feedback explicitly teaches the fine distinctions (zakah vs sadaqah, tzedakah as justice, seva vs dana) that pupils typically confuse, with excellent accessibility, keyboard support and clean editorial design. Interactivity is strong but capped because each act is effectively a 3-option multiple choice rather than genuine drag-matching across a wider value bank.
subject: Religious Education
name: Faith in Action

rationale: An accurate, intellectually demanding KS5 Philosophy of Religion tool that maps Swinburne's credulity/testimony and defeaters onto three well-chosen experience types plus a verification/falsification/analogy language section, with genuine manipulation (sliders, scored defeater checkboxes, model-comparison feedback). Technical execution is clean and reasonably accessible, held back only by somewhat arbitrary scoring and a generic safeguarding modal whose wording (child records, grades, attendance) is a poor fit for a philosophy app.
subject: Religious Education
name: Is This Experience Veridical?

rationale: An exceptionally well-conceived KS5 RS meta-ethics tool: a genuine branching Socratic dialogue with logic-aware graded feedback, a tolerance-scored drag/keyboard classification grid, and a combinatorial free-will argument builder that surfaces internal tensions, all accurately pitched at A-level and accessible. Technical execution is robust but not flawless (empty pointermove handler, DCT/intuitionism grid regions overlap), holding it just shy of perfect.
subject: Religious Education
name: Euthyphro and Beyond

rationale: A genuinely excellent A Level RS tool: accurate thinker placement (Hick, Rahner, Barth, Nostra Aetate, extra ecclesiam) plus a scenario stress-test whose feedback responds to the scenario-model combination with real nuance (e.g. pluralism's weakness on conflicting truth-claims, citing D'Costa), and a critical text-reading section showing how the same verses fund rival positions. Strong accessibility (ARIA slider, keyboard control, live regions).
subject: Religious Education
name: Pluralism Positions

rationale: Genuinely excellent KS3 MFL speaking tool: spontaneity slider scales curveball difficulty, a category-coded reply builder (openers/opinions/connectives/stalling phrases) targets exactly how fluent speakers cope under pressure, and the accurate three-language content maps precisely onto the KS3 requirement to initiate, develop and recover conversations. Strong accessibility and responsive design; only minor limit is that feedback grades phrase categories rather than actual linguistic correctness.
subject: Modern Foreign Languages
name: Conversation Roulette

rationale: A genuinely strong KS5 Drama resource: accurate, well-pitched content on five movements with a clever "one moment, five styles" comparison that builds the comparative evaluation A-level students need, plus a randomised quiz with tailored feedback. Clean, accessible build (ARIA tablist, keyboard nav, focus management, live regions) with no obvious bugs.
subject: Drama
name: Theatre Timeline Explorer

rationale: An exemplary KS1 being-safe app: branching scenarios with forced reconsideration of the unsafe choice, an animated feelings meter visualising relief, a secrets-vs-surprises sort and a trusted-adults builder, all with immediate meaningful feedback. Content maps accurately and sensitively onto statutory RSHE "Being Safe" with NSPCC PANTS-style underwear-rule messaging; technically robust, responsive and accessible (ARIA live regions, aria-pressed, focus management).
subject: Behaviour & Pastoral
name: Tell a Trusted Adult

rationale: A genuinely excellent KS3 health-education tool: three distinct, well-scaffolded activities (weighted risk meter, short/long/myth sort, levelled refusal scenarios) with accurate content, explicit misconception-handling, meaningful tailored feedback, and unusually strong accessibility (keyboard sorting, aria-pressed states, live regions). Alignment is to statutory RSHE/Health Education and non-statutory PSHE rather than NC subject programmes of study, which is the correct home for this content but slightly off a pure NC mapping.
subject: Behaviour & Pastoral
name: Risk and Reality

rationale: A genuinely strong KS4 PSHE/RSHE tool: five realistic scenarios with a three-factor reward/risk/harm model, harm-led verdicts, a "biggest protective win" coach and lowest-harm solver that teach harm reduction without abstinence-only lecturing, all accurately grounded in CMO/RSHE/PSHE guidance. Loses one mark on technical polish because the live gauges are aria-hidden and the computed "Risk" value never actually feeds the verdict, leaving the displayed model slightly more sophisticated than the underlying logic.
subject: Behaviour & Pastoral
name: Risk Trade-Off Engine

rationale: A genuinely strong KS4 careers/economic-wellbeing tool: misconception-aware, person-specific feedback with route-fit scoring, trade-off meters and a pin-to-compare discussion task that demands justification, all built on accurate England post-16 facts (45-day T-level placement, duty to stay in education to 18, earn-while-learn). Robust engineering with HTML-escaping, aria-live feedback, focus-visible styling and full keyboard parity for the drag-and-drop.
subject: Behaviour & Pastoral
name: Pathways Planner

rationale: A genuinely excellent KS5 RSE app: four well-written case studies on consent, commitment, conflict and parenting, each with a defensible healthiest-to-most-harmful ranking, per-response "why" feedback, banded accuracy scoring and a debate prompt. Accessible drag plus keyboard reordering with live announcements, misconception-aware content (compliance is not consent, reproductive coercion named as abusive) mapping accurately to statutory post-16 RSE.
subject: Behaviour & Pastoral
name: Healthy Adult Relationships

rationale: Excellent KS5 pastoral app: a two-stage judge-then-justify model across 12 realistic, varied adult-world scams with genuine-safe distractors, misconception-aware feedback (e.g. misinformation correctly routed to pause-and-check, "a deadline alone is not a scam sign"), and accurate UK reporting routes. Interactivity is real and meaningful (locked verdicts, shuffled clues, per-flag reveal, streak/trust/score, graded outcome) and accessibility is well handled; only minor dead-code clutter keeps technical off a 5.
subject: Behaviour & Pastoral
name: Spot the Scam

rationale: A genuinely excellent EYFS oracy-through-art tool: children build an open-ended SVG scene from stamps, then a two-mode design turns each placed item into a randomised art-talk question with vocabulary chips and per-part progress tracking, mapping cleanly onto EYFS Expressive Arts and Design plus Communication and Language. Accessibility (ARIA live regions, keyboard focus rings, pointer-event parity) and clean responsive design are well above average; only minor gaps (no z-order control on overlapping stamps, no save) keep technical off a 5.
subject: Art and Design
name: My Picture Story

rationale: A genuinely excellent KS1 printmaking model: pupils carve a 10x10 block, ink it and press identical repeats, with the app's stage-aware feedback explicitly teaching the core idea that printing makes the same mark every time (versus hand-drawing variation), plus full keyboard/aria-live accessibility and misconception guards. Maps cleanly onto NC KS1 'using a range of materials creatively, including printmaking' at the right pitch; only minor technical blemishes (row-layout overflow when a row fills, bare emoji in JS strings) keep technical off a 5.
subject: Art and Design
name: Print Press

rationale: A well-conceived observational drawing tool whose Look-then-fade mechanic directly enacts the core KS1 art principle of drawing what you see rather than a remembered symbol, with genuine canvas interaction, real geometry-based outline-coverage scoring, staged feedback and full keyboard support. Accurately pitched to the KS1 Art and Design programme of study; only minor technical roughness (fixed-centre detail detection at 320,260 that does not match every object's centre, and a keyboard cursor that vanishes on re-render) keeps it off a perfect score.
subject: Art and Design
name: Observation Station: Look and Draw

rationale: A genuinely excellent one-point perspective tool: real procedural SVG that redraws buildings, road and guide lines receding to a draggable vanishing point, with misconception-aware live feedback, true tonal aerial-perspective fading, a mastery badge and a quiz. Drag, keyboard and slider input all work with strong ARIA accessibility; alignment is accurate to KS2 but slightly let down by an auto-completed "VP on horizon" checklist step and somewhat schematic building geometry.
subject: Art and Design
name: Vanishing Point

rationale: A genuinely strong drag-and-drop sorting task where pupils file eight sketchbook artefacts into the four assessment objectives and receive tailored, misconception-aware feedback for both correct and incorrect placements, with full keyboard access, ARIA live regions and clean responsive design. Alignment is the one soft spot: assessment objectives are formally a KS4/GCSE construct, and although the app honestly frames itself as GCSE-style preparation at KS3, that pitch sits slightly above the KS3 National Curriculum's own language.
subject: Art and Design
name: Project Journey

rationale: A genuinely strong tool that scaffolds the exact structure of a GCSE AO1 contextual study page (source investigation, formal analysis of qualities and artist intent, named technique to trial, explicit link to own intentions) and weights its 100-point check sensibly toward the connection-to-own-work that distinguishes copying from development, with misconception-aware feedback that calls out description-masquerading-as-analysis. Drag-and-drop plus keyboard placement, live feedback banding and accessible announcements are robust; the only real limit is the keyword-bank scoring, which can be gamed by sprinkling formal terms without genuine analysis.
subject: Art and Design
name: AO1 Research Builder

rationale: A well-conceived GCSE Art AO2 tool: constant-motif/varied-media trials judged against a stated intention, with feedback and a verdict that correctly grade the refinement decision (selectivity, breadth, annotation vocabulary) rather than the drawing itself. Genuinely interactive canvas with technique-specific feedback, accessible controls and clean design; only limited by the fact that the Fit score is a deterministic lookup that ignores the marks actually drawn.
subject: Art and Design
name: Experiment Lab (AO2 Media Trials)

rationale: Genuinely excellent: a timed observational drawing pad with four distinct study modes feeds into a prompted Observe-Technique-Reflect annotation engine that scores against eight AO3 checkpoints using keyword banks and returns specific, forward-looking feedback rather than a generic mark. The AO3 framing ("Record ideas, observations and insights relevant to intentions") is accurate to GCSE Art specs and pitched correctly for KS4, with strong keyboard accessibility and clean editorial design.
subject: Art and Design
name: Observation Logbook (KS4 Art)

rationale: Genuinely excellent KS4 tool: pupils build a rule-of-thirds final-piece mock-up from tagged studies and run a misconception-aware connection check that rewards focal-point strength, theme/originality and visible AO1-AO3 evidence while penalising crowded collages and weak or off-theme choices. Robust drag/touch/keyboard interaction with full ARIA live-region accessibility; alignment is to awarding-body GCSE AOs rather than NC programme-of-study text, accurate but with a minor under-statement of AO2 (refining).
subject: Art and Design
name: Final Piece Planner

rationale: Genuinely excellent KS4 Art tool: four sliders drive a live SVG re-render (real HSL hue interpolation, contrast maths, feTurbulence grain, scale transforms) paired with vocabulary-rich per-element critique, a composite mood model, and a self-marking brief/match-meter, all on-pitch for the formal elements and meaning-making at KS4. Strong accessibility (aria-live, updating sr-only text, focus-visible) with only minor edges in the mood-threshold tuning holding technical off a 5.
subject: Art and Design
name: Visual Language Studio

rationale: A genuinely strong KS5 tool that teaches the point-quotation-practice triangulation of the personal study, with deliberate weak/strong distractors targeting real misconceptions (description vs analysis, dropped-in vs embedded quotation, vague vs specific practice link) and a card-aware feedback engine, strength meter and prose preview. Robust, accessible drag-and-drop plus full keyboard carry-and-drop; only modest caveats are the schematic SVG artwork and finite templated feedback.
subject: Art and Design
name: Personal Study Workbench

rationale: A genuinely sophisticated KS5 tool that models the exact synthesis thinking A-level examiners reward, with two real interaction modes (keyboard-accessible drag clustering, then thread selection that builds a dynamic outcome sentence) and misconception-aware distractors that distinguish active synthesis from inert research. Robust, accessible and accurately pitched; only minor technical polish keeps it from a perfect score.
subject: Art and Design
name: Synthesis Studio (KS5 Art)

rationale: A genuinely excellent KS5 tool that targets the real "borrowed connection" weakness in A-level critical and contextual studies, with draggable/keyboard-accessible multi-axis plotting, distance-based scoring, and a claim-parser that tests whether a written link is actually defensible against marker position. Alignment is strong and honest about its teaching simplification, held just off a 5 only because the three-axis model and surname-only claim matching are necessarily reductive.
subject: Art and Design
name: Contemporary Compass

rationale: A polished, offline EYFS listening game where each clip is genuinely synthesised via Web Audio to embody its mood (tempo, scale, articulation, vibrato), faces lock until the child has listened, and feedback is misconception-aware and warm rather than a buzzer, with talk prompts linking sound to feeling. Strong accessibility (aria-live, screen-reader region, keyboard focus management) and clean responsive design; only a slight mark against alignment as "spooky/busy" are interpretive labels rather than core EYFS musical vocabulary.
subject: Music
name: How Does It Feel?

rationale: Genuine real-time pitch detection (autocorrelation over live mic) drives a caterpillar that climbs with pitch, with three well-scaffolded modes (free sing, match-and-hold for a star, call-and-response echo) that target the KS1 aim of singing tunefully and matching high/low pitch precisely. Pedagogically strong, NC-aligned, with instant directional feedback ("too high/too low"), a keyboard fallback for no-mic accessibility, and a teacher note; only minor technical caveats (sine-tone targets can be hard for children to pitch-match against, octave errors common to autocorrelation, and the mic loop runs continuously) hold it just short of a perfect technical score.
subject: Music
name: Pitch Caterpillar

rationale: A genuinely excellent KS1 graphic-score tool: the core concept (marks stand for sound, height maps to pitch, left-to-right maps to time) is taught clearly and made audible via Web Audio, with contextual feedback that names pitch direction and note length, three timbres, an immediate drawing blip, a sweeping playhead, and a thoughtful keyboard-composing route plus ARIA for non-mouse users. It maps squarely onto the KS1 music programme of study (experimenting with sound, using voices/instruments musically, and being introduced to symbols/notation) at the right pitch.
subject: Music
name: Draw Your Music

rationale: A genuinely excellent KS2 Music tool: an accurate Web Audio pentatonic improviser with a backing groove, recordable Melody Sketchpad with playback, and intelligent live feedback on step/leap, melodic direction and rhythm, all mapped explicitly to the inter-related dimensions of music. Only the setInterval-driven groove (prone to timing drift versus the Web Audio clock) keeps technical short of a 5.
subject: Music
name: Pentatonic Improviser

rationale: Outstanding KS2 listening tool: a live Web Audio engine lets pupils strip and layer real synthesised parts, with an immediate texture meter, vocabulary labels, a tempo/dynamics-shifting form mapper, and a self-setting quiz that always plays the correct example. Accurate to the KS2 Music curriculum (texture, timbre, dynamics, structure) and strongly accessible; only minor blemish is the generic "no real child/grades/attendance" demo banner text that doesn't suit a music app.
subject: Music
name: Texture Layers Explorer

rationale: Genuinely excellent note-reading game with three coherent modes, EGBDF/FACE mnemonic hints, octave-specific and black-key misconception feedback, WebAudio tone reinforcement, progressive level ranges, and clean keyboard/ARIA support, mapping precisely onto KS2 "use and understand staff notation". One real rendering bug: space notes above/below the stave (G5 step 9, D4 step -1) draw without their ledger lines, which is musically incorrect at level 4.
subject: Music
name: Stave Climber

rationale: A genuinely strong KS3 Music aural-analysis tool: live-synthesised extracts with hidden answer maps drive real ear-training, and the marking engine is misconception-aware, distinguishing right-moment-wrong-element from timing errors and naming missed changes against the elements of music. Maps precisely onto the KS3 NC strand of listening, analysing and appraising, with solid accessibility and no evident bugs; only minor weaknesses are the crude oscillator timbres and a waveform that partly telegraphs dynamic changes visually.
subject: Music
name: Active Listening Annotator

rationale: A genuinely excellent, fully-implemented six-stage GCSE composing-to-a-brief scaffold with real Web Audio synthesis, accurate diatonic harmony and cadence detection across three keys, audibly-rendered development techniques (sequence, inversion, retrograde, ornamentation), and misconception-aware feedback scored against the four assessment focuses; maps precisely onto KS4 composition. Marked down a point only on technical edge cases (step/leap distance uses scale-index difference so octave-boundary intervals misclassify, and chord octave assignment is ad hoc).
subject: Music
name: Composition Brief Builder

rationale: A genuinely sophisticated KS5 Music tool: a correctly-engineered Web Audio lookahead metronome maps the audio clock to performance.now, captures taps via pointer/keyboard, and returns meaningful real-time metrics (accuracy, deviation, rush/drag drift, steadiness) plus an era-aware, misconception-conscious self-assessment that lands an indicative band. Robust, accessible and high cognitive demand; only the "programme" planning is thinner than its framing implies and the timing test is a pulse proxy rather than analysis of actual played sound.
subject: Music
name: Recital Prep Studio

rationale: A genuinely sophisticated A-level Music tool with two real activities (figured-bass realisation and first-species counterpoint) on an interactive SVG grand stave, offline Web Audio playback, full keyboard/ARIA support, and rule-by-rule checkers that correctly encode voice-leading theory (parallel/direct 5ths and octaves, consonance with the 4th as dissonant, leading-note resolution, contrary-motion ratio). It loses one point on alignment because chord-spelling verification is letter-based and the minor-key leading note is raised globally, both reasonable but imperfect simplifications.
subject: Music
name: Harmony and Counterpoint Lab

rationale: A genuinely excellent, fully working Web Audio mixing desk: four live synthesised stems through real signal chains (shelving EQ, stereo pan, post-fader convolution reverb), live metering with dBFS and clip detection, A/B reference playback, and three distinct target styles graded with weighted, tolerance-banded, misconception-aware engineer's notes (masking, low-end mud, over-reverb, gain staging). It maps tightly onto A-level Music Technology mixing and production at exactly the right pitch.
subject: Music
name: Mixdown Mastermind

rationale: A genuinely excellent KS1 Computing app that faithfully models the save/name/retrieve loop with real canvas drawing, named folders, a populated drawer of thumbnails and a scored find-it challenge, all backed by misconception-aware, specific feedback (rejects empty canvas and unhelpful short names). Accurately pitched to the KS1 "create, organise, store and retrieve digital content" curriculum statement; only minor blemishes are the generic injected demo-data modal text (referring to grades/attendance, irrelevant here) and the find-it prompt naming the target folder, which slightly eases the retrieval challenge.
subject: Computing
name: My Digital Drawer

rationale: A polished Scratch-style turtle environment that maps precisely onto the KS2 computing programme of study (sequence, selection, repetition, variables), with genuine drag/edit manipulation, immediate canvas feedback, step-by-step execution and misconception-aware prompts (repeat=0, turn=90 makes a square, move x repeats). Strong, robust and accessible; only minor blemishes (the variable concept stays a background chip rather than a fully manipulable on-screen value, and the safeguarding modal reuses generic grades/attendance boilerplate ill-suited to a drawing app).
subject: Computing
name: Loop Lab

rationale: A genuinely excellent KS2 computational-thinking app: it makes decomposition, pattern-spotting and abstraction concrete through growing SVG tile patterns, gates the general-rule builder behind a correct prediction, and gives precise misconception-aware feedback (forgotten constant, off-by-one step, over/under counts) while testing the pupil's rule against the hidden step to prove generality. The algebraic tiles = m×n+c rule sits at the very top edge of KS2 (nth-term territory) rather than dead-centre, which is the only slight pitch stretch.
subject: Computing
name: Pattern Spotter

rationale: A genuinely excellent KS2 Computing app: a coherent collect-sort-chart-reason workflow with authentic spreadsheet manipulation, keyboard-accessible sorting, and a misconception-aware "spot the fake" task that deliberately separates visual clues from plausibility reasoning ("60 minutes in an hour"). Accessible, responsive and bug-free; only minor friction is that typed values are silently replaced by the rostered figure to preserve the planted fake.
subject: Computing
name: Data Detectives

rationale: A genuinely excellent KS3 algorithms tool: a disciplined predict-race-score cycle that compares bubble vs insertion sort on identical, learner-editable data and counts comparisons as the cost measure, directly targeting the misconception that "best algorithm" is data-independent. Robust, accessible and responsive, with deterministic precomputed animation events, step-once tracing, clear teacher notes, and correct comparison maths.
subject: Computing
name: Sort Race

rationale: Excellent predict-then-reveal trace simulator: it makes the invisible "notional machine" visible (variable boxes, output console, line highlighting) and the multiple-choice distractors target real KS3 Python misconceptions (zero-indexing, range upper-bound exclusivity, return vs print, accumulator reassignment, append position), squarely mapping to the KS3 programming/computational-thinking strand. Technically solid with keyboard control, ARIA live regions and a responsive layout; the demo-data modal text ("no real child", "grades", "attendance") is generic boilerplate that is irrelevant to a code-tracing tool and slightly jars, hence one mark off.
subject: Computing
name: Python Trace Theatre

rationale: Excellent KS3 data-representation tool: three coherent modes (place-value byte explorer with live denary/hex/ASCII and sum-sentence, a randomised hit-the-target challenge with precise place-value feedback, and an 8x8 pixel-to-bytes canvas) that map accurately onto the KS3 computing curriculum and are misconception-aware. Strong accessibility (role=switch, tablist arrow nav, keyboard toggling, aria-live) and clean responsive design; only minor blemish is the irrelevant injected demo-data/child banner and modal, a trust-wide mandate rather than an app flaw.
subject: Computing
name: Binary Bulb Board

rationale: An accurate, step-by-stage Von Neumann CPU simulator that lets pupils drive a real LOAD/ADD/OUT/HALT program through fetch-decode-execute while watching PC, MAR, MDR, CIR and ACC update, backed by clear narration and a misconception-aware quiz; pitched well for KS3 computing and technically robust with keyboard control, auto-run and ARIA live regions. Minor blemish: the injected demo-data modal talks about "student records, names, grades, attendance" which is irrelevant to a CPU tool.
subject: Computing
name: Inside the CPU

rationale: An excellent, misconception-aware KS3 networks simulator: it genuinely splits a message into numbered header-bearing packets, BFS-routes them around live router failures, and explains reassembly by sequence number, with strong keyboard/ARIA accessibility and clean design. Maps precisely onto the England KS3 computing curriculum (networks, the internet, protocols) at the right pitch.
subject: Computing
name: Packet Switch Simulator

rationale: A genuine drag-and-wire logic-gate sandbox with live truth table, Boolean expression and a well-scaffolded AND/OR/NOT to XOR/NAND/NOR challenge ladder that maps exactly onto the KS3 Computing Boolean-logic objective. Robust evaluator (handles cycles and incomplete gates), strong accessibility and keyboard support; held off full marks only by dead port-colouring code and a boilerplate "student records" modal irrelevant to this tool.
subject: Computing
name: Logic Gate Workbench

rationale: Genuinely excellent: it teaches the full iterative design cycle with misconception-aware, evidence-demanding feedback and a clever honesty mechanism that caps user-test scores to what the build actually covers, all mapping cleanly onto the KS3 Computing programme of study. Technically robust with real pointer-drag plus keyboard building and good ARIA/live-region use; minor accessibility gap (uses aria-current rather than aria-selected on role=tab and no arrow-key tab navigation) keeps technical just short of perfect.
subject: Computing
name: Design-Build-Evaluate Studio

rationale: An excellent predict-then-reveal simulator that traces the fetch-decode-execute cycle micro-step by micro-step (PC->MAR, RAM->MDR, MDR->CIR, PC increment, decode, execute) with accurate register semantics, live state flashing, RAM PC-pointer highlighting, score tracking and narration; it maps precisely onto GCSE Computing systems architecture and is genuinely misconception-aware (separating MAR/MDR roles, PC increment timing, RAM volatility). Technically solid with sensible accessibility, losing one point only for minor rough edges (irrelevant "student records/grades/attendance" boilerplate in the acknowledgement modal, no Reset of the prompt if a user presses Reveal without checking on the final step).
subject: Computing
name: FDE Cycle Stepper

rationale: Excellent KS4 data-representation tool: live sliders re-quantise a real canvas bitmap, the file-size formula (w x h x depth / 8) is shown and accurate, and RLE is genuinely estimated by scanning pixel runs rather than faked, surfacing the key misconception that compression helps plain areas far more than busy ones. Maps cleanly onto GCSE CS (resolution, colour depth, KB maths, lossless vs lossy), with a challenge mode, targeted change-aware feedback, and strong accessibility; only the generic "no real child" demo modal text is mismatched to an image app.
subject: Computing
name: Bits Behind the Image

rationale: A genuinely excellent three-stage journey: a manipulable cipher wheel, an honest frequency-analysis cracking task, and a threat/countermeasure match that lands the key insight that encryption defends interception but not people-based attacks; correct maths, accurate KS4 system-security content, and strong keyboard/ARIA accessibility throughout. Technical is a notch below only because short intercepts make the top-letter heuristic statistically noisy and the injected demo-data modal text is generic boilerplate ill-fitting a cipher app.
subject: Computing
name: Caesar and Beyond

rationale: An excellent, exam-aligned KS4/GCSE algorithms tool: it races linear vs binary search on a shared sorted array, counts comparisons, fills genuine exam-style trace tables, and is misconception-aware (handles "not found", explains when linear can beat binary, and quizzes the sorted-data precondition). Only minor technical blemishes (a quiz wrong-answer path that does not actually reveal the correct option, plus some unused/competing highlight code) keep it off a perfect total.
subject: Computing
name: Search and Sort Visualiser

rationale: An exceptionally well-targeted KS4 GCSE Computing tool: it pairs exam-board-style pseudocode translation with boundary-aware validation-repair tasks (off-by-one, =/== confusion, exact-vs-minimum length) that directly attack the commonest programming misconceptions, all run against hidden test cases by a genuinely capable in-browser Python-subset interpreter giving immediate per-case feedback. Sequence, selection, iteration, arrays, subprograms and validation map cleanly onto the national curriculum and OCR/AQA pseudocode conventions; the only weakness is the interpreter's inevitable edge-case fragility (e.g. stubbed isdigit, limited error recovery) which could confuse a pupil whose otherwise-valid Python strays outside the supported subset.
subject: Computing
name: Pseudocode to Python

rationale: A polished, misconception-aware two-stage game (verdict then name the Act) that maps precisely onto the GCSE "impacts of digital technology" topic, with genuine interactivity, immediate explanatory feedback, a whole-class voting mode, and robust, accessible code. Alignment is held at 4 because the "classmate's photo" case is framed as flatly illegal under the DPA, which overstates legal certainty given the household/harassment nuances.
subject: Computing
name: Tech Ethics Courtroom

rationale: A genuinely excellent KS5 Computer Science tool that makes the hidden call stack visible by tracing four real recursive functions and replaying call/base/return events frame by frame, with a predict-then-check challenge carrying function-specific misconception hints. Well aligned to A-level recursion content, fully interactive, accessible (keyboard, ARIA live regions, focus styles), with only minor cosmetic blemishes.
subject: Computing
name: Recursion Unrolled

rationale: An exemplary KS5 Computing visualiser that makes the min-priority-queue ADT visible and ties it correctly to Dijkstra/A* and Big-O; the algorithm engine is genuinely accurate (lazy deletion, epsilon-safe relaxation, stale-entry skipping, admissible heuristic, unreachable-goal handling) with full graph authoring, keyboard and ARIA support. Knocked one technical point because the heuristic's admissibility depends on a hardcoded pixel scale (H_SCALE=0.02) that breaks if pupils space nodes very differently, and edge weights can only be changed by deleting and re-adding.
subject: Computing
name: Graph Pathfinder

rationale: A genuinely excellent KS5/A-level theory-of-computation tool: pupils build FSMs and trace strings through a correct subset-construction NFA simulator with live step-by-step set tracking, an auto-grading challenge bank, and accurate theory linking FSMs to regular expressions and the non-regular {0^n1^n} limit. Loses one point only because symbol entry relies on a clunky window.prompt, which is awkward on touch devices.
subject: Computing
name: FSM Builder

rationale: An exceptionally well-built A Level normalisation tool: it encodes real functional dependencies and determinants, computes and highlights redundancy correctly through 1NF to 3NF, gives precise misconception-aware feedback (partial vs transitive dependencies, PK protection), and adds an accurate ACID transaction explorer; accurately pitched and exam-board referenced. Marked down only on technical because the keyboard Enter-to-move handler always sends to the first destination table (no cycling despite the comment), so a keyboard-only user must rely on the move buttons for some targets.
subject: Computing
name: Normalise This

rationale: A genuinely excellent KS5 Computing app running real (verified-correct) RSA modular-exponentiation maths, with a well-sequenced journey from key pairs through wrong-key attempts, tampering and the hybrid TLS rationale, plus misconception-aware feedback and solid accessibility. Marked down only on technical polish: the "integrity failure detected" wording overstates what plain RSA shows (it caveats this), and the injected demo-data modal text about "student records, grades, attendance" is irrelevant boilerplate for a cryptography tool.
subject: Computing
name: Public Key Exchange

rationale: An excellent, conceptually rich KS5 ethics app: live weighted re-scoring, a correctly implemented four-fifths adverse-impact metric, misconception-aware audit feedback (proxy variables, indirect discrimination, Article 22, BCS) and a defensible accountability model make it genuinely worth lesson time and accurately pitched to A-Level "consequences of computing". It loses one technical mark only for a slightly on-rails dataset and a coarse 4-per-group denominator plus a flat +6 "rebalance" shortcut, but no bugs and strong accessibility.
subject: Computing
name: Algorithm on Trial

rationale: A genuinely strong KS5 Computing simulator that teaches SDLC sequencing through misconception-targeted distractors, methodology trade-offs (Waterfall vs Agile) and injected requirement-change events that make the cost-of-change curve and iteration tangible, all with live consequential meters. Accurate to A-level systematic-development content, with solid drag-and-drop plus full keyboard accessibility; only minor technical quibbles keep it off a perfect score.
subject: Computing
name: Lifecycle Simulator

rationale: Excellent KS1 D&T app: meaningful tool/material selection with purposeful, misconception-aware distractors (each carrying a child-friendly rationale), followed by cut-shape-join-finish sequencing and a build animation, with genuine drag-and-drop plus full keyboard and ARIA-live accessibility. Maps cleanly onto the KS1 Design and Technology "make" strand, though the rigid cut-shape-join-finish order is a slight simplification and the generic data-acknowledgement modal text is mismatched to a making app.
subject: Design and Technology
name: Choose Your Tools (KS1 D&T)

rationale: Three genuinely manipulable SVG mechanisms (lever pivoting on a fixed point, straight-line slider, rolling car with wheels turning on an axle) with mechanism-specific, misconception-aware feedback and accurate KS1 D&T vocabulary, all keyboard-accessible with ARIA slider roles and live regions. Maps precisely onto the NC "simple mechanisms: levers, sliders, wheels and axles" strand at the right pitch; only the abstract "degrees / out of 100" measures and reliance on raw client-coordinate geometry hold technical back slightly from full marks.
subject: Design and Technology
name: Make It Move

rationale: A coherent five-stage iterative-design workflow (research, brief, measurable spec, annotated SVG CAD with exploded view, synthesising review) that maps tightly onto the KS2 Design programme of study, with misconception-aware feedback distinguishing facts from opinions and measurable rules from vague ones. Genuine, varied manipulation and immediate diagnostic feedback throughout; held just short of full marks by window.prompt-based annotation, no state persistence, and menu-constrained spec authoring.
subject: Design and Technology
name: Design Brief Builder

rationale: An authentic model of the KS2 D&T iterative loop: pupils test a prototype against a five-line spec, get specific per-criterion failure reasons, then change one interacting variable and re-test, with a timeline making the change-test-learn cycle visible and genuine trade-offs (speed vs tipping, stability vs weight, braking vs cost) baked into the maths. Cleanly built, accessible and responsive, with strong NC Evaluate alignment.
subject: Design and Technology
name: Iterate and Improve

rationale: A focused, curriculum-accurate KS2 D&T structures app: pupils tap individual diagonals to triangulate weak square bays, choose three vehicle weights, and run an animated drive-through where the deck visibly sags, reddens and "snaps" with live verdict feedback, directly teaching that triangulation/bracing stiffens frame structures. Robust, keyboard- and touch-accessible SVG with clear design; only minor physical simplification (capacity scales with brace count regardless of where the load sits) holds technical short of a 5.
subject: Design and Technology
name: Bridge Load Lab

rationale: Excellent KS2 D&T mechanisms lab: four genuinely interactive, correctly-animated stations (gears, pulleys with crossed-belt toggle, three cam profiles, scored design challenge) with live ratios and misconception-aware, contextual feedback that maps directly onto the KS2 programme of study for mechanical systems. Strong accessibility and clean design hold it just short of perfect on technical robustness.
subject: Design and Technology
name: Gear and Pulley Playground

rationale: Excellent KS2 D&T activity that frames a circuit as a system inside a designed product (torch/alarm/fan), with genuinely misconception-aware feedback (incomplete loop, open switch, output mismatched to brief) and a strong three-step design-test-fit arc that mirrors the National Curriculum's electrical-systems strand. Robust dual input (native DnD plus pointer-events for touch/IWB), keyboard select-then-place, and solid ARIA make it production-grade; only minor blemish is the boilerplate demo-data modal referencing "student records and grades" that is irrelevant to this circuit task.
subject: Design and Technology
name: Power Your Product

rationale: Three well-designed activities map cleanly onto the KS2 D&T cooking and nutrition strands (balanced diet via Eatwell proportions, safe/hygienic technique sequencing, and seasonality/food provenance), with weighted foods driving live proportion meters and genuinely misconception-aware feedback (biggest group, fats overload, hygiene-first ordering). Interactivity is strong with drag, tap-to-place and keyboard paths plus solid ARIA, costing a technical point only for the generic demo modal text referencing irrelevant grades/attendance and the fragile step-drag reattach logic.
subject: Design and Technology
name: Balance the Plate (DT KS2)

rationale: A genuinely excellent three-stage user-centred design tool that targets the hardest KS3 D&T skill (turning user needs into measurable, testable specifications) with misconception-aware feedback, distractor-flagging and a draggable annotated-sketch studio that forces pupils to evidence each spec point. Accurate to the England KS3 Design and Technology design strand, highly interactive with meaningful immediate feedback, accessible and cleanly built; only the keyword/regex spec grader (gameable, no real understanding) and the single fixed scenario hold the technical mark just short of 5.
subject: Design and Technology
name: From Context to Spec

rationale: A genuinely strong KS3 D&T Make-strand simulator: pupils sequence manufacture, allocate precise tools and choose quality checks, with all three feeding one simulation that flags sequence inversions, safety-before-machining errors, tool-task mismatches and useless checks via specific, misconception-aware feedback (try square vs ruler, coping saw vs tenon saw for acrylic, dry-fit before gluing). Accessible (keyboard reorder, ARIA live regions, touch drag), responsive and accurately pitched to the National Curriculum; the only soft spots are a strictly enforced single canonical order that penalises some defensible alternative sequences and somewhat arbitrary timing figures.
subject: Design and Technology
name: Production Plan Simulator

rationale: A genuinely strong, three-stage tool (dissect, evaluate, improve) that maps precisely onto the KS3 Evaluate strand, with misconception-aware MCQ distractors, evidence-calibrated slider feedback, and a redesign scorecard that rewards targeting real weaknesses and matching fix to problem. Accessible, responsive and replayable across three products; only minor limitations are the keyword-based evidence check and the simple distance-from-ideal slider heuristic.
subject: Design and Technology
name: Critique the Product

rationale: A genuinely strong KS3 D&T tool: weighted brief-against-material scoring (not a lookup table) with budget ceilings, a smart-material requirement check, misconception cues (thermochromic film flagged as non-structural; smart materials as overkill), and sentence stems that scaffold the NC justification skill directly. Clean, accessible, responsive build; only minor dead code in the cost bar and an irrelevant generic demo-modal blurb hold technical back from a 5.
subject: Design and Technology
name: Material Selector

rationale: An exemplary KS3 D&T tool that models the input-process-output control loop with a genuine wiring canvas, a live microcontroller rule (operator plus threshold), a sensor simulator, gear-ratio mechanism links and four auto-checked design briefs; it directly hits the National Curriculum requirement to apply computing/electronic systems and mechanisms and is precisely misconception-aware about the programmable process changing behaviour without rewiring. Drag, keyboard nav and ARIA are well handled; the only weaknesses are that challenge checks sample just two sensor points (so a loose rule like "<=60" passes the "below 30" night-light brief) and the demo-data acknowledgement modal text about "student records, grades, attendance" is irrelevant boilerplate for this app.
subject: Design and Technology
name: DT KS3: Systems Block Builder

rationale: A genuinely strong KS3 D&T cooking-and-nutrition tool: the design-build-evaluate-adapt loop is real, with live macro/micro bars, four distinct dietary-target challenges, a savoury-dish enforcement flag matching the NC, and misconception-aware coaching (e.g. vitamin C aiding iron absorption). Accurate per-100g teaching data, accessible markup and robust dual drag-and-drop; only minor blemishes (a dead click-handler stub, redundant target-line maths, and boilerplate "grades/attendance" wording in the generic demo modal) keep technical off a 5.
subject: Design and Technology
name: Nutrient Lab

rationale: Genuinely excellent KS4 D&T tool: four smart/modern materials each rendered as a live SVG model that responds instantly to a stimulus slider with phase-accurate, misconception-aware explanations (martensite/austenite, leuco dye reversibility, stiffness-to-weight with a steel ghost comparison, piezoelectric lattice distortion), then a keyboard-accessible drag-and-drop product-matching task with reasoned per-drop feedback. Science is accurate and pitched correctly to the GCSE "modern and smart materials / energy and systems" content; only minor blemishes (generic demo-data modal copy about pupil records that does not apply here, and an aria-live region that announces only the latest drop) keep technical off a 5.
subject: Design and Technology
name: Smart Material Showcase

rationale: A genuinely excellent KS4 D&T tool that models the full GCSE iterative design cycle with a focus-budget that forces real trade-off thinking, live ergonomics/tolerance/capacity calcs, persona-based user testing, and a version log that rewards evidence-led refinement exactly as NEA examiners do. Engineering is simplified (honestly flagged as such) and the generic safeguarding modal text fits poorly, but the design, interactivity and curriculum fit are strong.
subject: Design and Technology
name: Iterative Design Loop

rationale: An honest, condition-driven simulator: ingredients carry real temperature/method thresholds for denaturation, gelatinisation, caramelisation, Maillard browning and aeration, with a food-safety core-temp check and a misconception-targeting "explain it" quiz that maps tightly to GCSE Food Preparation and Nutrition. Genuine manipulation with immediate, reasoned feedback and good accessibility; only minor crudeness in the core-temp proxy and some harmless dead code keep technical below a 5.
subject: Design and Technology
name: Cooking Science Bench

rationale: A genuine modelling task: pupils plan a day of meals for three contrasting profiles and get live, misconception-aware feedback (free vs intrinsic sugars, fibre/glucose link, life-stage salt/iron risks) mapped cleanly onto GCSE D&T food, nutrition and provenance content. Robust, accessible and well-designed; only minor refinements (fixed portions with no quantity control, three meal slots, and a slightly mismatched "no real child" safeguarding modal) hold it back from full marks.
subject: Design and Technology
name: Diet Profiler

rationale: A statistically rigorous, conceptually precise KS5 D&T tool: correct normal CDF/quantile maths, proper 50/50 mixture pooling, and the pedagogically vital "overall fit equals the worst-served dimension" plus the clearance-versus-reach (accommodateBelow/Above) distinction that sits at the heart of inclusive design. Rich live interactivity (dual percentile band, population toggle, dynamic per-product sliders, shaded SVG bell curve, auto-fit solver, RAG verdicts) with immediate meaningful feedback; minor technical deductions for the loose stature-based bench-height proxy and a boilerplate demo-data modal that references child records this tool never contains.
subject: Design and Technology
name: Anthropometrics Explorer

rationale: A genuinely excellent KS5 D&T tool: a defensible weighted scoring model with a log-scale volume slider that re-trials live so students see how the optimum process shifts with production scale, followed by a misconception-aware justification task whose distractors target classic errors (e.g. "additive gives the best finish", "injection is cheapest for one-offs"). Domain content is accurate and accessibility is strong (ARIA roles, roving tabindex, live regions).
subject: Design and Technology
name: Process Chooser Pro

rationale: A polished, genuinely interactive KS2 gymnastics composition tool: drag-and-drop plus full keyboard reordering, with a meaningful live variety/flow feedback engine and targeted natural-language coaching that maps precisely onto the KS2 PE gymnastics strand. Pedagogy is strong but capped at 4 because it assesses sequence design on paper rather than any physical execution, the actual PE learning outcome, and its flow metric is a useful but simplistic proxy.
subject: Physical Education
name: Sequence Builder (KS2 Gymnastics)

rationale: A genuinely excellent KS2 dance composition tool that targets real National Curriculum composing skills (motif, level, formation, and crucially motif development) with rich manipulation, a live animated canvas preview, and a criterion-based critique that names and rewards the exact KS2 skill of repeating a motif while altering it. Strong accessibility, responsive design and no obvious bugs; only minor weaknesses are crude stick-figure poses and a visual preview with no non-visual equivalent.
subject: Physical Education
name: Choreo Canvas

rationale: A genuinely strong tool that turns the abstract KS3 compositional devices (motif, motif development, unison, canon, dynamic contrast) into a manipulable eight-phrase timeline with an animated staged preview and a rubric-aware evaluator that rewards correct compositional structure (motif stated then developed, contrasting relationships, light-and-shade dynamics, strong ending). Pedagogy, interactivity and curriculum alignment are excellent; technical execution is robust and accessible (keyboard placement, ARIA live region, focus styling), held just short of full marks by minor rough edges.
subject: Physical Education
name: Composition Lab

rationale: Anatomically accurate, misconception-aware treatment of first/second/third class levers and mechanical advantage that maps precisely onto GCSE PE movement analysis, with genuine drag manipulation, a live MA readout that reinforces the force-versus-range trade-off, real-body scenarios and a marked quiz with rationale. Robust keyboard control, ARIA sliders and live regions make it accessible; only a minor dead variable and the abstract 0-100 "units" scale keep technical off a 5.
subject: Physical Education
name: Lever Lab

rationale: Genuinely excellent KS4 PE app: dragging the inverted-U peak with performance judged relative to the theory's optimal (not the curve's own apex) directly exposes the skill-demand misconception, backed by five well-differentiated scenarios and four diagnostic exam MCQs. Accurate to GCSE PE spec (arousal, skill classification, stages of learning), accessible (slider role, arrow keys, aria-live), and robust.
subject: Physical Education
name: Arousal and Performance

rationale: A polished, misconception-aware KS4 PE simulator with genuine multi-input manipulation (sliders, diet/activity segments, scenario presets) feeding live net-energy, macronutrient and long-term mass projections, plus a gated problem-solving challenge and strong accessibility. Only minor accuracy caveat: protein is fixed per diet split, so the challenge's "20% of intake" wording slightly oversimplifies how protein responds to changing calories.
subject: Physical Education
name: Energy Balance Simulator

rationale: A genuinely strong, well-scoped GCSE PE coursework scaffold: the four stages (Plan, Log, Graph, Evaluate) map tightly onto the NEA/PEP, with live FITT/SMART grading, a specificity misconception check, an editable log with progressive-overload detection, toggleable SVG trend graphs, and data-driven AO2/AO3 evaluation feedback, all honestly flagged as indicative only. Minor technical rough edges (the graph "keyboard step" actually cycles the metric, the legend promises hover tooltips that do not exist, and evaluation marking is heuristic/gameable) keep it just short of flawless.
subject: Physical Education
name: PEP Builder

rationale: Excellent A-level PE tool: accurately models the three-system continuum with live sliders, presets, an animated swept stacked-area graph and EPOC curve, plus misconception-aware coaching and a six-item quiz covering fuel, fatigue mechanisms, oxygen deficit and EPOC components. Genuinely manipulable, well-pitched to KS5 exercise physiology, robust and accessible; only minor model coarseness and a couple of stray HTML entities (e.g. "Fats & glycogen") keep technical below a 5.
subject: Physical Education
name: Energy Systems Continuum

rationale: An excellent, misconception-aware A Level PE sandbox: it numerically integrates projectile motion with proper velocity-component decomposition and a light drag term that correctly pushes the optimum angle below 45 degrees, and pairs genuine manipulation (sliders, ghost-path comparison, randomised coaching challenge) with state-diffing explanations that name the changed governing factor and explain the mechanics. Accessibility is strong (aria-live, canvas keyboard control, sr-only announcements, focus outlines); the only knocks are an approximate last-step impact-angle read and a generic demo-data modal whose grades/attendance wording does not fit a physics tool.
subject: Physical Education
name: Biomechanics Sandbox

rationale: An accurate, misconception-aware model of Weiner's attribution theory (locus, stability, controllability) with genuine drag-and-keyboard manipulation driving live expectancy/motivation predictions and a well-designed attributional-retraining challenge, pitched correctly at A-level PE. Technically solid with strong accessibility; only minor blemishes (controllability tags reuse the stable/unstable colour classes, creating slight semantic colour overload).
subject: Physical Education
name: Attribution Modeller (KS5 PE)

rationale: An authentic, well-scaffolded four-stage walkthrough of the KS5 PE EAPI coursework cycle with genuinely varied interaction (analytical-writing scoring, accessible drag/keyboard cause-matching, a programme builder doing real specificity and progressive-overload checks, and a weighted readiness rating), all accurately pitched to A-level/BTEC content. Strong accessibility and clean design; only minor blemishes are the crude progressive-overload heuristic (reads just the first number from intensity+time) and gameable regex scoring of free text.
subject: Physical Education
name: EAPI Studio

rationale: A genuinely excellent KS2 Citizenship app: pupils build manifesto cards, cast configurable secret ballots, and read an animated first-past-the-post board that is explicitly misconception-aware (it surfaces sub-50% winners, the meaning of low turnout, ties, and why ballots are secret), all reinforced by a targeted decision quiz.
