Robust, accessible and well-pitched; held off a perfect technical mark only because there is no full keyboard-arrow navigation within ballot/quiz option groups and the quiz never re-builds after first visit.
subject: Citizenship
name: Run a School Election

rationale: A genuinely strong KS2 Citizenship sorter: the rule/law/neither classification targets a real misconception, wrong attempts give specific who-makes-it feedback rather than just "try again", and the per-item consequence ladder deepens understanding of why laws exist and who makes them, all accurately pitched to the England NC. Three input modes (drag, tap-select, keyboard) plus ARIA live regions make it robust and inclusive, with only trivial cosmetic code blemishes.
subject: Citizenship
name: Rule or Law?

rationale: Genuinely excellent KS2 financial-education app: real cognitive demand (value for money, needs vs treats, running totals, plan completeness) with immediate, misconception-aware coaching, plus a robust accessible UI offering both touch drag-and-drop and a full keyboard path. Alignment is strong on the economic-wellbeing strand though "Citizenship" is slightly loose since this content is PSHE/financial education and non-statutory at KS2.
subject: Citizenship
name: Budget the Class Trip

rationale: A genuinely thoughtful active-citizenship planner whose awareness/support/decision-maker model, diminishing-returns scoring and balance bonus teach a real conceptual point, paired with multi-modal drag-and-drop, a live impact meter and misconception-aware feedback. Technically robust and accessible (ARIA, keyboard paths, progressbar semantics); alignment is a notch lower only because KS2 Citizenship is non-statutory, so it maps to the framework/PSHE rather than the statutory NC.
subject: Citizenship
name: Campaign Builder

rationale: Legally accurate, misconception-aware guided pathways that map precisely onto the KS3 Citizenship NC strand (civil vs criminal law, the courts, standards of proof, and how laws are made), with targeted reteach feedback and clean, accessible, XSS-safe code. Interactivity is strong multiple-choice progression with a progressively lit pathway map rather than the genuine sorting or construction the "sort and build" framing implies, which is the only thing holding it short of full marks.
subject: Citizenship
name: Courtroom Pathways

rationale: An excellent KS3 Citizenship budget simulator: six live sliders drive an immediate consequence dashboard, public-mood meter and verdict banner, with a statutory-floor/ideal model that genuinely teaches prioritisation, trade-offs and balanced-budget law, plus an accurate devolved-vs-reserved government panel mapped tightly to the Citizenship PoS. Build quality is high (responsive grid, strong aria-live/labelledby accessibility, robust over/underspend handling and a guarded auto-balance), held just short of full marks by minor threshold inconsistencies between the verdict bands and the mood-face cutoffs.
subject: Citizenship
name: Run the Council

rationale: A genuinely strong KS3 Citizenship finance simulator: real budget-allocation sliders, random life events with meaningful saving-vs-borrowing tradeoffs, month-end compound interest, and consistently misconception-aware feedback (BNPL framed as credit with an explicit premium, emergency-fund reinforcement). Maps accurately onto the KS3 programme of study for budgeting, managing risk and the uses of money, with solid accessibility (aria-live, slider valuetext, SVG text-summary fallback).
subject: Citizenship
name: Money Months

rationale: A genuinely strong KS3 Citizenship resource pairing a misconception-aware membership map (correctly distinguishing UN/NATO/Commonwealth, e.g. Russia and China are UN but not NATO) with a causal-sequencing chain builder that demands real reasoning and gives targeted, position-specific feedback. Accurate facts, well-pitched to KS3, and robustly built with full keyboard and screen-reader support; only minor technical fragilities (tile-matching by textContent, generic demo-modal copy referencing grades/attendance the app never uses) hold it just short of perfect.
subject: Citizenship
name: Global Connections Map

rationale: Tightly integrates an interactive constitution diagram (click/keyboard nodes light up power flows and checks, two-node selection reveals the relationship) with a five-scenario applied-reasoning lab whose misconception-aware distractor feedback and on-map power traces make the checks-and-balances concept explicit; content is accurate and well pitched for KS4 Citizenship. Marked down only on technical: SVG g-element keyboard focus is browser-inconsistent and the injected demo-data modal references irrelevant "grades and attendance".
subject: Citizenship
name: Power Map of Britain

rationale: A genuinely excellent KS4 Citizenship simulator: finite budget/days drive authentic trade-offs across seven real campaign tactics with diminishing-returns fatigue, probabilistic backfire risk, live polling, per-move feedback and tailored end-game insight, all misconception-aware (air vs ground war, negative-campaigning rebound). Clean, accessible, robust build with only minor polish gaps (result modal lacks focus trap/restore, occasional 1-point renormalisation drift).
subject: Citizenship
name: Win the Marginal

rationale: A genuinely excellent KS4 Citizenship tool: it models the full social-action arc (issue to SMART aim to advocacy method to measurable impact) with a live stakeholder board that meaningfully rewards evidence and a direct route to the decision-maker while penalising disproportionate disruption, mirroring exactly what GCSE Citizenship Studies assesses in the active citizenship component. The trade-off logic is misconception-aware (e.g. boycotts win attention but cost public support; opinion-only impact is weak evidence), and the design is clean, keyboard-accessible and ARIA-aware.
fixIt: Minor technical polish only: the SMART checker is keyword-based, so a plausible-looking aim with stray digits or a month word can falsely light pills, and the four stakeholder bars use static native text but the colour-only reaction words lean on hue; otherwise robust.
subject: Citizenship
name: Action Project Planner

rationale: An excellent KS4 Citizenship simulator: eight live dials feed an integrated model (revenue, deficit bar, weighted public approval, per-service quality and a real-time £28k payslip) with targeted Socratic coaching that explicitly surfaces misconceptions (NI is not income tax; VAT is regressive; no trade-off-free answer). Accessibility is strong (ARIA live regions, aria-valuetext, focus-visible outlines, visually-hidden status), and figures are clearly flagged as simplified teaching estimates.
subject: Citizenship
name: Where Your Tax Goes

rationale: An exemplary KS4 Citizenship app: three fictional outlets report identical facts and pupils tag bias, framing and missing context with phrase-specific feedback, then build a justification-gated reliability score, all mapping cleanly onto the media/critical-citizen strand. It is genuinely misconception-aware (it teaches that balanced framing is not spin and that a reliability score is a judgement, not a fact), with only minor technical untidiness holding back a perfect score.
subject: Citizenship
name: Spot the Spin

rationale: Genuinely excellent KS5 Citizenship/Politics tool: students manipulate real policies on a two-axis compass and get distance-based placement feedback, nuanced liberal/conservative/socialist responses with named thinkers (Mill, Burke, Bevan, Hayek, Rawls), and quadrant-keyed debate prompts, accurately pitched to A-level ideology, sovereignty and liberty concepts. Robust pointer/keyboard control with ARIA live regions and HTML escaping; only minor accessibility gaps (drag puck not directly focusable, generic demo-modal text about "grades" not fitting this app) hold technical short of a 5.
subject: Citizenship
name: Ideology Compass

rationale: Genuinely excellent KS5 Citizenship simulation: policy change is a derived outcome with synergy, backlash and diminishing-returns logic, plus per-phase climate and per-scenario bias, so insider/outsider trade-offs and the "insider dilemma" emerge authentically rather than being asserted. Strong accessibility, clean responsive design and well-targeted reflective prompts; the only modest limit is that the model is hand-tuned and deterministic with no save/print of the campaign log.
subject: Citizenship
name: Pressure Group Strategist

rationale: A genuinely excellent KS5 Citizenship financial-capability simulator: an 8-stage decade journey with live mortgage-deposit slider, payslip net-pay identification, and misconception-aware feedback, all built on accurate simplified UK rules (Plan 5 loans, 20% tax / 8% NI, credit-score drivers, correct amortisation). Manipulation is real and feedback immediate, with strong accessibility (radiogroup roles, aria-live, sr-only gauge text, keyboard support); the only weakness is the simplified net-worth/mortgage model that treats deposit as equity without tracking the outstanding loan as a liability.
subject: Citizenship
name: Adult Life Finance Simulator

rationale: A well-built KS5 Citizenship climate-summit simulation: four differentiated composite nations, three levers each, nation-specific misconception-aware feedback and a live dashboard that aggregates ambition, cooperation, strain and projected warming into a fair, durable-deal outcome that genuinely teaches CBDR, the collective-action problem and climate justice. Accessibility is unusually thorough (ARIA tablist with arrow-key nav, aria-valuetext sliders, live regions) and the code is robust; only the opaque underlying scoring maths keeps technical short of a 5.
subject: Citizenship
name: Global Challenge Room

rationale: A research-informed routine-design CPD tool with genuine cognitive demand: it scores user-built sequences against weighted behaviour-management principles, deliberately seeds weak distractor steps to force discernment, and generates rehearsal scripts plus a RAF transition timer and consistency audit. Accessible, responsive and bug-light; loses one technical point only for lack of persistence and a harmless dead-branch in the feedback class logic.
subject: CPD & Staff Tools
name: Routine Designer & Rehearsal Planner

rationale: A genuinely excellent CPD planning tool: real drag-and-drop plus full keyboard placement, with multi-dimensional diagnostics (coverage, mean gap, massing, interleaving) that give accurate, topic-specific feedback grounded in sound cognitive science, including the subtle gap-of-1 = re-reading-not-retrieval point. Strong accessibility (ARIA live region, grabbed states, focus-visible, keyboard parity) and clean, robust code; alignment is fitness-for-purpose strong rather than NC-mapped, with only minor modelling simplifications in how interleaving is counted.
subject: CPD & Staff Tools
name: Spacing & Interleaving Scheduler

rationale: A genuinely well-conceived CPD planning tool: it operationalises cognitive-science principles (prior knowledge, the over-listing planning trap, hinge questions) through a sort-with-feedback flow that auto-generates a targeted diagnostic, with robust accessible code (pointer drag plus full keyboard parity, ARIA, aria-live, focus management). Held off a perfect score because it ships only three topics and one or two prerequisite classifications (e.g. "ambitious vocabulary" as a hard prerequisite for a Y4/5 persuasive paragraph) are arguable rather than settled.
subject: CPD & Staff Tools
name: Prerequisite Knowledge Mapper

rationale: A genuinely strong CPD tool: staff map draft questions to KS3 objectives and cognitive demand, then get item-level diagnostic feedback plus a coverage matrix, demand-profile bar and validity verdict that surfaces real assessment-design issues. Robust dual-input interaction (touch-capable drag plus click/keyboard), accessible markup and clean design; only mild rigidity in the single "expert key" and a simplified (non-standard) demand taxonomy hold back full alignment.
subject: CPD & Staff Tools
name: Assessment Blueprint Builder

subject: CPD & Staff Tools
name: Vocabulary & EAL Support Planner

rationale: A genuinely excellent CPD planning tool: it models prerequisite dependencies, threshold concepts and prior/future links across three realistic, subject-accurate schemes, then gives precise, actionable per-link and threshold feedback rather than a single score. Interactivity is real (drag, keyboard, and button reordering all mutate state and re-evaluate live), and the implementation is robust and unusually accessible (ARIA live regions, Alt+arrow reordering, focus retention, 44px targets, pointer-capture re-attachment mid-drag).
subject: CPD & Staff Tools
name: Curriculum Sequencer

rationale: A pedagogically sophisticated CPD tool that walks teachers through anticipating, diagnosing and correcting canonical, well-evidenced curriculum misconceptions across five subjects, with distractors and feedback that genuinely model root-cause SPCK/PCK reasoning. Technically clean, accessible and robust; the only real limit is modest content breadth (8 misconceptions) and a session-bound, non-exportable planning bank.
subject: CPD & Staff Tools
name: Misconception Anticipation Bank

Technically polished with focus trapping, ARIA, search/filter, and clean responsive design; interactivity is meaningful though limited to filter/browse plus a short MCQ scenario set rather than deeper manipulation.
subject: CPD & Staff Tools
name: Evidence Summary Explorer

rationale: An genuinely excellent CPD data-literacy tool: it teaches the load-bearing distinction between a raw gap and a statistically significant one, is misconception-aware (foregrounds statistical neighbours as the fairest comparator and explicitly removes the "intake excuse"), and is accurate to England's KS4 accountability measures including P8 national being 0 by definition. Multi-modal interaction (drag, slider, keyboard) drives a live read-out plus a self-checking challenge bank, on a robust, accessible SVG build.
subject: Data & Analytics
name: National Benchmark Overlay

rationale: An excellent CPD planner that models the EEF three-tier Pupil Premium framework with genuine cognitive demand, immediate per-card verdicts, a live budget bar and a weighted scorecard; the deliberate "weak match" trap (music subsidy named against GCSE English) makes it authentically misconception-aware. Robust multi-input interaction (drag, pointer fallback for tablets, keyboard 1/2/3/0) with strong ARIA support; the only real gaps are a small dead-code line in setMeter and the absence of an explicit teaching-led upper-bound check, both minor.
subject: Data & Analytics
name: Pupil Premium Strategy Impact Planner

rationale: A genuinely well-conceived CPD moderation tool: it separates systematic bias (fixable by adjustment) from inconsistency (not fixable), gives accurate statistics and professionally sound standardisation advice, with real slider manipulation, completeness gating, live feedback and strong accessibility. Marked down only on technical for fixed/arbitrary verdict thresholds and a leftover patch comment, neither of which is a functional bug.
subject: Data & Analytics
name: Moderation Reliability Checker

rationale: A genuinely excellent, misconception-targeted sandbox: it isolates the three rival explanations (confounder, regression to the mean, missing counterfactual) and shows live that an observed rise is not proof of cause, with adaptive verdicts, well-chosen presets and a reinforcing quiz. Fit for professional purpose as a leadership impact-evaluation training tool, accurate, and accessible (aria-live, focus-visible, keyboard support).
subject: Data & Analytics
name: Correlation vs Causation Sandbox

rationale: A genuinely misconception-aware CPD tool that decodes IDSR significance, quintiles and headline gauges with accurate, plain-English inspector framing (white "not significant" band, small-cohort caveats, disadvantaged-vs-all line). Interactive decodes plus a scored, explained drag-or-keyboard ordering challenge make it fit for its professional purpose, with strong keyboard accessibility and clean responsive design.
subject: Data & Analytics
name: IDSR Walkthrough Simulator

rationale: An excellent, purpose-built governance CPD tool: three live toggles redraw an honest SVG attendance chart while a verdict and a four-round quiz teach the specific fixes for truncated axes, dual axes and cherry-picked baselines, with misconception-aware feedback and good accessibility. Marked down one point on alignment only because the dual-axis exclusion values are silently altered in baseline mode without the verdict flagging it, a minor inconsistency in an otherwise accurate, well-pitched resource.
subject: Data & Analytics
name: Honest Charts Lab

rationale: An excellent CPD governance trainer: five realistic, misconception-aware scenarios (public task vs consent, minimisation, jigsaw re-identification, small-number suppression, safeguarding DSL-to-DSL transfer) with accurate UK GDPR/KCSIE-grounded per-option feedback and pass/partial/fail scoring. Clean, accessible, bug-free implementation with proper HTML escaping and responsive design; the multi-select minimisation question gives genuinely graded judgement rather than binary right/wrong.
subject: Data & Analytics
name: Pupil Data Ethics Checker

rationale: A genuinely excellent KS3 Geography simulator: a draggable atmospheric cross-section (position and slope) linked live to a synoptic plan view, with responsive temperature/pressure/humidity/rainfall readouts, accurate UK-focused meteorology (air masses, cloud sequences, depression passage) and a well-written predict-then-check quiz. Technically robust (DPR-aware canvas, pointer capture, keyboard and ARIA accessibility, demo-data safeguard), with only minor drawing-overlap quirks holding it just short of full marks.
subject: Geography
name: Weather Systems & Fronts

rationale: An exemplary KS5 historiography tool: it models genuine orthodox/revisionist/post-revisionist (and intentionalist/structuralist) debates with named historians and correctly attributed, sourced evidence, then scaffolds from reading to a label-stripped sorting task to a judgement task with examiner-grade exemplar feedback. The three-mode progression maps precisely onto A-Level assessment objectives (deploying interpretations and reaching a substantiated judgement), and the drag-sort supports both pointer and keyboard with accessible roles.
subject: History
name: Historiography Debate

rationale: Excellent KS5 MFL resource: three languages, accurate connectives organised by discourse function with formal/neutral/informal register badges, and genuinely pedagogic feedback that distinguishes function errors from register slips, directly targeting the cohesion and register marks A-Level examiners reward. subject: Modern Foreign Languages
name: Essay Connectives and Register

rationale: A genuinely excellent MFL uplevelling tool across French, German and Spanish, with a real position-aware grammar engine (post/pre-nominal adjectives, German V2 inversion and dass verb-final order) and a standout pedagogic design that repeatedly separates structural range from accuracy and reinforces it with a dedicated error-spotting quiz. Rich interactivity (drag-drop, ladder, ring, tree, language-aware TTS) and honest GCSE-aware framing; technical is strong with minor harmless dead code (no-op global drop listener) and a duplicated-key articles set.
subject: Modern Foreign Languages

subject: Religious Education
name: Philosophy of Religion

rationale: Excellent KS1 MFL design: multisensory karaoke (sight, sound, action) with phonics-aware tempo control that genuinely slows the French TTS so children can copy phonemes, plus a true listen-and-choose quiz that withholds the printed word when speech is available so pupils must recognise it by ear. Technically robust with graceful no-speech fallback, async voice loading, French voice selection, live regions and keyboard control; alignment is strong (number patterns, greetings, intonation) but content is limited to two short songs covering only counting 1 to 5 and basic greetings.
subject: Modern Foreign Languages
name: Action Song Builder

rationale: Three authentic A-Level ethics dilemmas with accurately cited religious and secular frameworks, using productive-struggle design that gates the model perspectives behind a sort task so students reason before they see answers. Interactivity is genuinely formative (keyboard-accessible drag-and-drop, per-row match quiz, framework filters, TTS) and tightly aligned to AO2 evaluative exam demands; technical build is clean, self-contained and accessible.
subject: Religious Education
name: Medical Ethics Analyser

rationale: A genuinely excellent KS1 pastoral app: six misconception-aware playground scenarios with differentiated "why" feedback for both right and wrong votes, a non-punitive "have another think" loop, cumulative Friendship Charter, TTS read-aloud for emergent readers, and strong accessibility (aria-live, SVG labelling, focus management). subject: Behaviour & Pastoral
name: Good Friend or Not?

rationale: An exceptionally well-judged KS3 RSHE consent tool: misconception-aware three-tier feedback distinguishes genuinely tricky "nearly there" responses (sulking still coerces, "delete later" does not undo sharing, ghosting vs polite withdrawal), maps accurately to DfE statutory RSHE on respectful relationships, consent and online behaviour, and handles a sensitive topic safely with non-explicit scenarios and Childline/DSL signposting. Technically robust and accessible (XSS-escaped, focus management, ARIA, responsive); interactivity is meaningful but choose-and-reveal rather than the truly branching narrative the catalogue name implies, and there is no persistence or teacher view.
subject: Behaviour & Pastoral
name: Boundaries and Consent Lab

rationale: A genuinely strong KS3 PSHE/RSHE simulator: five well-written prejudice scenarios across distinct protected characteristics, each with graded options that are explicitly misconception-aware (it separates passive bystanding, well-meant-but-unsafe aggression, and victim-blaming), tied to the 5 Ds toolkit and the Equality Act, with accurate age-appropriate content, clean accessible design, TTS, focus management and keyboard support. subject: Behaviour & Pastoral
name: Upstander Simulator

rationale: A genuinely strong three-phase pastoral sequence (audit as recruiter, advise the teen to clean up, then lock down privacy settings) with sharp, misconception-aware feedback on every option and a live exposure meter that responds to slider changes; maps precisely onto KS4 RSHE/online-safety expectations (reputation, image data, phishing scams, privacy as habit). subject: Behaviour & Pastoral
name: Your Digital Footprint (KS4)

rationale: A genuinely strong KS5 PSHE/pastoral simulation: scenario-specific need profiles, a weighted scoring model that purposefully foregrounds help-seeking over self-soothing (a real and well-chosen misconception to target), and tailored crack feedback make it worth lesson time. Interaction is real manipulation (drag plus keyboard-accessible add/remove, live balance counts, animated pressure relief, ARIA live region and TTS), and the build is clean and robust; alignment to the England PSHE/RSHE health-and-wellbeing strand is accurate and well-pitched, just not tied to a statutory subject content list.
subject: Behaviour & Pastoral
name: Transition Resilience Lab

rationale: Genuinely strong KS2 citizenship app: it teaches the rights-and-responsibilities pairing with misconception-aware distractors (each right has both a confused-with-another-right scenario and a right-denied scenario, with feedback that explicitly distinguishes them), real drag-and-drop with a keyboard fallback, gated two-step flow, specific per-choice feedback, TTS and a balance-scale visualisation, all accurately mapped to the correct UNCRC articles at the right pitch.
subject: Citizenship
name: Rights of the Child Explorer

rationale: A genuinely excellent KS3 Citizenship tool: four well-chosen case studies map a slider onto five staged feedback bands per case, each with accurate, age-pitched legal grounding (HRA Articles 8 and 10, Public Order Act, Communications/Online Safety Acts, GDPR/DPA, school search powers) and a clear verdict, teaching that rights are qualified and limits must be proportionate. Interaction is real and immediate with live feedback, challenge-checking and an explored/solved progress tracker; keyboard support, ARIA live regions and aria-valuetext make it accessible.
subject: Citizenship
name: Balance of Liberties

rationale: Legally accurate, misconception-aware KS4 Citizenship app whose three-step judgement (breach, characteristic, type) gives real cognitive demand, with targeted per-part feedback, cited Equality Act sections, redress routes, and a deliberate lawful-exception case; technically robust with strong accessibility, TTS and responsive design.
subject: Citizenship
name: Equality Act in Action

rationale: A genuinely well-designed CPD tool: it teaches adaptive teaching through the graduated approach (APDR), distinguishes universal/targeted/specialist tiers, and crucially includes deliberate "trap" adaptations (easier worksheet, full-time TA, reading aloud) with misconception-aware feedback that targets dependence, capped expectations and workload sustainability. Interaction is real (pointer drag plus keyboard toggle, accessible roles/aria-live), feedback is specific and per-chip, and the exportable plan ties it to professional practice; only a few minor technical edges (chips have role=listitem but use div with tabindex rather than button semantics, and "✓/✗" marks are hardcoded inline rather than entity-escaped) keep technical off a 5.
subject: CPD & Staff Tools
name: SEND Classroom Adaptation Planner

rationale: An excellent CPD self-coaching tool: teachers build a four-phase lesson and at each of eight decision points get immediate, specific, colour-coded feedback that names the cognitive-science principle (retrieval practice, worked examples, dual coding, hinge questions, responsive teaching), with weighted scoring and a printable blueprint. Content is accurate and evidence-faithful, code is robust, responsive and notably accessible; it loses a point only on interactivity, being a polished but branchless multiple-choice format with no open input or adaptive pathways.
subject: CPD & Staff Tools
name: Evidence-Informed Lesson Planner

rationale: An excellent CPD tool that turns the abstract principle of "minimal effective help" into a concrete, well-modelled drag-and-sort task: three credible cross-subject scenarios, items tagged by scaffold level and difficulty, and genuinely diagnostic feedback that checks both band placement and within-band difficulty progression while explaining the pedagogical reasoning. Robust pointer-and-keyboard interaction, accessible markup, and clean responsive design; only minor limits (three scenarios, no export) keep alignment from a 5.
subject: CPD & Staff Tools
name: Practice Task Designer

rationale: A genuinely well-built attendance-modelling tool: accurate to DfE PA (<90%) and SA (<50%) definitions with correct 380-session maths, a fully custom accessible slider (pointer drag, rich keyboard support, ARIA valuetext, live region), and intelligent feedback that separates sessions-still-needed from total-needed and flags mathematically-impossible recovery. Fitness-for-purpose as a professional early-warning tool is strong; the only slight conceptual looseness is that the planner lets a user model below the natural projection without comment.
subject: Data & Analytics
name: Persistent Absence Early-Warning Board

rationale: A genuinely strong data-literacy CPD tool: three input modes (drag, slider, keyboard with full ARIA slider semantics), least-squares slope projection, a state-driven regenerating quiz, and misconception-aware verdicts that correctly refuse to project parity when the gap is flat or widening. Alignment loses one mark only because linear extrapolation to a fixed parity year is a simplification (the tool does flag this), and "others"/non-disadvantaged attainment is held artificially fixed.
subject: Data & Analytics
name: Gap Trajectory Trendline

rationale: A genuinely strong KS3 Drama tool: it teaches subtext and intention through graded cards with misconception-aware feedback (surface meaning vs hidden meaning), then lets pupils manipulate tone, pace, pause and action with a live "audience reading" and a delivery-to-subtext match check, plus TTS contrasting a flat read with a shaped performance. Accessible (keyboard pick-and-place, ARIA, live regions) and robust, losing one technical mark only because the match heuristic collapses all good subtexts into a single guarded/open model and TTS shaping is approximate and degrades silently with no speech engine.
subject: Drama
name: Subtext Decoder (KS3 Drama)

rationale: A genuinely excellent GCSE drama scaffold: content-aware live feedback targets real examiner expectations and misconceptions (interpret-not-retell, process language over "we practised", balanced AO4 judgement) across all four devising-log phases, with strong accessibility, clean code and a coherent AO coverage export. Only caveat is that the AO1/AO2/AO4 mapping is presented as standard when boards vary, though the footer sensibly tells students to check their own specification.
subject: Drama
name: Devising Log Builder

rationale: An exemplary GCSE Drama directing tool: pupils stage a set-text extract beat by beat across staging, intention and design, receiving specific misconception-aware feedback, a coherence check that teaches choices must reinforce each other, and a colour-coded page-to-stage model answer woven from their own decisions. Accurate KS4 terminology and exam framing; only a few undefined CSS accent variables (--orange-300/400) cause minor cosmetic fallback.
subject: Drama
name: Set Text Director

rationale: An exceptionally well-conceived A-Level Drama devising tool: students pick a practitioner, make rehearsal choices the studio classifies live as on-style/stretch/off-style against accurate practitioner models, then draft working-notebook reflections checked for the precise justification language examiners reward. The pedagogy, interactivity and curriculum alignment are genuinely excellent; only minor technical polish (two undefined CSS vars referenced in the entry colour, raw emoji in script strings rather than escapes) holds it back from full marks.
subject: Drama
name: Devising in Style Studio

rationale: A genuinely strong KS2 spelling/punctuation app: misconception-aware feedback, a "read it back" strategy for they're/their/there, and a picture-confirms-meaning mechanic that ties spelling to meaning, all delivered with shuffled choices, hover previews, scoreboard, TTS and a clean, well-accessibilised interface (ARIA tabs, live regions, escapeHtml). Held off a perfect score only by a minor accuracy slip: the "dash" is modelled and reinforced as a spaced hyphen (" - ") rather than a true em/en dash.
subject: English
name: Homophone Showdown

rationale: An exemplary KS3 Shakespeare staging tool: genuine drag-and-drop blocking plus lighting drives a position-aware interpretation engine that generates defensible, director's-eye commentary on depth, proximity and status, with challenges, glossing, keyboard control and TTS. Pitched accurately to the KS3 literary-heritage/drama curriculum and built cleanly; only minor technical nits (a slight status/proximity conflation in the y-axis logic and the spotlight tracking x but not y) keep technical off a 5.
subject: English
name: Stage It Yourself

rationale: A genuinely excellent KS4 literature tool that teaches the AO2/AO3 relationship by toggling accurate historicist overlays onto authentic Macbeth and A Christmas Carol extracts, with a sharp word-level Quotation Explode and misconception-aware feedback. Knocked one mark on interactivity because each text only carries one meaningful overlay and the dual-toggle "both" state degrades to its native layer rather than producing real cross-period synthesis.
subject: English
name: Context Layers

rationale: A genuinely strong KS4 tool: the Venn shared/distinct model is checked against each chosen pair using a per-poem applies map, so feedback is accurate and misconception-aware (it flags ideas that are actually shared, or present in neither poem), and stems scaffold AO2/AO3 comparison precisely. Robust, accessible drag/keyboard interaction and correct AQA anthology/AO content; only minor reservation is that mixing Power and Conflict with Love and Relationships diverges from how clusters are compared in the actual exam.
subject: English
name: Poem Pair Comparer

rationale: A genuinely excellent KS5 tool: students layer lexis/syntax/form annotations onto a copyright-safe Gothic passage, receive live AO2-aware feedback that punishes labelling and rewards technique-to-meaning links, and watch a breadth-plus-depth "sustained argument" meter weave their strongest readings into a thesis scaffold. Accurate, well-pitched and robust; only minor flaws (loose syntax keyword heuristics, an "asyndeton/syndetic" label that conflates opposites, an empty hidden label) hold technical back from a 5.
subject: English
name: Close Reading Lab

rationale: A genuinely excellent KS5 app that targets the production-vs-reception distinction at the heart of AO3, with accurate, well-pitched content across three texts and a "weight" mechanic that pushes from sorting into evaluation. Interactivity is real (drag-and-drop plus full keyboard operation, immediate targeted feedback, written-evaluation phase with self-check and TTS model answers); only minor technical limitations (keyword-based reasoning checker, no scroll-into-view for placed chips) hold it back from full marks.
subject: English
name: Production vs Reception

rationale: Genuinely strong KS1 Geography tool: a draggable/keyboard/tap thermometer over a spinnable globe gives immediate colour-coded hot/temperate/cold feedback tied accurately to the Equator-to-Poles NC objective, reinforced by vocabulary banks, exploration-tracking hints and a shuffled six-question quiz. Accessibility (ARIA slider, live regions, TTS, focus-visible) is thorough; only minor technical nits (the generic "grades/attendance" demo modal text mis-fits a geography app, and the globe pointerdown also fires when releasing a thermometer drag, risking an extra reading) keep technical off a 5.
subject: Geography
name: Equator Hot and Cold

rationale: A genuinely excellent KS3 migration tool: push/pull factors are dragged onto source/destination countries with live flow strength, an animated arrow, and differentiated, threshold-driven impacts (remittances, brain drain, urbanisation), while the coach actively surfaces and corrects the classic push-in-destination misconception. Maps precisely onto the KS3 human geography strand (population, migration, urbanisation) with accurate concepts, strong keyboard/ARIA support, and clean responsive design.
subject: Geography
name: Migration Flows Map

rationale: A genuinely excellent KS3 ecosystems simulation: three pressure sliders plus a 40-year timeline drive a thoughtful non-linear model (biodiversity collapses faster than canopy area, irreversible desertification tipping point, indigenous-land security) with a live map, trend chart, gauges and lever-specific feedback that targets common misconceptions. Clean, responsive and well-instrumented for accessibility; only minor blemish is the generic injected demo modal referencing grades and attendance that do not exist in this app.
subject: Geography
name: Rainforest Under Pressure

rationale: Excellent, misconception-aware model of the rainforest nutrient cycle: the constrained drag-the-parcel loop enforces correct directionality, intact vs deforested modes make the broken cycle and leaching visible, and the explained quiz plus teacher notes map cleanly onto GCSE Living World. Technical build is strong (genuine pointer/keyboard/click interaction, live meters, solid ARIA), held off full marks only by a generic data-focused acknowledgement modal that does not fit this non-data app.
subject: Geography
name: Ecosystem Nutrient Cycle

rationale: An exceptionally well-designed app that takes pupils through the entire GCSE fieldwork enquiry cycle (question, sampling, plotting, Spearman's rank, evaluation, conclusion) with misconception-aware distractors at every stage, genuine drag-and-keyboard plotting with proximity feedback, a live statistical calculation, and a standout evaluation task that distinguishes real limitations from distractors specific to the method. Maps cleanly onto AQA/Edexcel KS4 fieldwork requirements with geographically accurate content; only minor technical note is that both datasets are perfectly monotonic so r_s is always 1.00, which is slightly artificial.
subject: Geography
name: Fieldwork Enquiry Builder

rationale: An excellent KS5 Geography systems model: live accumulation/ablation sliders drive net balance, ELA shifts and a lagged snout, with an animated SVG long profile, year-balance strip and four scaffolded challenges. It is explicitly misconception-aware (the response-time lag is modelled and explained), accurate to A-level specs, and strongly accessible (aria-live, sr-only summary, aria-valuetext, SVG title/desc).
subject: Geography
name: Glacier Mass Balance Modeller

rationale: Excellent KS5 Geography simulator that mechanistically distinguishes Malthus (fixed ceiling, positive checks) from Boserup (pressure-driven innovation lifting the ceiling), with four sliders, a live SVG chart, marked crisis point, contextual verdicts, and four scaffolded challenges including a same-drivers divergence task that targets the core A-level skill. Accurate, misconception-aware, strongly accessible; only minor code dead-ends (a no-op live-evaluate stub, a redundant Malthus settle expression) keep technical short of a 5.
subject: Geography
name: Population and Resources Modeller

rationale: Excellent KS5 Geography NEA tool that targets the genuine misconception of test selection (correlation vs association, continuous vs categorical) with misconception-specific diagnostic feedback, then shows full correct Spearman/chi-square working and a live significance slider teaching the critical-value relationship; statistics are accurate and the build is clean, accessible and robust. Loses one mark on technical polish only: the significance slider has just three coarse stops with no visible tick labelling.
subject: Geography
name: Stats Test Chooser

rationale: Teaches the core KS1 disciplinary idea that we learn about the past from real evidence, using three contrasting source types with misconception-aware distractors and a genuine cross-source synthesis task, all wrapped in robust, accessible, read-aloud-enabled interactivity. Loses a point only because there is a single fixed case with no content variation across replays.
subject: History
name: History Detective: Clues from the Past

rationale: Genuinely strong teaching of second-order historical concepts (causation, significance, historical interpretation): pupils sort, rank and justify causes, get per-card and summary feedback that probes reasoning, then a class vote models that historians weigh the same evidence differently. Three accurate KS2-pitched enquiries (Romans, Great Fire, Mayflower), dual drag/keyboard input with ARIA, and the deliberate "no single right answer" framing is exactly the right disciplinary stance.
subject: History
name: Why Did It Happen? Cause Cards

rationale: A sensitively designed, historically accurate four-step KS3 journey (timeline, testimony-matching with clue-based feedback, bystander/resister/rescuer dilemmas grounded in real events, and a remembrance pledge) with genuine manipulation, keyboard-accessible drag-drop, ARIA live regions and clean editorial design. Only minor blemishes (a generic "no real child" demo modal that fits awkwardly here, and clumsy repeated "I will remember by" phrasing in the spoken pledge) keep technical short of a 5.
subject: History
name: Voices of the Holocaust: Why It Matters

rationale: A genuinely excellent KS4 History resource that mirrors the Elizabethan depth study plus historic-environment enquiry, cleanly separating substantive knowledge (themed MCQs with misconception-aware per-option feedback) from disciplinary reasoning (a drag-and-drop inference builder with a deliberate anachronistic decoy clue and a nuanced "weave both claims" verdict). Interactivity, accessibility (full ARIA, keyboard paths, TTS) and editorial design are all strong, with no functional bugs found.
subject: History
name: Elizabethan England Investigator

rationale: An exceptionally well-conceived KS5 History tool that targets the genuinely hard A-level skill of periodisation and cross-strand change/continuity analysis, with historically accurate Tudor events, weighted scoring against recognised break zones, misconception-aware examiner feedback, full keyboard/ARIA accessibility and an editorial design. The thread-placement interaction is the weakest link (date is fixed, only lane matters, and "place all" lets students bypass it), so the real manipulation lives in the marker-sliding and periodisation check rather than the plotting itself.
subject: History
name: Breadth Study Change Mapper

rationale: An exemplary KS5 AO2 tool whose central pedagogical insight (the same source ranging 26 to 92 out of 100 purely as the enquiry changes) is made tangible through a gated three-step workflow with sharp, misconception-aware feedback and genuine drag/keyboard/touch interactivity. Pitch, accuracy and accessibility are all strong; held off a perfect 20 on technical only because the curated clause bank means students assemble rather than compose, and the gating logic (Step 3 unlocks after visiting any two enquiries) is slightly permissive.
subject: History
name: Source Value NOP Workbench

rationale: A polished KS1 (Year 2) Statistics app that maps precisely onto the NC pictogram/tally strand: pupils sort objects, watch a live 1:1 pictogram and tally chart build from their own data, then answer auto-generated totalling and comparison questions (how many, most/fewest, sums, differences) with gentle try-again scaffolding. Interactivity is genuine and multi-modal (pointer drag, keyboard, tap, TTS, undo) with strong accessibility; minor technical niggles only (the combining-stroke tally gate glyph renders inconsistently across fonts, and numberOptions can occasionally yield fewer than four distractors).
subject: Maths
name: Sort and Count Pictogram

rationale: A genuinely strong CPA-aligned manipulative: four accurate, well-pitched KS3 modes (collecting like terms, expanding single brackets, factorising via array, balance-method solving) with real drag-and-drop, zero-pair cancellation, a live-updating symbolic expression and specific misconception-aware feedback. Marked down only on technical: the say() helper writes to a dead data-say attribute rather than a live region so several screen-reader announcements never fire, and free-placement factorise row-detection is fragile.
subject: Maths
name: Algebra Tiles Workbench

rationale: An excellent KS4 statistics tool that targets the core frequency-density misconception head-on: dragging handles or editing class widths makes bars rescale by density in real time, with adaptive feedback that flags when the modal class diverges from the highest-frequency class and accurate CF-interpolated median/IQR. Manipulation, alignment to GCSE histograms, and the reasoning quiz are all strong; only minor technical roughness (coarse drag-to-frequency rounding for wide classes) keeps it short of full marks.
subject: Maths
name: Histogram and Frequency Density

rationale: An exemplary KS4 algebra tool that solves any quadratic three ways (factorising, completing the square, formula) side by side, all live-linked to the discriminant and the curve's x-axis crossings, with a misconception-aware challenge giving targeted diagnostic feedback. Mathematically accurate with exact surd/fraction rendering and strong accessibility; only minor technical edges (occasional stale aria-label on the probe readout, rare repeated-root challenge items) keep it from a perfect technical score.
subject: Maths
name: Quadratic Solver Studio

rationale: An unusually complete KS4 probability tool: a live tree where branches auto-multiply and outcomes auto-add, a correct with/without-replacement toggle that recomputes conditional second-stage branches, a self-marking question bank (including "at least one" via complement and "one of each" via two added paths) with genuinely misconception-aware diagnostics that name the slip (added not multiplied, used the with-replacement value, found only one order, gave P(no A)), plus a bonus conditional Venn lab. Maths is accurate and pitched squarely at GCSE Higher; accessibility (live SVG descriptions, keyboard-editable branches, aria) is strong, with only the prompt()-based branch editing and the decimal-to-/1000 fraction conversion holding technical back from a 5.
subject: Maths
name: Tree Diagram Builder

rationale: A genuinely excellent A-level statistics tool: dragging points live-updates PMCC, the least-squares line, residuals, r-squared and SSR, with misconception-aware narration (outlier influence, weak-correlation caution) and an on-spec sampling tie-in (census vs systematic vs random) plus a linked data table and quick check. Robust, responsive SVG with full keyboard and screen-reader accessibility; the only quibble is labelling standard deviation as population sigma, a defensible but not universal A-level choice.
subject: Maths
name: Correlation and Regression Studio

rationale: An excellent KS5 mechanics lab: build a free-body diagram by drag/drop or keyboard, then a live solver correctly resolves forces, handles the static/kinetic friction threshold and connected-particle tension (T = m_B(g - a) verified across both motion directions), with a line-by-line working panel, predict-before-reveal challenges with sensible tolerances, animation, and strong ARIA/keyboard support. It is accurate, misconception-aware (rest vs motion, shared acceleration) and pitched precisely at A-level M1, with only minor polish issues keeping technical off a 5.
subject: Maths
name: Force Diagram Lab

rationale: An excellent, misconception-aware KS5 Pure tool: live a/b/c/d transformation of six base functions with highlighted algebra, accurate live domain/range, plus genuine extension via a mapping diagram (composite/inverse), a self-marking challenge engine with targeted hints, and an orderable proof builder. Maps cleanly onto the A-level graph-transformation and functions content at the right pitch; only minor technical edges (b=0 degenerates silently for some bases, and the graph-focus arrow keys quirkily nudge only d) keep it from a perfect technical score.
subject: Maths
name: Function Transformer

rationale: An exceptionally well-built KS5 Pure numerical-methods tool: three coherent modes (Newton-Raphson tangent iteration, change-of-sign bracketing, trapezium rule) all backed by correct derivatives, exact antiderivative integrals and accurate root values, with strong misconception-aware feedback (turning-point divergence, moving-away detection, even-number-of-roots caveat) and genuine pointer plus keyboard interactivity with live ARIA. It maps precisely onto A-level numerical methods; only minor technical niggles (signed-area "area under the curve" wording for sub-axis curves, a slightly fiddly keyboard sign-preview) keep technical off a 5.
subject: Maths
name: Newton-Raphson Visualiser

rationale: An excellent KS2 French sentence-builder whose live adjective-noun gender agreement (auto-switching noir/noire, with explanatory feedback and a masc/fem subject chooser) targets exactly the right NC grammar concept, backed by three accessible input modes, French TTS and a class gallery. Technically strong but not flawless: the gallery remove handler closes over a stale index, so deleting a non-top card can remove the wrong sentence.
subject: Modern Foreign Languages
name: Phrase Remix (MFL KS2 French)

rationale: Excellent KS2 MFL speaking tool: models a two-way exchange then scaffolds substitution practice toward oral production, with thoughtfully chosen distractors carrying specific error feedback, multi-language support, TTS modelling, and strong accessibility (full keyboard path, ARIA, focus outlines, 44px targets). Marked down only on technical accuracy because the French/Spanish/German lacks accents, a real flaw in a language-teaching app.
fixIt: Restore diacritics throughout the DATA object (e.g. "âge", "préférée", "Comment t'appelles-tu", "Cómo", "años", "heißt") so the modelled target language is orthographically correct; also trim the demo-modal boilerplate referencing "grades and attendance figures", which is irrelevant to a speaking app.
subject: Modern Foreign Languages
name: Two-Way Talk Cards (MFL KS2 Speaking)

rationale: Excellent KS3 MFL listening trainer with genuine pedagogy: chunked replay, adjustable speed, Levenshtein-based near-miss detection that separates spelling slips from genuine misses, per-word reveal, and targeted coaching driven by the learner's actual errors across Foundation/Higher French, German and Spanish clips. Well built and accessible; the only real limitation is reliance on device SpeechSynthesis and accent-stripped source text, which can degrade audio fidelity, though it fails gracefully.
subject: Modern Foreign Languages
name: Catch the Detail (MFL Listening)

rationale: A genuinely well-built MFL retrieval tool: a real SM-2-style scheduler resurfaces missed words, accent-tolerant Levenshtein matching gives nuanced correct/close/wrong feedback with per-character diffs, and curated false-friend coaching plus TTS pronunciation are accurate and exam-aligned across French, Spanish and German. Lightly held back only by no cross-session persistence (state resets on reload, so the spaced effect is bounded to one sitting) and a purely cosmetic leaderboard.
subject: Modern Foreign Languages
name: Theme Vocab Sprint (MFL KS4)

rationale: An genuinely strong KS5 MFL lab: bidirectional idiom-heavy translation across FR/DE/ES with listening mode, calque detection, a literal-vs-idiomatic pitfall panel and examiner-band feedback explicitly modelled on AQA/Edexcel A-level descriptors. Marking is keyword-based (brittle, and the "From English" direction checks only set-phrase keywords without grammar/accuracy), and content is thin (DE/ES have a single passage each), which keeps technical from a 5.
subject: Modern Foreign Languages
name: Advanced Translation Lab

rationale: A linguistically precise, genuinely A-level tool: the two-step "judge the mood, then conjugate" workflow with a live justification panel builds the metalinguistic reasoning that distinguishes strong KS5 linguists, and the content deliberately targets the classic over-application traps (espérer que vs vouloir que, French si-clauses, affirmative vs negative creer que, cuando + future, German Konjunktiv I reported speech vs Konjunktiv II conditionals) across three languages. Clean accessible build (ARIA roles, focus-visible, 44px targets, native button keyboard handling, language-aware TTS, sticky live regions) with all logic inline and no network calls.
subject: Modern Foreign Languages
name: Subjunctive Studio (KS5 MFL)

rationale: An excellent A-level MFL tool that trains the precise essay-evidence skill (argument vs statistic) across the four French societal themes, using authentic-style quotes with English glosses, pair-matching and auto-generated bilingual evidence paragraphs plus model French exemplars. Interactivity is rich and genuinely accessible (drag-and-drop with ghost plus full keyboard path and live ARIA feedback), with only minor technical blemishes.
subject: Modern Foreign Languages
name: Theme Dossier Builder (A-level MFL)

rationale: A genuinely excellent EYFS RE picture-book app: four traditions handled reverently, multi-scene tappable stories with audio, a constructive "what happened next" choice game, and reflective "I wonder" questions that mirror real EYFS RE pedagogy. Self-contained with hand-drawn SVG art, strong ARIA/accessibility, listen toggle and keyboard nav; only minor technical nits keep the fourth dimension off a 5.
subject: Religious Education
name: Story Carpet

rationale: An excellent, self-contained KS1 RE explorer: four faith interiors with pannable SVG scenes, tappable hotspots that accurately name and explain each feature in age-appropriate language, plus a checklist, progress bar, TTS and an unlockable retrieval quiz, all directly delivering the catalogue brief. Technically robust (offline SVG, pointer-capture drag, keyboard and ARIA support), docked one point because the "360-degree" pan is a flat non-wrapping horizontal scroll and the injected demo modal carries data-app boilerplate ("student records, grades, attendance") that does not fit an RE explorer.
subject: Religious Education
name: Inside the Place of Worship

rationale: A genuinely well-built KS2 RE matching activity: each of the 12 practices carries a specific, accurate "why" explanation, wrong placements give directional feedback ("belongs to the Torah, look at the clue there") rather than just marking wrong, and the closing "big idea" panel surfaces the shared-reverence concept rather than leaving it implicit. Technically strong with full keyboard parity, ARIA live announcements, a working undo, shuffle, reset, and clean pointer-based drag/drop with hit-testing.
subject: Religious Education
name: Handle With Reverence

rationale: Pedagogically careful KS2 RE comparison app: theologically nuanced child-friendly statements grouped by belief-about-God, an explore-then-test design with story/symbol scaffolds, and a quiz that gives per-option formative feedback explaining why each choice fits or not. Clean, accessible, self-contained code with proper tablist ARIA, keyboard navigation, TTS and the required demo-data safeguard.
subject: Religious Education
name: What Do They Believe About God?

rationale: Genuinely strong KS3 RE app: the "why this branch exists" framing teaches intra-faith diversity through historical causation (1054, 1517, the 632 CE succession question) rather than rote facts, with belief/practice/authority tagging that scaffolds the disciplinary lens RE demands; content is accurate, balanced and appropriately caveated, and the detective quiz reinforces the same causal reasoning. Clean, accessible, self-contained build (ARIA states, keyboard support, TTS, demo-data block) with no errors.
subject: Religious Education
name: One Religion, Many Branches

rationale: A genuinely strong KS4 RE tool: the AO2 level-of-response model is sound, the deliberate weak-filler blocks teach pupils to distinguish developed reasoning from vague assertion, and the priority-ordered live coaching gives precise formative feedback. Excellent interactivity (custom drag-and-drop with full keyboard, ARIA and TTS fallbacks) and clean, robust code; held off a perfect alignment mark only because it is mono-faith Christian scripture and uses fixed exemplar blocks rather than pupil-authored text.
subject: Religious Education
name: 12-Mark Essay Builder

rationale: Excellent KS4 RE lab that scaffolds the teleological and cosmological arguments premise-by-premise, then stress-tests them with accurately named GCSE critiques (Hume, Darwin, Russell, fine-tuning, problem of evil) and an explicit "evaluate" judgement task; varied, keyboard-accessible interaction and tight syllabus alignment to the Existence of God and revelation strand. Loses a technical point only for keyword-heuristic (rather than genuinely evaluative) feedback on the free-text judgement and a generic demo-modal that references "student records/grades/attendance" irrelevant to this app.
subject: Religious Education
name: Does God Exist? Argument Lab

rationale: A polished, GCSE-aligned RE revision tool with a clear three-step pedagogy (pick, study quote-evidence cards, sort common/divergent) tied explicitly to the "explain two similar/contrasting beliefs" exam skill, strong feedback, and excellent accessibility (full keyboard drag alternative, ARIA, live regions). Loses a pedagogy point because the common/divergent labels are fixed per card rather than computed against the actual two religions chosen, so some "common" tags can mislead for certain pairings (e.g. Buddhism on the nature of God).
subject: Religious Education
name: GCSE Beliefs Revision Engine

rationale: A genuinely strong A-level RE tool: the predict-then-test loop targets the assessable skill (reasoning from each theory's method, not its conclusion), the contrast explorer and verdict matrix surface methodological fault lines precisely, and the content is accurate to standard exam readings (double effect, agape, phronesis, categorical imperative, hedonic calculus) with a footnote honestly flagging that verdicts are defensible. Technically clean: escaped HTML, accessible fieldsets/ARIA-live regions, TTS, demo-notice block, and intellectually honest "depends" handling (e.g. Bentham on the transplant case).
subject: Religious Education
name: Ethical Theory Workbench

rationale: A polished, highly accessible EYFS Science app with a sound two-phase pedagogy (discover then classify by observable features) that is explicitly mapped to the Understanding the World: The Natural World ELG, with feature-teaching feedback on wrong answers. Interaction is rich and inclusive (custom pointer drag plus keyboard/tap chooser, TTS, ARIA live regions), and it matches its catalogue brief exactly.
subject: Science
name: Minibeast Hunt

rationale: Excellent KS1 Science resource: the alive/dead/never-alive distinction is taught explicitly via MRS GREN, every item carries an age-appropriate explanatory clue, wrong answers give targeted Socratic hints rather than just "try again", and a second habitat-matching round extends the living-things-and-habitats strand directly. Interaction is genuinely strong with pointer-event drag-and-drop, full tap-to-select and keyboard (Enter/Space) fallbacks, TTS narration, and accessible ARIA roles/live regions throughout.
subject: Science
name: Alive, Dead or Never Alive?

rationale: A genuinely strong KS1 working-scientifically tool that scaffolds comparative/fair testing through five logical steps, with misconception-targeting feedback that explains why a variable must stay the same rather than just marking it wrong. Clean, accessible code (radiogroup ARIA, keyboard arrows, focus management, TTS for pre-readers) and tight alignment to the KS1 fair-test strand; interactivity is solid though largely on rails given the single scripted scenario.
subject: Science
name: Test It! Fair Test Maker

rationale: Exemplary KS2 classification app that correctly models a dichotomous key, rejects non-discriminating splits with specific reasoning, and lets pupils build, check and live-test the key on a randomised mystery animal with honest trait-based evaluation and a life-cycle extension. Accessible (keyboard path, ARIA live regions, focus-visible) and squarely on the KS2 'Living things and their habitats' curriculum.
subject: Science
name: Build a Classification Key

rationale: Excellent KS2 electricity app that cleanly separates the three failure/success states (broken loop, switch off, conductor vs insulator) with accurate per-object explanations and a sorting tray, all curriculum-true to Year 4 NC. Polished, accessible build with drag-and-drop plus a full keyboard path, animated circuit feedback, TTS, and progress tracking; only minor blemish is the generic demo modal text about "grades/attendance" that doesn't fit a science manipulative.
subject: Science
name: Light the Bulb (KS2 Electricity)

rationale: A genuinely strong KS3 interdependence simulator: a properly-implemented predator-prey-producer model (logistic grass regrowth, saturating predation, fox starvation) drives a live pyramid of numbers, a multi-series time chart and a gas-exchange lab that correctly links photosynthesis/respiration to populations, with contextual diagnostic feedback, presets, TTS and solid accessibility. Maps tightly onto the KS3 biology curriculum (food chains, interdependence, ecosystems, gas exchange) with clear teacher aim and pupil-facing language.
subject: Science
name: Ecosystem Balancer

rationale: A genuinely excellent KS3 photosynthesis model: the limiting-factor maths is sound, the live word equation, dynamic feedback that names the limiting factor, the four self-checking investigation challenges and the respiration caveat together give strong conceptual coverage with real cause-and-effect interactivity. Maps cleanly to the KS3 "material cycles and energy / photosynthesis" curriculum, with accessibility (ARIA, aria-live, TTS) handled well; only the boilerplate demo modal copy referring to "student records, names, grades, attendance" is mismatched to this non-data app.
subject: Science
name: Leaf Factory (KS3)

rationale: A polished, genuinely investigative KS3 tool that has pupils collect class data, classify it as continuous or discontinuous, auto-plot a correctly binned SVG histogram with adaptive insight text, and bridge cleanly from variation to natural selection via two graded MCQs; strong NC alignment, accessibility (SR data table, focus management, aria) and input validation. Loses a point on technical/depth only because the natural-selection question is a fixed scenario decoupled from the surveyed trait and the quiz is limited to two items.
subject: Science
name: Variation Survey

rationale: An exemplary KS4 Physics simulation covering the full I-V required practical: live, comparable curves for an ohmic resistor, filament lamp and diode, region-aware coaching grounded in the particle model, a circuit with power-proportional lamp glow, point-plotting, and a shuffled predict-then-check quiz. Physics is qualitatively correct, accessibility is strong, and UK terminology (p.d.) is used throughout; the diode model parameters are illustrative rather than physically exact, but this is appropriate and acknowledged.
subject: Science
name: I-V Characteristics

rationale: Outstanding KS4 physics simulator: a real-time Newtonian model drives coupled distance-time and velocity-time graphs with shaded area-under-curve, contextual feedback, momentum and stopping-distance breakdowns, and a graded read-off challenge that mirrors exactly what GCSE exams ask. Tightly aligned to the specification with strong accessibility; only let down technically by messy, partly dead car-positioning code and noisy frame-by-frame acceleration values.
subject: Science
name: Motion Graph Lab

rationale: A genuinely excellent KS4 biology simulation that embeds a full predict-observe-explain cycle: coupled concentration sliders drive an accurate live SVG of osmosis, a recordable mass-change graph that interpolates the isotonic point, a prediction quiz, and well-pitched extension content (exam definitions, magnification, cell cycle), all with strong accessibility. The only minor blemish is that recording runs while varying the inside concentration can blur the plotted trend line, but this is a fair modelled approximation that the footnote openly flags.
subject: Science
name: Osmosis Visking Lab

rationale: A genuinely excellent KS5 biology simulation: four transport mechanisms with mechanism-aware dynamic feedback, an A-level-accurate challenge targeting the classic "active transport moves against the gradient" misconception, and strong spec links (Fick's law, water potential, channel vs carrier saturation, ATP). Technically robust and self-contained, though the physics is deliberately qualitative and the pump direction is hard-coded rather than derived from the live gradient.
subject: Science
name: Membrane Transport Explorer

rationale: A rigorous, well-sequenced KS5 particle physics tool: hadron building with a live charge/baryon-number ledger, a conservation lab that correctly distinguishes charge, baryon and lepton number violations, and dynamically drawn Feynman-style diagrams with accurate physics throughout. Strong accessibility (tablist with arrow-key nav, aria-live, accessible SVG) and clean vanilla JS, with only minor polish issues holding back a perfect technical score.
subject: Science
name: Particle Zoo

rationale: A genuinely strong KS5 chemistry tool with a locked predict-then-check loop, rich pointer-and-keyboard drag-and-drop, scoring, and exam-board-linked teacher notes mapping coordination number, shape, ligand substitution and Delta-oct to colour. Minor technical compromises (four sites abstractly standing in for six octahedral donors, an unreachable square-planar option, and boilerplate demo-modal text about student records that does not fit a chemistry sim) keep it just short of perfect.
subject: Science
name: Transition Metal Complexes

rationale: A genuinely strong KS3 tool: students drag control points to shape a smoothed tension curve and place labelled events, supported by six structural models (Freytag, three/five-act, Hero's Journey, Kishoten Ketsu) and eight set-text examples (Macbeth, Jekyll and Hyde, Animal Farm), with full mouse/touch/pen and keyboard-accessible interaction, ARIA live feedback and PNG export. It maps cleanly onto the KS3 reading and writing curriculum's structural-analysis strand; the only pedagogic gap is that it presents structure without any self-check, prompt or misconception-aware scaffolding to push students toward justifying their curve against textual evidence.
subject: English
name: Tension Curve Builder

rationale: A polished, well-scaffolded KS1 clock tool with genuine two-hand dragging, 5-minute snapping, hour rollover, and a proportionally-moving hour hand, presenting analogue, digital and word forms together across a sensible difficulty progression that maps precisely onto the Year 1/2 telling-the-time curriculum. Loses marks only for minimal corrective feedback beyond revealing the answer and no keyboard/screen-reader access to the clock.
subject: Maths
name: Clock Explorer

rationale: Genuinely strong KS2 measurement tool: draw rectangles and compound shapes by drag or touch, with a correct exposed-edge perimeter algorithm that handles concavities, live area/perimeter/dimension readouts, formula reveal for true rectangles, visual perimeter highlighting, and a challenge mode constraining both area and perimeter (surfacing the key misconception that same-area shapes can have different perimeters). Mathematically accurate and well-pitched to Years 4-6.
subject: Maths
name: Area & Perimeter Explorer

rationale: A polished, genuinely interactive angle tool: drag-to-form rays with live measurement, protractor scale, dynamic arc/right-angle symbol, and four well-pitched modes (explore, make, classify, estimate) that build real angle sense for KS2 geometry. Robust pointer handling and responsive SVG; held off top marks only on accessibility (no keyboard drag or SVG ARIA) and a touch of reflex/full-turn content that drifts toward KS3.
subject: Maths
name: Angle Explorer

rationale: A polished, mathematically correct simulator covering dice (1d6 and 2d6), coins, cards (three classifications) and a custom weighted spinner, with theoretical probabilities computed accurately and experimental relative frequency surfaced live against them via chart, frequency table and summary stats, making the law-of-large-numbers convergence visible across 1 to 10,000 trials. It maps cleanly onto KS3/GCSE probability (relative frequency, equally and unequally likely outcomes, with 2d6 a strong misconception target) and is genuinely interactive with thoughtful aria-live accessibility; it loses a point on pedagogy only because it is a sandbox with no built-in questions or guided prompts framing the convergence idea for pupils.
subject: Maths
name: Probability Simulator

rationale: Exceptionally strong, intuition-first treatment of significance, confidence intervals and sample size, with rich linked-slider interactivity, a live canvas bell curve, a working calculator and an explicit misconception card ("Not Significant does not mean Not Good"). Maths is sound apart from a subtle inconsistency: the significance badge uses a CI-overlap rule (sample SE) while the reported p-value uses a hypothesis-test z-score, so they can disagree at the boundary.
subject: Maths
name: Significance & Confidence Intervals

rationale: A polished, mathematically accurate A-Level binomial explorer: the live canvas chart with PMF/CDF toggle, hover detail, auto-updating mean/variance/SD/mode and skew commentary gives genuine real-time manipulation, backed by correct worked examples, exam-style extract/solution reveals and a code-words cheat sheet well pitched to Edexcel/AQA Statistics. Strong keyboard and pointer accessibility on the canvas lift the technical quality; it stops just short of top marks on pedagogy because misconception work is limited to the cheat-sheet table rather than targeted diagnostic feedback.
subject: Maths
name: Binomial Distribution

rationale: An exceptionally well-built cell-biology resource: clickable/hover/keyboard-accessible canvas diagrams of eukaryotic and prokaryotic cells, working standard-form and orders-of-magnitude calculators, a shuffled 10-question quiz with misconception-aware feedback, a comparison table and revealable key terms. The build quality and cognitive demand are excellent, but the content is plainly pitched at GCSE/KS4 (spec codes WS 1.2 / MS 1b,2a, "11 Biology", 70S/80S Svedberg, peptidoglycan) rather than the KS3 label in the catalogue.
fixIt: Alignment: the material is GCSE-pitched but catalogued as KS3. Either re-label it KS4, or add a KS3 mode that hides the 70S/80S Svedberg, peptidoglycan and spec-code detail and softens the maths-skills demands to match the lower-secondary NC pitch.
subject: Science
name: Biology Cells Demo

rationale: A genuinely strong KS3/KS4 tool: live SVG Sankey rendering, accurate presets (the incandescent-vs-LED contrast is pedagogically pointed), real-time efficiency and conservation-balance feedback that directly tackles the "energy disappears" misconception, plus a full custom builder. Minor caveats: useful/wasted ribbons are zone-clamped so widths aren't strictly joule-proportional in edge cases, the Food Chain preset stretches a physics-device frame onto biology, and range inputs lack ARIA labelling.
subject: Science
name: Energy Transfer Sankey Builder

rationale: A polished, genuinely interactive EM spectrum explorer with a draggable pointer, synced wavelength/frequency sliders, region buttons and an animated wave, all driven by real physics (c=fλ, E=hf computed from constants) with immediate, accurate feedback. Strong accessibility (ARIA slider, keyboard control, live region, responsive) and good misconception awareness lift it to tier A; minor content quibbles (overlapping radio/microwave wavelength labels, slightly artificial dual-slider pairing) hold it just short of full marks.
subject: Science
name: EM Spectrum Explorer

rationale: A well-built KS4 half-life simulator that genuinely manipulates sample size, half-life and decay model while overlaying theoretical against simulated curves, explicitly targeting the randomness misconception and mirroring the classic dice experiment. Accurate, accessible (ARIA live status, labelled canvases) and responsive; minor blemishes are duplicate dead resize code and a generic safeguarding-modal wording ("grades, attendance") that does not fit a physics tool.
subject: Science
name: Radioactive Decay Simulator

rationale: An excellent, conceptually rich KS4 simulation: four live sliders drive a coloured net-gas-exchange graph with annotated compensation and light-saturation points, plus gauges, limiting-factor bars and an enzyme-temperature visual, all accurately modelled and accompanied by sound GCSE explanation. Marked down slightly because the displayed limiting-factor bars use a different formula from the underlying rate model (so "lowest factor is limiting" does not always tie cleanly to the curve), and the boilerplate "no real child" demo modal is mismatched to a non-data simulation.
subject: Science
name: Photosynthesis & Respiration Balance

rationale: A polished, accurate KS3 tectonics simulator: all three boundary types animate live with drag, button and keyboard control, convection currents, a stress-to-earthquake mechanic and an 8-question scored quiz, backed by correct terminology and real-world examples (Andes, Himalayas, Mid-Atlantic Ridge, San Andreas) and unusually strong accessibility (ARIA, live-region announcer, pointer/keyboard parity). Marked down slightly because the quiz only tests boundary-type naming rather than processes/landforms, and the generic injected "student records/grades" demo-data modal reads as nonsensical for this subject.
subject: Geography
name: Plate Tectonics

rationale: A genuinely strong KS3 hydrological-cycle sandbox where four inputs drive six coupled flow rates, correctly modelling the impermeable-surface runoff-versus-infiltration trade-off that targets the key urbanisation/flood-risk misconception, with live particles, snow/rain thresholds and nine clickable explanatory zones. Accessibility (keyboard zone buttons, ARIA live region, pointer parity) is well above average; minor marks lost for the irrelevant fabricated-child demo modal and unitless, somewhat arbitrary rate values.
subject: Geography
name: Water Cycle Interactive

rationale: An excellent, content-rich climate-graph tool offering three genuine modes (single, compare, build-your-own) with live dual-axis Chart.js rendering, hemisphere-aware Koppen classification and well-judged analytical prompts that suit KS3 geography. Koppen is enrichment beyond core KS3 but framed appropriately, and the data is accurate; the only blemish is a contextually mismatched demo-data modal referencing student records that do not exist in this app.
subject: Geography
name: Climate Graph Builder

rationale: A genuinely sophisticated, accurate KS3 tool for the second-order concept of cause and consequence: students drag historically sound cards into short/medium/long-term lanes and draw cascading causal arrows, with intended-vs-unintended framing built in. Polished drag-and-drop with proper touch support, lane-snapping, connect mode and undo make it strongly interactive; the only real limit is that it structures thinking without ever evaluating the chain, so it depends on teacher mediation.
subject: History
name: Consequence Chain Builder

rationale: Genuinely strong teaching tool built on Partington's 5Rs, a real second-order significance framework, with accurate UK-pitched KS4 content (1066, WWII causes, Industrial Revolution, Civil Rights) and an exemplary stance that there is no single correct ranking, only justified reasoning. Live star ratings, custom radar charts, leaderboard and an explicitly-caveated historian comparison make it richly interactive; only minor gaps (no persistence/export of work, single fabricated expert weighting) hold it back from perfect.
subject: History
name: Significance Ranking

rationale: A genuinely strong KS4/GCSE History tool: four historiographically accurate debates with a Claim/Evidence/Judgement/Opinion annotation taxonomy, live Venn, evidence meters and a structured evaluation prompt that train the core "compare interpretations" skill. Held just short of full marks because the rich pre-coded answer keys (claims/agreements/disagreements arrays) are never used to give the student corrective feedback, and the Venn's "shared" count double-counts both panels rather than identifying genuinely shared claims.
subject: History
name: Interpretations Comparator

rationale: A historiographically literate source-analysis tool: the supportive/critical x personal/official grid, nuanced bias notes (Fischer Controversy, drain theory) and deliberate trap sources (the "anti-suffrage" postcard that is actually pro-suffrage) teach that bias does not equal unreliability, mapping cleanly to KS3/KS4 source-enquiry skills. Genuine drag-placement with a self-check guide overlay, clusters and four accurate topics, let down only by mouse-only placement with no keyboard or touch path for the core interaction.
subject: History
name: Bias & Perspective Spectrum

rationale: A genuinely pattern-first conjugation explorer with strong cognitive demand: it surfaces the silent-ending insight, handles -ger/-cer spelling changes correctly, explicitly warns when a verb is irregular and the regular forms cannot be trusted, and pairs a randomised, rule-explained quiz with audio, phonetics, comparisons and context sentences. Pitched well for KS3 MFL; only minor flaws (custom -ir verbs default to the -iss- pattern, over-eager verb-highlighting regex, and a mismatched safeguarding modal about "student records/grades") hold back the alignment and technical scores.
subject: Modern Foreign Languages
name: French Verb Conjugation

rationale: A genuinely strong KS3 MFL planning tool with a real French grammar engine (gender agreement, -er conjugation, passé composé, elision, adjective agreement) that keeps generated material grammatically valid, plus six material types, model answers, a teacher/student answer toggle, browser TTS and a self-marking quiz with per-item feedback. Accurately pitched to the KS3 MFL National Curriculum and robustly built; minor limits are thin irregular-verb coverage and the incongruous safeguarding modal.
subject: Modern Foreign Languages
name: French Word Planner

rationale: Outstanding KS3 MFL tool: three languages, graded sentences, role-coloured tiles, and a parallel English-structure reveal with per-sentence explanations that explicitly target genuine cross-linguistic misconceptions (ne...pas, French pronoun fronting, German V2/verb-bracket/subordinate verb-final, Spanish gustar inversion and pro-drop). Interactivity is rich (drag, click, touch) with immediate meaningful feedback; alignment and technical lose a mark because the checker only accepts one canonical order (rejecting linguistically valid alternatives, e.g. optional pro-drop subjects) and accents are stripped throughout.
subject: Modern Foreign Languages
name: Word Order Puzzle

rationale: A genuinely strong KS3 RE tool: the parallel-coordinates chart lets pupils spot cross-religion similarities and intra-faith diversity (Catholic/Quaker, Sunni/Sufi, Orthodox/Reform) across six well-chosen worship dimensions, backed by accurate vocabulary, detailed practice cards and high-demand evaluative reflection questions. It is careful to frame axis positions as tendencies not absolutes, which is misconception-aware and pitched well above a simplistic "religions are all the same" treatment.
subject: Religious Education
name: Worship Practices Comparator

rationale: A genuinely strong KS4 RS tool: the drag-and-drop classification is built around Kohlberg's core teaching point (judge the reasoning, not the conclusion), with same-stage responses reaching opposite conclusions to attack that exact misconception, plus a substantial Critique tab (cultural/gender bias, reasoning-action gap, Gilligan) that delivers the AO3 evaluation GCSE specs require. Content is accurate, UK English, and well pitched; the only real weakness is the lack of true keyboard/screen-reader access to the drag interaction.
subject: Religious Education
