name: Moral Development Stages

rationale: Excellent KS4 RE resource: six escalating trolley variations (classic, footbridge, loop, transplant, bystander, self-sacrifice) each analysed accurately against utilitarianism, Kantian deontology, virtue ethics and situation ethics, surfacing the genuine philosophical distinctions (means vs side-effect, rule utilitarianism, Kant's duties to self) that anchor the AQA/Edexcel/OCR ethics specs. Branching choices, animated canvas diagrams, a running framework tally and an emergent ethical profile give real manipulation with meaningful feedback, with thoughtful screen-reader status text and a clean responsive design.
subject: Religious Education
name: Trolley Problem Variations

rationale: A genuinely strong KS4 RS ethics tool: five substantive, exam-relevant scenarios with accurate, perspective-specific weightings and scriptural references that surface how worldviews prioritise rights versus responsibilities differently. The drag-and-drop see-saw and discussion prompts give real interactivity, though the balance mechanic is more visual metaphor than analytical model.
subject: Religious Education
name: Rights & Responsibilities Balancer

rationale: A polished, accurate CPD tool covering the revised Bloom's taxonomy with a genuine drag-and-drop sorting activity (desktop and touch), immediate feedback, scoring, and well-pitched KS3/KS4 subject examples. Fit for professional purpose; only minor blemishes (a mislabelled "generate" that merely samples existing stems, and no keyboard alternative to drag-and-drop) hold it back from full marks.
subject: CPD & Staff Tools
name: Bloom's Taxonomy Question Builder

rationale: A genuinely useful CPD instrument: clear professional purpose (questioning equity), real-time click-to-record heatmap, correctly implemented Gini-based equity score, layered alerts, multiple seating layouts, undo, name/gender tagging and a clean text export, all grounded in named research. Minor flaws keep it off full marks: the horseshoe layout renders only 20 of the 30 seats while coverage stats still divide by 30, and there is no persistence (a page refresh wipes the session).
subject: CPD & Staff Tools
name: Questioning Distribution Heatmap

rationale: A polished, genuinely interactive CPD wait-time tool: real-time ring timer, spacebar control, live dot plot, running stats and zone analytics, all grounded in Rowe (1974) with sensible thresholds and good responsive/ARIA design. Minor marks off only for an opinionated zone framing (labelling 3-7s "good" rather than centring Rowe's 3-second threshold) and a cosmetic display-rounding quirk above 10s.
subject: CPD & Staff Tools
name: Wait Time Analyser

rationale: A genuinely strong CPD tool for responsive teaching: misconception-aware by design, with a click-to-cycle 30-pupil grid, live canvas pie and stacked-bar charts (with aria-live text descriptions), named pupil grouping and concrete intervention prompts, plus four credibly pitched subject presets. Marked down only for no persistence (refresh loses all data), one garbled maths preset misconception, and percentages computed against responded rather than whole class.
subject: CPD & Staff Tools
name: Exit Ticket Response Analyser

rationale: A genuinely strong staff/CPD data tool: draggable boundary lines on a generated mark distribution with live grade cards, stacked bar, 4+/5+/7+ metrics, presets and distribution shapes, backed by an accurate, well-pitched explainer on comparable outcomes and awarding. Technically excellent with DPR-aware canvas, unified pointer events, full keyboard control and ARIA live announcements; only slight pedagogic caveat is that free boundary-dragging inverts real awarding, though the info panel addresses this.
subject: Data & Analytics
name: Grade Boundary Explorer

rationale: A polished, professionally fit data tool for analysing grade movement between two assessment points, pairing a linked transition matrix and Sankey diagram with live editing, filters, export and a genuinely sophisticated limitations section. Strong accessibility (aria-live flow summary, focusable canvas, pointer/touch support) lifts it; minor gaps are no persistence and click-only matrix cells.
subject: Data & Analytics
name: Transition Matrix

rationale: A polished, technically robust value-added demonstrator that computes real least-squares regression, residuals, R-squared and outliers live on filtered data, with genuine manipulation (group filters, axis switch, draggable what-if line with touch support) and immediate feedback. Fit for leadership/CPD purpose; only slightly oversells rigour (the confidence band is a flat plus-or-minus 1 residual SD rather than a true prediction interval, and the what-if line is illustrative, not recomputed).
subject: Data & Analytics
name: Value Added Scatter Plot

rationale: A polished, genuinely interactive EYFS small-world drama tool: drag-and-place characters across three scenes plus a thoughtful 5-step narrative scaffold (character, setting, goal, problem, resolution) where children author their own lines and a chosen character speaks them aloud in UK English via SpeechSynthesis, deliberately keeping authorship with the child rather than auto-filling. Strong accessibility (keyboard placement, arrow-key movement, ARIA, focus rings) and clean, bug-free pointer-event code lift it to tier A; pitch fits EYFS Communication and Language / Expressive Arts and Design well.
subject: Drama
name: Story Stage

rationale: A polished, genuinely interactive KS2 drama stimulus generator that rises above a novelty randomiser by layering in skill-focus objectives, success criteria, peer feedback and reflection prompts well matched to KS2 spoken-language and drama skills. Technically robust with eased canvas spinners, careful selection maths, keyboard/ARIA accessibility, a timer and clean responsive design.
subject: Drama
name: Improvisation Spinner

rationale: A genuinely strong KS1 sentence-construction tool: it teaches capital letters, full stops and word order with misconception-aware, differentiated feedback (right-words-wrong-order vs wrong-words, first-word vs "always" capitals like "I"), accepts multiple valid orderings, and adds read-aloud and accessible markup. Pitch and vocabulary map accurately onto the Y1/Y2 English National Curriculum.
subject: English
name: Sentence Builder

rationale: A robust, well-aligned KS1 everyday-materials app that uses a genuine multi-select sort with item-level feedback distinguishing wrong picks from missed objects, plus spoken explanations of why each property applies. Strong NC fit, clean accessible build, and meaningful (if KS1-shallow) cognitive demand; a few property tags (book "soft", teddy lacking "smooth") are mildly contestable but defensible.
subject: Science
name: Materials Sorter

rationale: A genuinely strong A-Level mechanisms tool: four mechanisms stepped with canvas curly-arrow diagrams, predict-before-reveal MCQs that gate (blur) the explanation, misconception-aware feedback, scoring, speech narration and solid accessibility (aria-live, keyboard nav, screen-reader canvas descriptions). Chemistry is accurate and well pitched to KS5; it loses marks only because the promised curly-arrow "animations" are actually static per-step redraws and a couple of details are loose (Markovnikov is illustrated on symmetrical ethene, and the SN2 transition state is described as "five partial bonds/pentacoordinate").
subject: Science
name: Organic Reaction Mechanisms

rationale: A polished, accurate KS1 History app: significant individuals (including local figures Anning and Brunel) presented as accessible picture cards with read-aloud support for emerging readers, an explored-progress tracker, and a gated "Who am I?" retrieval-practice quiz with shuffled options and immediate feedback. Maps cleanly onto the National Curriculum and is technically robust, accessible and responsive; it stops short of an A* only because cognitive demand is mainly recall/matching with no chronology, comparison or misconception work.
subject: History
name: Famous People Cards

rationale: A polished EYFS RE app pairing an explore-the-cards discovery phase with a shuffled, retry-on-wrong matching game that demands recall rather than reveal-and-collect; content is accurate, respectful and well-pitched, with strong accessibility (keyboard nav, ARIA, en-GB speech synthesis). Cognitive demand stays light (recognition matching, no comparison or reasoning across festivals), which is appropriate for EYFS but caps the pedagogy ceiling.
subject: Religious Education
name: Celebrations Around the World

rationale: Genuinely well-designed KS1 app: it isolates the real cognitive demand of two-step problems by forcing pupils to choose the operation, identify the correct numbers, and compute at each step, chaining step 1's result into step 2, with operation-specific feedback that targets the core misconception. Interactivity is strong (staged unlocking, meaningful per-error feedback, concrete counter animations), alignment to Year 2 problem-solving is sound, and the build is clean and responsive with only minor accessibility gaps.
subject: Maths
name: Two-Step Word Problems

rationale: A polished, genuinely interactive EYFS oral-language tool: tappable scene hotspots reveal full-sentence stems with a working tense toggle, turn-taking partner loop, vocabulary pops and live contextual feedback, all accessibly built (keyboard-focusable SVG hotspots, aria-live regions). It maps cleanly onto the EYFS Communication and Language ELG (full sentences, tenses, ambitious vocabulary) at the right Reception pitch.
subject: English
name: Talk Partner Story Prompts

rationale: A genuinely strong EYFS tool: letters grouped by the recognised handwriting formation families with an animated stroke replay, live pointer-tracing scored separately for start point, on-line accuracy and completion with kind, specific next-step feedback, plus a CVC segment-and-spell mode with speech and distractor tiles. Robust, accessible and offline-capable; minor caveats are stylised (not type-accurate) letter paths and a scorer that does not verify stroke direction.
subject: English
name: Magic Letter Tracer

rationale: A focused, well-built KS2 grammar app that teaches not just the fronted-adverbial comma rule but the emphasis/flow effect of moving the adverbial, with genuine drag-and-drop, animated comma feedback, a side-by-side emphasis read-out, keyboard access and ARIA live regions. Only minor weaknesses: a single adverbial per round with no distractors, and a slightly overgeneralised "end adverbials don't need a comma" claim.
subject: English
name: Adverbial Mover

rationale: A genuinely strong KS4 Spoken Language Endorsement planner: it scaffolds the real difficulty (formal Standard English register) with a misconception-aware checker that gives reasons, distinguishes hard errors from soft register tips, and pairs it with live timing, readiness scoring and printable cue cards for authentic, immediate feedback. Alignment is accurate and well-pitched (minor over-eager rules like flagging every "a lot of"/"don't", correctly softened), and the build is robust and accessible bar a harmless dead rule and generic modal boilerplate.
subject: English
name: Presentation Planner

rationale: A genuinely strong KS1 array tool: live row/column manipulation drives repeated addition, both multiplication facts, both division facts and a skip-count track, with a real rotate-to-show-commutativity animation and a quiz that explains both correct and incorrect answers. Accurate, well pitched and accessible (aria-live, spinbutton roles, keyboard control, focus rings), with only minor polish gaps.
subject: Maths
name: Array Builder

rationale: A broad, misconception-aware tool that forces genuine digit-by-digit prediction across all four formal written methods plus BIDMAS and factors/multiples/primes, with carry/exchange modelling, tailored wrong-answer feedback, hints and reveal. Robust, accessible (ARIA tablist, live regions, focus styles) and responsive; minor gaps (factor candidates never include 1 or the target; struck value in subtraction is not shown numerically) keep it just short of perfect on alignment and technical.
subject: Maths
name: Column Method Animator

rationale: A polished, accurate KS2 "Animals including humans" app with an animated organ-by-organ food journey, a quiz-unlock gate, per-option teaching feedback that reveals the correct answer, and a working BPM tap-pad linking digestion to circulation; strong accessibility (ARIA, focus states, live regions, 44px targets) and clean design. Held off top marks on pedagogy/interactivity because the "order challenge" is plain multiple-choice rather than true sequencing and the heart section is a light thematic link rather than deep circulatory teaching.
subject: Science
name: Journey Through Digestion

rationale: A polished, genuinely misconception-aware KS2 states-of-matter app that cleanly separates state descriptions from named transition messages (melting/freezing/evaporation/condensation) and applies them in an accessible water-cycle builder, with rich keyboard, pointer and touch interaction. Tightly aligned to Year 4 with correct 0/100°C points; loses marks only on the particle render not making the solid noticeably more ordered/packed than the liquid.
subject: Science
name: Heat It, Cool It

rationale: A genuinely strong KS4 chemistry tool with a coherent three-step arc (choose pair, manipulate electrons via drag or keyboard with branched feedback, then reason structure-to-properties), well aligned to the AQA/Edexcel/OCR bonding topic and unusually accessible. Held off top marks because the header promises "metallic bonds" that no pair actually delivers and the generic demo-data modal copy about student records/grades/attendance is irrelevant boilerplate for a chemistry app.
subject: Science
name: Bonding Builder

rationale: A polished, historically accurate KS1 sequence that builds a genuine causal chain (timber and proximity, wind and drought, firebreaks, rebuilding in brick) with misconception-aware distractors, gated quiz progression, an animated SVG fire and a strong then/now significance finale; it maps precisely onto the National Curriculum's "events beyond living memory" strand and is well pitched for Year 1/2. Accessibility (aria-live, SVG title/desc, keyboard nav, focus-visible) is above average for these apps.
subject: History
name: The Great Fire of London: 5 Days

rationale: A genuinely strong dual-tool: a correctly projected, drag-rotatable globe with toggleable Equator/Tropics/Prime Meridian (with proper back-of-globe culling) paired with a draggable clock that accurately models 15 degrees of longitude per hour, plus a scored challenge with direction-aware feedback. Maps precisely onto the KS2 geography curriculum's named lines and time zones, with an honest footer caveat about the simplified model; only modest deductions for boilerplate demo-modal text irrelevant to a globe app and the absence of an explicit hemisphere/latitude learning check.
subject: Geography
name: Lines on the Globe

rationale: A genuinely strong KS3 locational-knowledge tool: four toggleable thematic overlays plus inspectable markers and two well-designed, misconception-aware challenge types (cities vs climate bands, Amazon vs Sahara, reef location) that teach the underlying physical processes, with immediate feedback, scoring and solid accessibility. Held just short of perfect by the deliberately crude stylised map, where some marker positions only loosely fit the continent blocks, and a boilerplate demo-data modal referencing "student records/grades" that does not match a geography map.
subject: Geography
name: World in Layers

rationale: A genuinely strong KS3 resource-geography tool: pupils balance six energy sources against demand across four geographically distinct fictional countries, with live cost/emissions/security gauges and a misconception-aware feedback engine that ties advice to each country's wind, sun and rivers. Clean, accessible, responsive build with no real bugs; only let down slightly by the supply bar reading nameplate percentages while the model's own intermittency multiplier (eff) is computed but never applied to the headline balance.
subject: Geography
name: Energy Mix Balancer

rationale: A genuinely strong KS3 RE app: pupils layer four identity dimensions and the analysis engine detects authentic lived-religion patterns (believing without belonging, etc.), generates tailored challenge questions and structure-aware reflection feedback, directly targeting the "religion equals a single label" misconception with accurate sociology and solid accessibility. Minor blemishes are the somewhat arbitrary "complexity percent" metric and a gameable keyword-counting reflection checker.
subject: Religious Education
name: Identity Mosaic

rationale: A rigorous, misconception-aware A-level RE tool that stress-tests three theodicies claim-by-claim against natural and moral evil cases, with accurate scholarship (Plantinga's defence-vs-theodicy distinction, Hick's epistemic distance/universalism, process non-omnipotence) and an exam-paragraph scaffold plus Rowe/evidential-problem discussion prompts. Interactivity is real (filter, case x theodicy selection, per-claim survive/strain/break verdicts and meter) though it stays a curated lookup matrix rather than letting pupils argue or weigh claims themselves.
subject: Religious Education
name: Theodicy Tester

rationale: A genuinely strong KS3 MFL translation tool: chunked translation tagged by grammar structure, an aggregating confidence heat-map that surfaces reteach priorities, and structure-aware feedback that prompts metacognition. Technically excellent with custom pointer drag plus full keyboard parity, ARIA labelling, live regions and a responsive layout; the only real ceiling is that fixed-bank matching allows some solving by elimination rather than recall.
subject: Modern Foreign Languages
name: Translate the Text

rationale: A genuinely strong KS4 MFL speaking trainer that rehearses all three GCSE tasks (photo card, role-play unpredictable element, general conversation) across French, Spanish and German with accurate, well-pitched target-language content, scaffolded chunked model answers, a colour-shifting pressure timer and a misconception-aware self-check engine that names what is missing. Interactivity is real but capped because assessment is self-reported (inherent to a browser speaking tool), and there is a cosmetic init quirk where the ring shows 12s while the active duration is 45s.
subject: Modern Foreign Languages
name: Photo Card Coach

rationale: A genuinely strong KS2 Drama peer-evaluation tool whose standout feature is misconception-awareness: two trap cards (a non-existent laugh, a redundant posture "wish") are rejected with targeted feedback that teaches pupils to evaluate against real evidence and specificity rather than generic praise. Polished, accessible (full keyboard path, ARIA live regions), with robust pointer drag-and-drop, capacity-limited zones, and a generated spoken-feedback summary; only minor limits are the single fixed scene and a generic demo-data modal.
subject: Drama
name: Two Stars and a Wish

rationale: A genuinely strong KS3 Drama app: four sliders drive a live SVG performer and a weighted scoring engine that names the effect of each skill on the audience, with four well-characterised briefs, signature-skill weighting, directional tips and clear teacher notes that build performance vocabulary. Robust, offline, accessible (aria-valuetext, live regions, focus states) and cleanly designed; the SVG posture transforms are the most expressive feedback channel while pitch/projection are conveyed more abstractly.
subject: Drama
name: Voice and Body Lab (KS3 Drama)

rationale: A genuinely strong EYFS app: full pointer and keyboard drag-and-drop with diagnostic feedback (correct slots greened, first wrong flagged amber with a positional hint referencing the prior step), star progress, localStorage persistence, and a thoughtful pastoral "Safe Grown-Ups" panel, all robustly built and accessible. Pedagogy is capped only by the modest cognitive demand of a single fixed six-step sequence, which is nonetheless age-appropriate for the EYFS belonging/routine strands it targets.
subject: Behaviour & Pastoral
name: Our Day Picture Timeline

rationale: A genuinely strong KS1 RSE app: the gallery plus sort cleanly teaches "families look different but all share love, care, listening and safety", mapping precisely onto the statutory Relationships Education theme, with diverse, sensitively-worded family models and misconception-aware, direction-specific feedback. Robust pointer-drag, a full keyboard path, zone-click fallback, ARIA and live regions lift the build well above static.
subject: Behaviour & Pastoral
name: All Our Families

rationale: Excellent, misconception-aware KS1 health app: time-of-day coaching flags night-time active play, daytime sleep and wrong tooth-brushing times, with junk/screen distractor cards, and offers genuine drag, tap and full-keyboard interaction with immediate specific feedback. Accurate, well-pitched PSHE/Health Education content with robust SVG wheel logic and strong accessibility.
subject: Behaviour & Pastoral
name: Build a Healthy Day (KS1)

rationale: Excellent three-stage pastoral design: the iceberg metaphor is exploited well, feedback is misconception-aware (it reminds pupils that even correctly-placed visible traits reveal nothing about thoughts/feelings), and the self-reflection plus stereotype-challenge stages give genuine cognitive demand on identity, diversity and respect for difference. Robust drag-and-drop with a real keyboard fallback, ARIA live regions and demo-data safeguard; the only soft spots are minor accessibility/UX gaps (SVG-placed tags rely on undocumented single-key controls, and self-reflection "Not me" buttons still flag chosen-correct styling) and that PSHE/RSE sits outside the statutory National Curriculum proper.
subject: Behaviour & Pastoral
name: Identity Iceberg

rationale: A genuinely interactive KS3 budgeting simulator with real manipulation (toggles, price tiers, randomised surprise events) and specific, misconception-aware live feedback, notably the bike-tyre event that exposes how the "cheaper" choice can cost more. Pitch and figures are accurate for KS3 money/economic wellbeing (PSHE/Citizenship rather than a strict NC subject), and the code is robust and accessible bar a couple of minor gaps.
subject: Behaviour & Pastoral
name: First Budget Simulator

rationale: A genuinely excellent KS5 financial-capability simulator: 12 months of money decisions where earlier choices (insurance, emergency fund) causally compound into later surprise bills, with consistently misconception-aware feedback (0% is not free money, minimum payments keep you in debt, pension opt-out forfeits the employer match) and an accurate, age-appropriate UK pitch. Robust state-machine logic, dynamic state-dependent branching, immediate tiered feedback, running ledger, accessibility (aria-live, keyboard, focus styles) and a graded year-end review make it a strong, lesson-worthy tool.
subject: Behaviour & Pastoral
name: Adulting Finance Simulator

rationale: A well-built EYFS pattern-continuation game with genuine pointer-and-keyboard drag-and-drop, AB/ABB levels, misconception-aware feedback that names the repeating unit, and colour-blind-friendly shape cues; strong accessibility and clean responsive design. Pedagogy is capped slightly because each train only ever asks for one "next" carriage rather than extending a sequence or identifying the unit boundary.
subject: Art and Design
name: Pattern Train

rationale: A polished, genuinely interactive KS1 colour-theory app: pupils drag (or keyboard-select) primaries to discover secondaries with immediate visual splats, written feedback and a progressively-filling SVG colour wheel, accurately pitched to the KS1 Art curriculum. It is misconception-aware (same-colour-twice "stays red") and offers a dual drag/keyboard input path with aria-live status, though the "mix all three gives muddy brown" teaching prompt is unreachable in-app (only two paints mix at once) and screen-reader users get colour discovery only secondhand via the hidden status line.
subject: Art and Design
name: Colour Mixer Easel

rationale: Models the genuine KS2 artistic process (gather ideas into a mood board, select the strongest, develop through four stages, then self-evaluate) with a misconception-aware critique engine that gives specific contextual feedback and an iterative improve-and-retry loop. Robust custom drag-and-drop with full keyboard parity, ARIA roles and live regions; maps cleanly onto the KS2 sketchbook/develop-and-review programme of study.
subject: Art and Design
name: Idea Web Studio

rationale: A genuinely interactive composition trainer: pupils drag a focal point onto rule-of-thirds and golden-ratio grids and get an immediate, position-specific balance score with misconception-aware coaching (dead-centre, edge-crowding, lead room) plus formal-element vocabulary. Robust, accessible SVG implementation with keyboard, ARIA slider and live regions; only minor weaknesses are the slightly simplified golden-ratio model and the largely scene-fixed (rather than composition-driven) formal-element chips.
subject: Art and Design
name: Composition Grid

rationale: A pedagogically sharp KS3 app that makes pupils test individual visual cues (with deliberate cross-movement distractors and specific corrective feedback) before naming the movement and reading accurate period/context cards, mapping cleanly onto the NC "history and contexts of art" and analysis strands. Technically robust and accessible (keyboard, ARIA, aria-live, original SVGs); held just below full marks by the necessarily schematic artworks, the thin upload self-check mode, and only four works limiting replay.
subject: Art and Design
name: Style Decoder

rationale: A genuinely strong critique tool: drag-and-keyboard tagging of strengths vs next steps against accurately worded GCSE AOs, with specific per-card coaching and well-chosen misclassifiable distractors, then a transfer task that turns the same evaluative language onto pupils' own work. Self-contained SVG artworks, real accessibility (ARIA, aria-live, keyboard path), and the required demo-data safeguards are all present.
subject: Art and Design
name: Gallery Critique

rationale: An unusually thoughtful KS5 metacognitive tool: students build a Question/Experiment/Decision node graph and receive feedback derived from real graph topology (answered questions, reflective decisions, strands, orphans) plus AO1-AO4 meters, which genuinely models sustained personal investigation rather than a tidy linear outcome. Interactivity is real (pointer drag, keyboard move/link/delete, accessible roles, a strong worked example) and feedback is specific and misconception-aware; minor deductions because AO labels are a simplified composite of generic AQA/Edexcel wording rather than a single named spec, and the percentage AO scoring is a heuristic that could mislead students into chasing card counts.
subject: Art and Design
name: Enquiry Tracker

rationale: A well-crafted EYFS music app that teaches steady beat and tempo through three contrasting modes, a synced animated character, a Web Audio metronome and a drum-tap game that genuinely scores timing error against the real beat with a meter, stars and tiered feedback. Strong accessibility (keyboard, ARIA live, focus-visible, reduced-motion), robust audio fallbacks, accurate pulse-vs-tempo teacher note and correct EYFS Expressive Arts and Design framing.
subject: Music
name: March, Tiptoe, Stomp

rationale: A polished, genuinely interactive KS1 composing tool with real Web Audio synthesis, multi-modal input (drag, tap-preview, keyboard) and a clever stir mechanic that distinguishes sequence from layering, mapping accurately onto the KS1 Music NC's "experiment with, create, select and combine sounds" alongside age-appropriate texture vocabulary. It falls just short of top marks on pedagogy and technical because the stir-then-play distinction is subtle for the youngest pupils and the texture meter tracks unique sound types only, so adding duplicate layers leaves the bar unchanged.
subject: Music
name: Sound Soup

rationale: A polished KS2 listening-and-appraising tool: live Web Audio synthesis gives each era a distinct, era-appropriate sound, the explore-then-test structure with diagnostic, misconception-aware quiz feedback genuinely builds aural discrimination, and the engineering (playToken guarding, ARIA live regions, aria-pressed, responsive timeline, visibility pause) is robust. Held off full marks because the clips are honestly stylised synth impressions rather than real timbre and the era dates overlap (Jazz Age 1900 to 1950 vs Modern 1900 to 1975), slightly muddying historical precision.
subject: Music
name: Music Through Time

rationale: A genuinely excellent KS3 keyboard performance trainer: playable Web Audio keyboard, scrolling piano-roll guide with a timing-judged play line, chord-hold detection rewarding hands-together coordination, and misconception-aware feedback that distinguishes wrong pitch from mistiming and offers directional hints. Music theory and chord progressions are accurate and well pitched, with progressive scaffolds; loses a point on alignment because the "notation" is a piano roll rather than real stave reading, and on technical for a minor help-text/key-map mismatch.
subject: Music
name: Keyboard Skills Studio

rationale: A genuinely impressive Web Audio multitrack sequencer with real synthesis, drag-paint editing, a chord progression lab with Roman-numeral analysis, style presets and a misconception-aware live feedback engine (thin texture, missing kick, backbeat, melodic contour, harmonic resolution) that maps cleanly onto KS3 composing and the elements of music. Held off full marks on alignment because the G major scale is deliberately flattened to naturals (the F should be F#, so G-major melodies play a wrong note), and on technical because the setTimeout scheduler can jitter against the audio clock.
subject: Music
name: DAW Lite Composer

rationale: A well-conceived GCSE listening trainer with a genuine pedagogical arc (scaffolded score-following, then recall, then unscaffolded transfer that mirrors the real exam), live Web Audio synthesis, a scrubable highlighting playhead, and notably misconception-aware distractor feedback (cadence types, sequence vs ostinato, pedal vs drone). Alignment and technical sit at 4 because the synthesised triangle/sine timbres and the abstract two-lane block "score" are a thin proxy for real instrumental timbre and staff notation, and the pause-by-recreating-AudioContext pattern is slightly heavy though functional.
subject: Music
name: Set Work Dissector

rationale: A broad, genuinely interactive KS4 Music drill suite (keys/scales, intervals, chords, notation, rhythm builder) where every answer is confirmed aurally via Web Audio, with levelled difficulty, misconception-aware feedback and a manipulable rhythm builder that checks the bar maths and plays it back; pitched well for GCSE. Marks held back only by sharps-only enharmonic naming that can mislabel flat-key/notation contexts and by reliance on Unicode SMuFL clef and note glyphs that render unreliably across systems.
subject: Music
name: GCSE Theory Bootcamp

rationale: A genuinely sophisticated KS5 tool: a working SVG score grid with keyboard/pointer note entry, Web Audio playback, and a live, misconception-aware rule-checker covering parallels, hidden fifths/octaves, spacing, crossing, range, leaps and leading-note doubling, all mapping cleanly onto A-level pastiche composition conventions. Strong accessibility and editorial design; only minor rule simplifications (soprano-only leading-note resolution, adjacent-only crossing) keep alignment and technical just short of a 5.
subject: Music
name: Pastiche Composer

rationale: A genuinely excellent KS5 Music tool combining live WebAudio synthesis, a pitch-accurate SVG score with synced playhead, multi-strand annotation with specific per-bar feedback, adaptive higher-order MCQs, and exam-style aural dictation with contour and direction feedback. Theory is largely accurate and the cognitive demand suits A-level set-work analysis; it loses a point on alignment for using fictional rather than prescribed set works and a slightly debatable modal harmony label.
subject: Music
name: Analytical Score Annotator (KS5 Music)

rationale: A polished, accessible KS1 algorithms app that nails the "create and debug simple programs / predict behaviour" strand with a genuine reorder-and-debug task, targeted misconception-aware feedback, and excellent dual input (drag plus keyboard with live announcements). Slightly marred only by enforcing a single "correct" sequence when several get-dressed orderings are physically valid.
subject: Computing
name: Algorithm Detective

rationale: A polished, misconception-aware KS1 online-safety sorting game that maps precisely onto the NC Computing requirement to keep personal information private and identify where to get help, with per-card right/wrong rationales, subtle non-obvious cases (the park vs a photo showing a house number) and genuinely robust pointer-drag, button and keyboard interaction with ARIA live feedback and focus management. Held back from a higher pedagogy/interactivity mark only by the inherently binary, low-ceiling sort task.
subject: Computing
name: Keep It Private

rationale: An excellent KS3 online-safety tool whose standout strength is teaching inference (a stranger deducing location, age and identity from data never explicitly typed) rather than a simplistic "don't overshare" message, with a nuanced model that handles friends-only exposure, single-vs-multiple identifiers, and screenshot persistence. Live meter, inference cards, verdicts and the "Make Jay safe" contrast give genuine manipulation with immediate, meaningful feedback, accurately pitched to the England KS3 Computing safe-and-responsible-use strand.
subject: Computing
name: Footprint Tracker

rationale: A genuinely well-designed model of encapsulation and de-encapsulation across the four-layer TCP/IP stack, with bidirectional flow, misconception-aware wrong-layer feedback, accurate GCSE-level detail (ports, IP vs MAC per hop, packet switching) and multiple input modes including keyboard. Held back from full marks only by a small drag-affordance gap (the .hovering hover state is toggled in JS but never styled in CSS, and the packet stays in the tray rather than visibly moving under each layer).
subject: Computing
name: Protocol Stack Builder

rationale: A genuinely strong EYFS structures app: free drag-and-stack building with a snap, plus a three-rule stability model (floating, off-centre/top-heavy, base-width-to-height ratio) that returns distinct, child-friendly, misconception-aware explanations on each wobble test. Manipulation is real, feedback is immediate and meaningful, and it is keyboard-operable with sensible ARIA, though the boilerplate "student records/grades/attendance" demo modal is incongruous for a block app and the live measurements are pitched slightly above EYFS.
subject: Design and Technology
name: Build It Up

rationale: Genuinely strong EYFS DT planner: it teaches the design cycle (purpose, user, materials-for-a-job, evaluate against plan) with material-specific reasoning feedback that targets real misconceptions (paper sinks, sponge soaks, box will not fold), and a self-evaluation that normalises iteration. Alignment to EYFS "Expressive Arts/UTW: making for a purpose" and the design-make-evaluate pitch is accurate and age-appropriate.
subject: Design and Technology
name: What Shall We Make?

rationale: A genuinely strong KS1 D&T app that maps cleanly onto designing purposeful, functional, appealing products for a chosen user: three clear steps (choose user, drag-and-place features, communicate with a "because" sentence), misconception-aware distractors (spikes/brick) with targeted per-user feedback, real pointer drag plus a working keyboard path, ARIA live regions and focus styles. Marked down only because the product template is a single generic box for every user and some feature-to-user fits are arguable/duplicated, slightly weakening the design-criteria authenticity.
subject: Design and Technology
name: Design for a User

rationale: A tightly focused KS1 evaluate-strand tool that maps precisely onto the NC requirement to explore existing products and evaluate against design criteria, with the key strength of carrying the same criteria onto pupils' own make and generating a real comparison; clean, accessible, robust code with verdict-aware per-criterion feedback. Held off a perfect score because criteria are fixed (no pupil-agreed criteria step) and products are emoji rather than real images, slightly limiting cognitive demand and authenticity.
subject: Design and Technology
name: Good Design Detective

rationale: A genuinely interactive KS1 D&T app: three independent choices redraw the SVG bridge live, an animated load test gives three-band outcomes with emoji and targeted improvement tips, modelling a real predict-test-improve cycle with strong accessibility and clean, age-appropriate design. Maps well onto the KS1 structures programme of study, lightly held back only by a slightly arbitrary mapping of strong/stiff/stable vocabulary to layering/folding/bracing and a loose engineering framing.
subject: Design and Technology
name: Strong, Stiff, Stable

rationale: A well-built KS4 D&T lab that lets pupils pair forces (tension, compression, shear, torsion) with reinforcement strategies and run live tests with per-force failure animations, a predicted-resistance meter, and best-match/warn/fail verdicts with sound reasoning, mapping cleanly onto GCSE specialist technical principles. Alignment and technical fall just short of 5 because the resistance model is a deliberately simplified load-times-ten heuristic and the failure overlays (shear slip, buckle) are visually crude, while the trust-injected demo-data modal references student records/grades that this app never contains.
subject: Design and Technology
name: Stress and Reinforcement Lab

rationale: A focused, misconception-aware KS4 D&T simulator that genuinely models the cost/tooling/time trade-offs of one-off, batch and mass production with live feedback, plus a sensibly weighted 6 Rs and lifecycle decision that correctly frames recycling as the weakest R. Accurate to GCSE D&T, accessible and clean; only minor quibbles (scale changes don't auto-suggest a fitting quantity despite a code comment implying they should).
subject: Design and Technology
name: Production Scale Simulator

rationale: An authentic A-level D&T decision-making tool: the compare-weight-matrix-justify scaffold mirrors real systems-engineering practice, with live radar overlays, re-ranking weight sliders, and a free-text justification checker that enforces a full reasoning chain and is genuinely misconception-aware (e.g. the actuator brief teaches that a weighted score can hide a functional must-have). Clean, accessible, robust code with only minor gaps (no roving-tabindex keyboard nav on the tab/radio groups; the 0-10 property scores and +1.2 smart-material bonus are somewhat arbitrary), which is why alignment and technical sit at 4 rather than 5.
subject: Design and Technology
name: Advanced Material Matrix

rationale: A genuinely strong KS5 DT tool that models cradle-to-grave LCA across six stages with live carbon, circular, social and legal feedback, and its verdict logic teaches the key misconception that hitting a carbon target while ignoring social and legal duties is not responsible design. Robust, accessible build; the circular-percentage and legal-compliance mappings are somewhat arbitrary simplifications rather than defensible LCA, which keeps alignment and technical just short of full marks.
subject: Design and Technology
name: Lifecycle Analyst

rationale: A genuinely strong KS5 DT tool that scaffolds the full NEA iterative design journey with varied, meaningful interactivity (text analysis, accessible drag-sort, iteration-link detection, live SVG test rig, viability matrix) and immediate, misconception-aware feedback. Alignment and technical robustness are good but not flawless: AO labels are board-generic, the test physics is illustrative, and there is no persistence so all work is lost on refresh.
subject: Design and Technology
name: NEA Design Journey

rationale: A polished, teacher-led EYFS PE circuit builder with genuine manipulation (tap/drag/keyboard reorder, timing dials, quick packs) and a robust ready/move/rest/done run engine with animated SVG figures, countdowns and live feedback. It is well pitched to EYFS gross-motor development and actively coaches the teacher toward a balanced, age-appropriate circuit; child-level cognitive demand is necessarily low by design.
subject: Physical Education
name: Movement Mat

rationale: A genuinely strong KS2 invasion-games tactics board: pupils drag or keyboard-nudge attackers into space, then watch an animated pass evaluated by real geometry (forward gain, nearest-defender space, pass-lane interception) with differentiated, misconception-aware coaching feedback. Robust, responsive and accessible (ARIA, keyboard nav, aria-live, space meter), with only minor rough edges (fiddly keyboard token model, one dead ternary) keeping alignment and technical just below a 5.
subject: Physical Education
name: Tactics Board Live

rationale: A genuinely strong KS2 striking-and-fielding tactics tool: pupils read the batter's hit, drag/keyboard-position seven fielders, and a 100-ball Monte Carlo simulation gives an honest percentage plus misconception-aware coaching on side-loading, depth and bunching. Cleanly built, accessible (ARIA, live regions, keyboard control, focus-visible) and well pitched to the NC tactics strand for invasion/striking games.
subject: Physical Education
name: Field the Ball

rationale: A polished, genuinely interactive coaching tool that breaks three accurate KS3 skills into phases with observation cues, tap-to-rate marking, keyboard support and a computed report distilling marks into two precise refinement targets, mapping cleanly onto the NC KS3 PE "analyse and improve performance" strand. Crude stick-figure SVGs and minor tri-state aria-pressed imperfections are the only weak spots.
subject: Physical Education
name: Technique Analyser

rationale: A genuinely strong OAA decision-making simulator: pupils build a constrained route and the app models real terrain-by-weather-by-kit consequences with morale, time, risk and efficiency feedback plus misconception-aware tips. Accessible (keyboard checkpoints, ARIA live regions) and accurately tied to the KS3 PE OAA programme of study, with only minor blemishes (mislabelled "drag" kit chips, opaque efficiency formula).
subject: Physical Education
name: Expedition Planner

rationale: Three genuinely interactive, misconception-aware KS3 PE activities (best-fit vs near-fit matcher, live-coverage circuit builder, HR zone modeller) with immediate graded feedback and strong keyboard/touch accessibility. Held off full marks on alignment/technical because the heart-rate zone bar's visual segment widths and labels do not line up with the actual %MHR boundaries used in code, making the marker position decorative rather than accurate.
subject: Physical Education
name: Fitness Components Lab

rationale: A genuinely interactive KS3 PE assessment tool: dual slider/draggable-radar input with full keyboard support, live recomputation of the lowest-scoring development priority, a tailored coaching action, strengths and average, all mapped cleanly onto the NC "analyse and evaluate to improve" skill across three sport-appropriate scenarios. Held off a perfect score because the analysis is a simple lowest-score rule with no misconception-awareness (e.g. uniform over-rating).
subject: Physical Education
name: Performance Profiler

rationale: A polished, accurate KS4 GCSE PE tool that genuinely teaches antagonistic pairs and double circulation through a clickable muscle model, a stepped cardio walkthrough, and a misconception-aware quiz, with strong accessibility and clean, bug-free code. It loses a point on interactivity because the muscle "animation" is a colour/pulse state change rather than actual joint movement, and the squat-as-hamstring-agonist framing slightly overstates the hamstring role versus eccentric quad control.
subject: Physical Education
name: Body in Motion (KS4 PE)

rationale: A genuinely strong KS4 GCSE PE tool: pupils build a six-week periodised plan applying SPORT and FITT, and a live coaching engine surfaces misconceptions (load spikes, missing taper, off-target specificity, reversibility dips) in the moment, backed by an integrated fitness-test bench linking tests to components. Accurate, accessible and well-engineered, with only minor heuristic simplifications and dead code holding it back from full marks.
subject: Physical Education
name: Training Programme Designer

rationale: A polished, accessible two-stage KS1 money app: a drag-or-keyboard needs/wants sorter with per-item reasons, plus a 20p shop with affordability guards, live change calculation and a needs-vs-wants till summary. Genuine manipulation, meaningful immediate feedback, robust code with no obvious bugs, and a sensible PSHE/Citizenship economic-wellbeing pitch for the age.
subject: Citizenship
name: Needs or Wants?

rationale: A genuinely strong KS4 Citizenship case-tree on consumer, employment and police-encounter rights, with legally accurate, misconception-aware feedback citing real statutes (CRA 2015, NMW Act 1998, ERA 1996, PACE), scoring and remedy summaries that map precisely onto the "legal system, justice and rights of citizens" strand. Solid accessibility and clean design; held back only by short, largely convergent trees and a mismatched boilerplate demo-data modal.
subject: Citizenship
name: Know Your Rights

rationale: A genuinely strong KS5 Citizenship resource: students set a balance, select real legal arguments, get a live prediction meter, then compare against accurate ECHR/HRA rulings with per-argument feedback, the legal principle, and the public-interest-vs-curiosity misconception explicitly targeted. Accurate, accessible (aria-live, aria-pressed, focus-visible), responsive and cleanly built; only minor knocks are the deliberately simple linear prediction model and the boilerplate demo-data modal copy referencing "grades/attendance" irrelevant to this app.
subject: Citizenship
name: Rights on Trial

rationale: A polished, research-grounded CPD planner that operationalises faded worked examples and think-aloud modelling with genuine manipulation and rubric-based live feedback on both the fade gradient and narration quality. Fit for its professional purpose; only constraint is that narration scoring is shallow keyword matching (gameable) and the example bank is fixed with no custom-content option.
subject: CPD & Staff Tools
name: Worked Example Planner

rationale: A genuinely useful, research-grounded oracy CPD planner: six well-sequenced steps with contextual, non-trivial feedback (question openness, talk-move variety, challenge-role balance) and a synthesised plan output. Interactivity is real, with keyboard-accessible drag-and-drop, live validation and a progression slider; the only blemishes are an ill-fitting boilerplate demo-data modal and a heuristic question regex that can misfire.
subject: CPD & Staff Tools
name: Oracy and Talk Protocol Planner

rationale: A genuinely well-engineered CPD reflection tool: impact-weighted sliders drive a live score, conditional targeted feedback, and trust-policy reduction chips that re-model the week, with strong accessibility and no obvious bugs. The score's ratio basis (impact share, not absolute load) is a minor conceptual limitation, mitigated but not fully resolved by the separate sustainable-week budget line.
subject: CPD & Staff Tools
name: Workload Self-Audit Tool

rationale: A polished, genuinely interactive data-literacy tool for staff/leaders: draggable absence-threshold line, keyboard-navigable dots, a residual-aware pupil inspector, live regression/correlation stats, and a misconception-targeting MCQ (no relationship, reverse causation, no within-band spread) including an explicit correlation-is-not-causation prompt. Accurate to the England persistent-absence (<90%) and 96% targets with clean SVG rendering and strong accessibility; the only soft spots are the loose "X of a grade per subject" gloss on the P8 gap and a slightly forced narrative built around just two severe-absence outliers driving the correlation.
subject: Data & Analytics
name: Attendance-Attainment Scatter

rationale: A genuinely strong QLA tool: a colour-banded marks/percentage heatmap with question, pupil and class-average inspectors, a live grade-4 boundary slider that recomputes the distribution and borderline list, and an auto-generated, marks-weighted reteach priority plan (not pre-written). Technically robust with full keyboard grid navigation, ARIA live regions, sticky headers and a sensible caution that boundary modelling is illustrative; the only soft spots are the cautious 4s for professional pitch (the simplistic priority weighting and the assumption that low marks always equal a topic gap rather than exam technique).
subject: Data & Analytics
name: Question-Level Analysis Grid

rationale: A focused, misconception-aware CPD tool that makes the abstract idea of statistical significance tangible: the live SVG interval widens as the cohort shrinks, the three-way verdict logic is accurate, and the quiz reinforces exactly the right governor-level judgement. Marked down slightly on alignment and interactivity because the confidence-interval model is a simplified fixed-k approximation that will not match published DfE figures, and the Progress 8 input is limited to five preset buttons rather than a continuous control.
subject: Data & Analytics
name: Confidence Interval Visualiser

rationale: A genuinely useful governance reporting tool: live RAG rating with correct higher/lower-is-better inversion, real-time jargon flagging with plain-English swaps, a board-readiness checklist and a rebuilding one-page report, with sensible comparators and solid accessibility. Fit for its professional purpose and accurate; only a missing print-specific stylesheet and slightly loose amber-threshold labelling for inverted measures hold it back from full marks.
subject: Data & Analytics
name: Governor Data Summary Builder

rationale: A well-built KS1 synthetic-phonics blender with sound-out and quiz modes, digraphs, CVC-to-CCVC progression, multi-sensory audio feedback and robust TTS fallback; pedagogy is held just below top because the TTS phoneme spellings ("buh", "cuh") introduce the schwa that synthetic phonics explicitly warns against.
subject: English
name: Phonics Blender

rationale: Excellent EYFS chronology task: ordinal slot labels (First, Next, Then) make sequencing something the child reasons out rather than reads off ages, formative feedback marks correct slots green and prompts swapping, and TTS read-aloud throughout genuinely supports pre-readers. Tap-to-select-then-place interaction is accessible (keyboard roles, aria-labels) and developmentally apt, though it stays a single fixed-order task with no drag, levels or variation.
subject: History
name: Family Timeline

rationale: A polished, misconception-aware KS2 online-safety game with genuine three-way classification, multimodal input (drag/button/keyboard), immediate specific feedback, streak scoring and a screen-time-balance extension; robust code with escaped injection, aria-live, focus management and a working restart. Held just short of full marks by only 8 fixed-order posts (no shuffle, thin replay value) and a slightly counter-intuitive swipe mapping.
subject: Behaviour & Pastoral
name: Real or Fake Feed

rationale: A genuinely strong three-phase design (classify scenes, construct a class rule, then stress-test against edge cases) that escalates cognitive demand from recognition to application, with feedback that is explicitly misconception-aware ("it was only a joke", kind-meant stereotypes) and maps cleanly onto the KS2 RSHE statutory themes of respectful relationships, courtesy, online behaviour and challenging stereotypes. Loses a point on interactivity and technical because the manipulation is essentially structured multiple-choice (verdict buttons, chips, two-option scales) rather than open construction, and the edge-case "correct" gating treats nuanced grey-area items as having one right answer despite the lead text claiming there isn't always one.
subject: Behaviour & Pastoral
name: Respect Detectives

rationale: A genuinely strong KS3 media-literacy tool: pupils make granular trustworthy/misleading/tactic judgements on fictional posts and get differentiated, misconception-aware feedback that names specific persuasion tactics, with one deliberately trustworthy post so the lesson isn't "everything is fake." Maps tightly onto statutory RSHE/PSHE online-harms, body-image and pornography content at the right pitch, with solid accessibility (live regions, ARIA, TTS, focus styling) and no obvious bugs.
subject: Behaviour & Pastoral
name: Decode the Feed

rationale: A genuinely strong KS4 RSHE app: realistic scenarios with deliberate healthy-behaviour distractors make it misconception-aware, the two-stage spot-then-route structure builds from recognition to safe action, and content maps accurately to statutory RSHE on abusive relationships, coercive control, harassment and reporting routes. Interactivity is real (taggable lines plus drag/keyboard chip sorting with per-item rationale feedback) with thoughtful accessibility (aria-pressed, live regions, keyboard placement, safeguarding banner).
subject: Behaviour & Pastoral
name: Red Flags and Routes to Help

rationale: A genuinely strong CPD explainer: a real normal-distribution model anchors grade 7 to prior attainment and moves the boundary as difficulty changes, with a counterfactual "if the boundary never moved" row and a quiz that directly dismantles the "pass marks slashed / standards dropped" misconception. Accurate and honest with a clear caveat that real awarding tolerates small movement and uses examiner judgement, costing it only the top marks on alignment and technical polish.
subject: Data & Analytics
name: Comparable Outcomes Explainer

rationale: Sharp, purposeful CPD tool that makes the key leadership insight (headline P8 hides differential progress across prior-attainment bands) tangible through live recalculation, with strong keyboard/ARIA accessibility and a clear plain-English verdict. Slight conceptual fudge (whole-cohort P8 as a simple mean rather than the proper national-comparison method) and limited dot-staggering stop it short of perfect on alignment and technical.
subject: Data & Analytics
name: Prior-Attainment Band Splitter

rationale: A focused, misconception-aware data-literacy tool whose deliberately engineered dataset (with interaction terms) actually demonstrates that compound disadvantage hides behind single-characteristic charts, reinforced by an adaptive verdict and a consistent generated quiz. Robust, accessible and sector-accurate; held off full marks only by a narrow interaction surface and no small-n warning on tiny overlapping cohorts.
subject: Data & Analytics
name: Intersectional Gap Explorer

rationale: An excellent data-literacy CPD tool: 16 live sliders, three sharply-chosen scenarios (real/noise/borderline) and spread controls let staff feel how variance and group size, not just the mean change, decide whether a gain is trustworthy, with a misconception-aware verdict that explicitly checks the CI against zero. It loses a point on statistical fidelity because it applies an independent-samples pooled SD and z-based SE to data that is genuinely paired pre/post, despite labelling it "paired".
subject: Data & Analytics
name: Intervention Effect-Size Calculator

rationale: A genuinely strong KS1 Drama tool: the feeling/want/problem framework is sound characterisation pedagogy, the drag-and-drop builder gives immediate scene-sentence feedback with live coaching, and the "Act it out" steps with physical cues and a reflective change question turn it into a real whole-class lesson activity. Technically excellent with full keyboard and ARIA support, robust drag handling and TTS; cognitive demand is appropriate but modest and the core drama happens off-screen via the teacher.
subject: Drama
name: Character Builder (KS1 Drama)

rationale: Teaches four named drama conventions with worked-example animations then an apply phase whose distractors are themselves the other conventions, directly targeting the common KS2 confusion of mixing techniques, with explanatory feedback. Technically robust and notably accessible (semantic buttons, aria-pressed, aria-live, focus-visible, keyboard support, TTS), with progress tracking and reset.
subject: Drama
name: Convention Cards (Drama)

rationale: A well-built KS2 Drama rehearsal tool that genuinely scaffolds reading a script aloud: part selection with glowing cues, synced word highlighting, TTS with timer fallback, pace/pause markers and a pulsing "your turn" banner all map accurately to the KS2 spoken-language performance curriculum. The only real weakness is Step 3's self-check, which is pure self-report and always returns praise regardless of the button pressed.
subject: Drama
name: Read-Aloud Rehearsal Room

rationale: A polished, misconception-aware KS4 Drama tool that scaffolds live-theatre review across acting, design and audience impact with live band scoring, targeted feedback and a Band 3 to 4 exemplar, accurately mapped to GCSE expectations and built with genuinely strong accessibility. The main ceiling is that pupils select pre-written sentences rather than composing their own, making the cognitive demand recognition-led rather than full authorship.
subject: Drama
name: GCSE Live Review Coach

rationale: A polished, genuinely interactive KS1 punctuation activity: pointer drag-and-drop with keyboard fallback, type-specific feedback, prosody-based misconception support, TTS read-aloud and a conjunctions bonus, all well-aligned to NC KS1 sentence demarcation. Held off a perfect score because it teaches "! = command" as a fixed rule (commands can take a full stop too) and leaves minor cosmetic loose ends in the wrong-answer flash handling.
subject: English
name: Punctuation Power-Up

rationale: An exceptionally well-built KS2 morphology app: genuine drag-and-drop with keyboard and tap fallbacks, TTS, and outstanding misconception-aware feedback that distinguishes wrong-slot, wrong-part, extra-part and missing-part errors while teaching how stress and sound shift. Marred only by a spelling bug where the "active" + "-ate" challenge concatenates literally to display "activeate" rather than "activate".
subject: English
name: Prefix and Suffix Builder

rationale: A polished, genuinely interactive EYFS sorting task with pointer and full keyboard drag-drop, TTS read-aloud, immediate specific feedback that names the correct habitat when wrong, and clean accessible markup; it maps well to EYFS Understanding the World (the natural world / similar and different environments). Pedagogy is strong but the task is single-attribute matching with one fixed answer per token, so cognitive demand stays low and it does not surface the common misconception that some plants/animals can live in more than one habitat.
subject: Geography
name: Habitat Explorer Garden

rationale: A coherent, misconception-aware three-step sequence (ground-vs-aerial flip, N/S/E/W quiz, symbol-and-key plan builder) that maps precisely onto the KS1 geography curriculum, with genuine manipulation (real 3D flip, drag-and-drop plus tap-to-place, TTS) and strong accessibility. Pedagogy is heavily scaffolded (target cells and position labels are pre-shown), which caps cognitive demand just short of excellent.
subject: Geography
name: Bird's Eye School

rationale: A polished, accessible KS1 chronology activity that maps precisely onto the National Curriculum aim of sequencing events and using graded time vocabulary (long ago, a while ago, recently), with snap-to-slot drag, full keyboard control, ARIA live regions and per-photo spoken feedback. Cognitive demand is modest (only three items and the slots are pre-labelled with the answers, so it cues correct placement rather than truly testing it), which is the main thing holding it back from top pedagogy and interactivity marks.
subject: History
name: When Did It Happen? Class Timeline

rationale: A polished, historically accurate KS4 decision-tree across Berlin, Cuba and Vietnam, where each superpower choice nudges a live tension meter with tiered feedback and a "what really happened" note, then a debrief that connects escalation to detente. Genuine manipulation and immediate meaningful feedback, accurate to the GCSE non-British depth study, with accessible keyboard map, ARIA live regions and read-aloud.
subject: History
name: Cold War Flashpoints

rationale: Excellent KS2 proportion tool that ties three representations together (scaled ingredient table, double number line, and missing-number machine) with genuine drag/keyboard/button interactivity and strong accessibility. Pitch slips slightly when servings below the base produce sub-1 fractional multipliers (e.g. x0.25, 1.5 olives), which strays from the integer scale factors central to Year 6 ratio.
subject: Maths
name: Recipe Scaler

rationale: A genuinely thoughtful KS2 French article-sorting game with real pointer-based drag plus a keyboard/click route, per-item explanatory feedback, hint-aware scoring and adaptive levelling; it is explicitly misconception-aware (flags le livre as an -e exception and la maison as a feminine consonant-ender). Marked down on accuracy/robustness because French content drops the cedilla in garcon (should be garçon) and the heavily advertised j'aime verb step is only a prompt, never actually assessed.
subject: Modern Foreign Languages
name: Le, La, Les Sorting Game

rationale: Pedagogically excellent KS2 French phonics app: it explicitly links spelling, sound and meaning through authentic grapheme-phoneme correspondences (ou, oi, an, on, ch, eau) with plain-English cues, in-word highlighting, a two-stage explore-then-game flow, and targeted corrective feedback that names the real hidden sound on errors. Interaction is genuinely rich and multimodal (TTS in French and English, touch-ready drag-and-drop, full keyboard play, shuffled scored rounds) with strong accessibility, clean dependency-free code and proper demo-data safeguards.
subject: Modern Foreign Languages
name: Sound Spotter (French Phonics)

rationale: A genuinely well-designed KS3 MFL writing scaffold that moves pupils from facts to connected prose across three languages, with mark-scheme-aligned scoring (subordination and tense variety weighted highest), a worked model, live prose preview, per-language TTS and targeted next-move feedback. Polished, accessible (real keyboard drag-and-drop, ARIA, live regions) and self-contained, with the required demo-data safeguards.
fixIt: Accents are stripped from the target-language content (e.g. "musee", "Prufung", "Espana", "ano que viene"), which undermines accuracy in a writing tool; restore correct diacritics. Optionally tighten the prose-joining logic, which can produce slightly off punctuation in some tile orders.
subject: Modern Foreign Languages
name: Paragraph Climber (MFL KS3 Writing)

rationale: An exemplary KS3 RE tool that treats non-religious worldviews as positions in their own right rather than as the absence of religion, with three live sliders feeding a 2D belief map, a nearest-worldview match with specific reasoning, closeness bars and a strong teacher panel that nails the atheism/agnosticism/Humanism distinction. Technically clean (accessible labels, ARIA live region, keyboard/pointer support, TTS, demo modal); the only soft spot is conceptual rather than coded, as life-after-death is mapped only to ring size and so reads less clearly than the two plotted axes.
subject: Religious Education
name: The Non-Religious Map

rationale: Three accurate, well-pitched KS2 forces-and-magnets activities (magnetic sorting, pole push/pull with flip, friction ramp) with contextual graded feedback, strong teacher notes and probing questions, and excellent interactivity including drag, full keyboard support, ARIA tabs and aria-live regions. Marked down because objects auto-sort on touch rather than prompting predict-before-test, and the friction model is illustrative rather than physical.
subject: Science
name: Magnet Playground

rationale: A genuinely impressive real-time canvas that blends five emotion sliders into accurate colour palettes, weather, lighting and tree morphology, paired with model descriptive passages and an accurate, well-pitched Ruskin-grounded info panel; immediate meaningful feedback and strong KS3 fit. Loses marks because it demonstrates more than it asks pupils to produce, and the passage text mutates on a time-based/random index independent of user action, plus the reset handler carries dead/confused code.
subject: English
name: Pathetic Fallacy Landscape

rationale: A polished, robust etymology reference with a rich, mostly accurate dataset, canvas descent trees, working search and related-word cross-navigation, plus strong stats and history tabs that map directly onto the KS3 requirement to understand word origins and Latin/Greek/Anglo-Saxon roots. It loses a point each on pedagogy/interactivity for being exploratory rather than offering active retrieval or a check-for-understanding loop, and on alignment for minor mislabels (whiskey tagged Anglo-Saxon is actually Gaelic; clue's Greek myth node conflates story with word descent).
subject: English
name: Etymology Explorer

rationale: A genuinely rich KS4 writing-craft tool that deconstructs a paragraph and offers four real manipulation modes (sentence drag-reorder, clause drag-reorder, punctuation cycling, tiered vocabulary substitution) with live, misconception-aware scoring and diagnostic tips mapping directly onto AO5/AO6. Classification heuristics oversimplify (subordinating conjunctions mislabelled as adverbials, any -ly word tagged adverbial, arbitrary length-variance scaling) and drag-drop is not keyboard-accessible, but there are no crash-level bugs.
subject: English
name: Sentence Surgeon

rationale: A polished, genuinely interactive scansion trainer with real canonical lines, three-state syllable marking, per-syllable feedback, a rhythm waveform and scoring, well pitched for KS4 metre study. It loses marks because several keyed answers flatten famously contested or irregular lines (e.g. the "To be or not to be" feminine ending and "But soft" inversion) into a single binary-correct iambic pattern, which can entrench misconception despite the "dominant pattern" tip.
subject: English
name: Iambic Pentameter Scanner

rationale: A well-built EYFS tap-to-count app that genuinely teaches one-to-one correspondence (numbered badges, no double-tapping) and cardinality (the "There are N!" reveal), with strong immediate feedback and difficulty scaling from 1-5 up to 8-20 across 8 rounds, appropriately pitched to the Reception Number ELG. Minor quirks (the count display starting at "0" before counting, and div-based objects with no keyboard or screen-reader support) keep it just short of perfect.
subject: Maths
name: Counting Adventure

rationale: A well-pitched EYFS number-bonds app using the canonical ten-frame and part-part-whole number sentence, with two interaction modes, animated fill and warm immediate feedback. The main weakness is that the tap-cell path auto-submits only when the count happens to equal the answer, so a child can never produce a wrong answer through the manipulative itself, and distractors are random rather than misconception-targeted.
subject: Maths
name: Number Bonds to 10

rationale: A polished, genuinely interactive chart builder with live data editing, four chart types, touch/whiteboard support and accessible text summaries; statistics and a self-regenerating "read the graph" quiz add good cognitive value. Pitch is slightly over the KS2 programme of study (only mean is required at KS2; median/mode/range are KS3), and computing the "mode" of a frequency table's counts is statistically meaningless and mildly misconception-feeding.
subject: Maths
name: Statistics Graph Builder

rationale: A polished, accurate sequences and series explorer with four genuinely interactive modes (arithmetic, geometric, Fibonacci, custom pattern identifier) that update terms, formulas, bar/line charts, sums-to-infinity and a Fibonacci spiral live, with custom-built accessible canvas rendering. It demonstrates concepts very well but is light on misconception-probing tasks, and the sum-to-infinity/convergence content reaches above the stated KS3 pitch.
subject: Maths
name: Sequences & Series Explorer

rationale: A genuinely strong KS5 tool: dragging h toward zero shows the red secant converging to the green tangent while live secant/true/error readouts and correct step-by-step algebra make the limit definition concrete, with accurate derivatives across six functions including the harder 1/x case. It falls just short of a clean sweep because the symbolic working is static per function (only the final line substitutes x), and the generic safeguarding modal about "student records, names and grades" is irrelevant boilerplate for a maths visualiser.
subject: Maths
name: Differentiation from First Principles

rationale: A genuinely excellent open-ended circuit sandbox backed by a real Modified Nodal Analysis solver (Gaussian elimination with partial pivoting), correctly modelling series/parallel topologies, switches, variable resistors, high-resistance voltmeters and near-zero-resistance ammeters, with magnitude-scaled animated current and short-circuit detection. It maps well onto KS3 electricity (current, voltage, resistance, series vs parallel, correct meter placement), though it lacks any built-in scaffolding of the learning objective beyond worked examples and is essentially inaccessible to keyboard-only users.
subject: Science
name: Circuit Builder

rationale: A physically accurate two-source ripple-tank simulator with correct superposition (A·sin(kx−ωt)), 1/√r attenuation and proper (n+0.5)λ nodal lines, paired with genuinely rich manipulation: draggable sources (pointer/touch/keyboard), live sliders, single-source vs superposition toggles and accurate live readouts. Pitched well for KS4 GCSE waves, with strong accessibility (ARIA live region, keyboard source control); marred only by leftover dead nodal-line scaffolding and an amplitude control that cancels out in the normalised superposition view.
subject: Science
name: Wave Interference Simulator

rationale: A polished, accurate KS4 periodic table explorer: complete 118-element dataset, correct electron configurations and trend data, four colour-gradient trend overlays with legends, Bohr shell diagrams, live search and a substantial explanatory info panel. It teaches periodicity well through genuine manipulation; the main gaps are the lack of any prediction/comparison task to raise cognitive demand and clickable-div cells with no keyboard accessibility.
subject: Science
name: Periodic Table Explorer

rationale: A polished, accurate KS4 tectonics tool: P/S-wave animations plus a draggable triangulation map where moving stations live-updates seismogram traces, arrival times, time-differences and distance circles, with strong accessibility (keyboard control, aria-live, labelled canvases). Falls just short of a calculation-checking task and carries a minor formula inconsistency between the displayed ~8 km/s S-factor and the code's ~7.58 lag factor.
subject: Geography
name: Earthquake Waves & Triangulation

rationale: A polished, accurate GCSE-pitched treatment of the three classic urban models with genuine clickable-canvas interactivity, keyboard/aria-live accessibility, comparison panels and real UK city overlays that explicitly tackle the key misconception that no city matches one model literally. Loses marks only because the interactivity stays at click-to-reveal (no prediction, drag, sorting or self-checking task) and the generic safeguarding modal text is mismatched to the subject.
subject: Geography
name: Urban Land Use Models

rationale: A genuinely interactive temperature-driven glacier simulation that erodes a V-shaped valley into a U-shape with live process/landform highlighting, before/after comparison, and accurate, well-pitched KS4 content backed by correct UK exemplars (Red Tarn, Striding Edge, Cwm Idwal, Glen Coe). Robust and accessible; held off top technical marks only because several named landforms (arete, hanging valley, medial moraine) are triggered by a single erosion-depth threshold rather than being spatially modelled, so labels outrun the visual fidelity.
subject: Geography
name: Glaciation Landscape Sculptor

rationale: A genuinely strong KS3 source-evaluation tool built on the NOP/reliability/utility framework, with five historically accurate, well-pitched sources, select-to-tag annotation, a scaffolded analysis panel and formative feedback. Held back from top marks by crude keyword-only checking that matches on any single word and so can be gamed or yield false positives, ASCII-art placeholders for the visual sources, and no genuine cross-source comparison despite the comparative enquiry question.
subject: History
name: Source Analysis

rationale: A genuinely strong KS3 history tool: four accurate, well-chosen scenarios drive a coherent context-decision-reveal-reflection cycle that explicitly teaches historical empathy, presentism and the empathy/sympathy distinction. Interactivity is real (choice plus gated written reasoning plus reflection), let down only by a gameable keyword-counting "empathy score" that can reward word-matching over genuine thought.
subject: History
name: Historical Empathy Builder

rationale: A genuinely interactive select-highlight-label tool across four well-chosen, clearly-labelled-as-adapted historical sources, with live frequency charting, partial-credit scoring and misconception-aware corrective feedback that maps well to KS4 GCSE source-analysis and media-literacy aims. Held back slightly by a reductive single-technique answer key that ignores the legitimate interpretive overlap propaganda analysis depends on, and an indexOf-based offset match that could misalign on repeated slogans.
subject: History
name: Propaganda Techniques Decoder

rationale: A genuinely strong KS3 MFL tool: drag-and-drop blocks across French, Spanish and German with live person-correct verb conjugation in three tenses, automatic adjective gender/number agreement, colour-coded word classes, connectives for extended writing, validation tooltips and a challenge mode. Marks held below top because the "correct" check is shallow (it only confirms a subject and verb are present plus a few ordering rules, so wrong word order passes), French attributive-adjective placement is not modelled, Spanish "gustar" is conjugated as a regular verb (a known inaccuracy), and drag-drop has no keyboard-accessible alternative.
subject: Modern Foreign Languages
name: MFL Sentence Builder

rationale: A genuinely strong, accurate French tenses tool: an explorable timeline, a correct 7-tense conjugation table for 10 key irregulars, a randomised quiz with sensible distractors and misconception-aware feedback, and a guide that directly tackles the passe compose/imparfait distinction and etre-verb agreement. Pitched explicitly at GCSE rather than KS3 and the clickable timeline nodes lack keyboard accessibility, which keeps it just short of full marks.
subject: Modern Foreign Languages
name: Tense Timeline

rationale: A rich, phonetically accurate KS3 MFL tool spanning French, German and Spanish, with misconception-aware sound detail panels, a dynamically drawn articulation/mouth diagram, minimal-pairs browser and a working scored quiz; accessibility (aria-live canvas description) is genuinely considered. Held back from full marks chiefly by the absence of audio playback, a notable gap for a pronunciation app, leaving learners reliant on written IPA and approximations.
subject: Modern Foreign Languages
name: Pronunciation Rules Guide

rationale: A genuinely strong KS4 German tool: the Learn tab teaches all four cases with colour coding, question words, preposition groupings and the Wechselpräpositionen Wohin/Wo distinction, while the three-tier practice engine gives shuffled multiple-choice gap-fills with misconception-aware feedback (helfen/danken + dative, auf movement vs location, weak -(e)s endings). The main blemish is that umlauts are spelled out (Maedchen, schoen, waehrend) rather than using proper ä/ö/ü, which models non-standard orthography to language learners.
subject: Modern Foreign Languages
name: Case Declension Trainer

rationale: A content-rich, accurate KS4 conjugation trainer across French, German and Spanish with full paradigms, regular/irregular flagging, formation/usage notes and live accent-sensitive feedback, well pitched to GCSE MFL. The wheel is a slightly cosmetic framing and a few mechanics are rough, but the core typed-conjugation practice is genuinely valuable and robust.
subject: Modern Foreign Languages
name: Verb Tense Wheel

rationale: A polished, accurate KS3 RE gallery whose four-layer progressive-reveal model (surface, historical, theological, contemporary) delivers genuine cognitive scaffolding and misconception-aware content, well-pitched to the RE expectation that pupils grapple with symbolism, diversity and contested meaning. Technically robust with canvas-drawn symbols, full keyboard/ARIA accessibility and responsive layout; the main limitation is that interactivity peaks at the reveal mechanic while the quiz only tests shallow name/religion recall rather than the layered meanings.
subject: Religious Education
name: Religious Symbolism Gallery

rationale: A polished, accurate KS3 RE comparative-religion tool with multiple genuine interactions (clickable map, drag-and-drop theme matrix with scored feedback, hover overlap diagram) and a misconception-aware literal-vs-metaphorical section. Content is respectful and well-pitched; minor weaknesses in touch-drag reliability and feedback that flags wrong placements but ignores omissions hold it just short of exemplary.
subject: Religious Education
name: Creation Narratives Timeline

rationale: A genuinely capable argument-mapper for KS4 RE/Philosophy and Ethics: accurate, balanced content across four well-chosen topics, with real node placement, dragging, directional connections and meaningful structural validation plus a self-evaluation tab. It is held back from top marks because the "validation" rewards structure over reasoning (a supporting premise wired to an opposing conclusion still scores well) and the fallacy detection is shallow.
subject: Religious Education
name: Argument Mapper
