rationale: A genuinely strong pastoral/SEL tool: the draggable SVG thermometer with intensity-matched strategies, body map, scenarios, journal and crisis signposting (Childline/Shout) build the full self-regulation cycle (awareness, understanding, strategy, reflection) with robust cross-device pointer handling and safe HTML escaping. It maps soundly onto KS3/KS4 PSHE (mental wellbeing, emotional self-regulation) rather than the academic National Curriculum, and stops short of top marks because the strategy/scenario matching is fixed-content lookup with limited cognitive challenge or progression.
subject: Behaviour & Pastoral
name: Emotion Thermometer

rationale: A well-built PSHE/RSHE reflection tool: students assemble a concentric support network with smooth drag-to-reposition, live re-scoring, and genuinely misconception-aware prompts (it counters "more friends is better" and flags the safeguarding-critical absence of a trusted adult). Solid pointer/touch/mouse drag, XSS-safe name handling and responsive design; only the slightly gameable "health score" and the keyboard-inaccessible drag interaction hold back a top mark.
subject: Behaviour & Pastoral
name: Friendship Network Mapper

rationale: A genuinely useful CPD tool: live timed observation with tap-to-log across six research-grounded categories, evidence capture, auto-generated Strengths/EBI/Takeaway feedback with credible CPD reading links, and a pure-canvas dashboard (pie, radar, timeline, RAG coverage) with copy/print export. Held back by templated/formulaic feedback prose and, notably, no persistence so a refresh mid-observation loses everything.
subject: CPD & Staff Tools
name: Observation Feedback

rationale: A genuinely useful CPD planning tool: real drag-and-drop seating onto four configurable layouts with six live data overlays (gender, attainment, SEN, PP, EAL), recalculating composition stats, save/load, JSON import-export and a clean print view, all closely fitted to a teacher's professional planning purpose. Held back from top marks by mouse-only drag interaction with no keyboard/accessibility path and a random-placement Add Desk that can overlap existing desks.
subject: CPD & Staff Tools
name: Seating Plan Builder

rationale: A genuinely useful staff data tool with editable tables, sound RAG/flight-path/intervention logic, four live Chart.js views, and strong accessibility (sr-only summaries, ARIA). Fit for professional purpose, though the cohort-average target comparator, latest-grade-only RAG, and absence of persistence keep it just short of exemplary.
subject: Data & Analytics
name: Cohort Tracker

rationale: A genuinely capable teacher-facing data tool: editable score table, adjustable target band/Y-axis, custom-rendered flight-path chart with on-track/above/below flagging, and a draggable what-if projection that updates stats live, all wrapped in strong keyboard and screen-reader accessibility. Fit for its professional purpose of progress tracking and intervention triage, with only minor conceptual looseness (the "band" is a flat corridor rather than a true prior-attainment-derived flight path, and "distance from midpoint" is a slightly arbitrary metric).
subject: Data & Analytics
name: Flight Path Tracker

rationale: A polished, genuinely interactive radar-comparison data tool (multi-entity modes, PP/gender/national quick-loads, per-subject drill-downs with a national-average line, draggable what-if vertices, scale and grid toggles) that is fit for school-leader data-literacy and attainment-profile analysis. Marked down slightly because the percentage scale is a naive grade/9 mapping, the "radar area" coverage metric is order-dependent and somewhat arbitrary, and SVG interactions are mouse/touch-only with no keyboard or ARIA support.
subject: Data & Analytics
name: Subject Comparison Radar

rationale: A genuinely strong KS3 drama tool with real drag-and-drop blocking, multi-moment timelines, movement-path lines and playback, all built on robust Pointer Events that work on touch and whiteboards. Stage-geography terminology is accurate and well-pitched, and the only flaws are cosmetic (dead arrowhead-angle code, a generic demo modal mentioning grades/attendance that do not apply here).
subject: Drama
name: Stage Blocking

rationale: A genuinely useful GCSE Drama revision tool covering all the core specification practitioners accurately, with three complementary modes (explore, compare, self-marking quiz) that move beyond static reference into manipulation and immediate feedback. Falls just short of top pedagogy/interactivity marks because the learning is recognition-and-recall rather than applied (no scenario-building or open response), and the compare view is a fixed lookup rather than learner-driven analysis.
subject: Drama
name: Theatre Practitioners Explorer

rationale: A genuinely useful GCSE Drama rehearsal tool: word-level annotation across an accurate performance-skills taxonomy (pace, pitch, projection, gesture, proxemics, etc.), live annotation tallies, a working rehearsal timer, custom-text loading and a clean annotated-script print/export. Well-engineered with consistent word-indexing across render and print paths; the feedback is reflective rather than evaluative, which is appropriate for a coaching aid.
subject: Drama
name: Monologue Coach

rationale: A well-built, accurately pitched KS5 Drama scaffolding tool: seven analytical sections, correct A-Level terminology with definitions, sentence starters, live preview, progress tracking and export map cleanly onto the live theatre review task. Feedback is reflective rather than evaluative (it mirrors writing rather than critiquing it), and user text is injected via unescaped innerHTML.
subject: Drama
name: Live Theatre Review Builder

rationale: A physically accurate A-Level field simulator with genuine drag manipulation, live E/V/F readouts, field-line tracing, presets and a properly engineered keyboard/aria-live accessibility path; well-pitched to KS5 Fields content. Marked slightly down because equipotentials are rendered as a potential heatmap rather than true contour lines, and all tool/reading panels are hidden below 800px leaving mobile users only the canvas.
subject: Science
name: Electric Fields Simulator

rationale: A genuinely strong EYFS geography app: five scaffolded tasks teach relative-location vocabulary (near, across, between, route) with specific formative feedback rather than free placement, and the interaction layer is excellent (tap-to-place, pointer-based drag for touch/whiteboard, keyboard nudge, number keys, en-GB speech, ARIA). Pitch and content map accurately onto EYFS Understanding the World and early KS1 map skills.
subject: Geography
name: My World Map

rationale: A polished, accurate KS1 app that genuinely teaches the human/physical feature distinction and pairs it with a mathematically real tally chart (strokes grouped in fives with gate marks), with strong accessibility (keyboard, ARIA, speech) and immediate hint-driven feedback. Marked down slightly because the binary classify gives the answer away on a wrong try and the "fieldwork" is purely virtual rather than observational.
subject: Geography
name: Seaside Fieldwork

rationale: A polished, genuinely interactive EYFS counting app: real native-voice speech synthesis (not just phonetic text), one-to-one object correspondence linking to early number sense, and an active-retrieval "Listen and Find" quiz with named formative feedback. Accurate pronunciation, strong ARIA support and graceful audio fallbacks; pitched well for pre-literate learners as early language exposure rather than overclaiming formal MFL NC coverage.
subject: Modern Foreign Languages
name: Counting in Languages

rationale: A polished, accurate KS2 RE app with three genuinely distinct modes (explore, side-by-side compare with auto-generated insight, and an accessible drag/keyboard/touch matching quiz), strong ARIA and responsive design. Held back slightly by uneven religion coverage (no Sikhism or Buddhism; only two religions in three of the four rites) and a quiz that tests recall rather than the deeper comparison the app otherwise scaffolds.
subject: Religious Education
name: Rites of Passage

rationale: A polished, well-coded EYFS app with three genuinely progressive task types (find the identical missing item, complete an AB repeat, continue a growing 1-2-3 pattern), thoughtful shape-vs-colour distractors, immediate animated feedback, retry-on-error, progress dots and a score screen, all pitched correctly for early-years copying and continuing patterns. It falls just short of top marks because Level 1 is essentially "spot the same one" rather than true logical reasoning, and choice cards lack keyboard navigation and an aria-live region for feedback.
subject: Maths
name: What's Missing?

rationale: A genuinely strong KS1 reasoning tool: the two-stage judge-then-justify structure with misconception-based distractor reasons and category-specific visual proofs (base-ten blocks, number lines, parity pairs, commutativity arrays) directly targets the National Curriculum's reasoning aim and is well-pitched. Held back from top marks by reason practice being skipped after a wrong true/false answer and by the contested "circle has 1 side = false" convention.
subject: Maths
name: Reason and Explain

rationale: A well-built, misconception-aware KS1 app that genuinely teaches grapheme-level irregularity in common exception words and apostrophe contractions through real manipulation (grapheme tapping, drag/keyboard apostrophe drop) with accurate, curriculum-aligned "why" explanations and clear immediate feedback. It is robust, responsive and accessible, held just below top marks by the simplification of multi-irregular CEWs to one "tricky bit" and the generic, ill-fitting "student data" acknowledgement modal boilerplate.
subject: English
name: Tricky Word Detective

rationale: A well-built KS1 oracy app with three genuinely distinct activities (scaffolded talk-frame builder, role-play prompts, fair-turn timer), multi-modal input (drag, tap, keyboard), and word-class-aware feedback that gives reasons not just crosses. Robust, accessible and age-appropriate; held back only because the sentence builder checks a single exact correct word per slot, so it drills one fixed model sentence rather than supporting open spoken composition.
subject: English
name: Show and Tell Frames

rationale: A polished, genuinely interactive KS3 spoken-language tool: drag-and-drop speech building with specific, misconception-aware coaching (claim-first ordering, appeal balance, weak endings) plus a teleprompter rehearsal and live self-assessment checklist, all well aligned to the NC and cleanly built with strong accessibility. Cognitive demand is capped because pupils assemble pre-written blocks rather than composing their own persuasive language.
subject: English
name: Rhetoric Rehearsal

rationale: A well-sequenced, misconception-aware KS4 tool covering three GCSE Number skills (simplifying surds, rationalising denominators, bounds) with genuine drag/keyboard interaction, targeted distractor feedback, and clean accessible code. Maths is accurate and pitch is correct; held just short of top marks because the bounds bar is purely decorative (fixed 20-80% fill, mid always 50%) rather than scaled to the actual interval.
subject: Maths
name: Surds Simplifier

rationale: A well-built EYFS Understanding the World activity with a genuine predict-then-test loop: children sort into floaters/sinkers hoops, then drop objects into an animated tank and get immediate, misconception-aware feedback (air pockets, heavy-for-size key), with chips re-colouring to confirm or correct each guess. Robust dual input (real pointer drag plus a keyboard placement menu, live regions, focus management) and an ice-melt extension lift it above the typical drag-sort app.
subject: Science
name: Float or Sink Tank

rationale: A well-built, curriculum-accurate KS2 evolution and inheritance simulator: pupils alter the environment, choose an inherited trait, and watch deterministic natural selection shift the population over generations, closing with a fossil-evidence panel and clear vocabulary. It is explicitly misconception-aware ("Real animals do not choose; nature selects") with strong accessibility, though the pupil's trait choice does not actually steer the outcome, which slightly undercuts the agency of the interaction.
subject: Science
name: Survive the Island

rationale: A well-built, accurate four-tab KS3 chemistry tool: the equation-balancer offers genuine manipulation with live per-element atom tallies and misconception-aware feedback, and chemistry (Mr values, balanced forms, pH, energy profiles) is correct and well-pitched to the NC. It falls just short of top marks because the conservation-of-mass tab only ever reveals pre-balanced answers (mass in always equals mass out, with a dead "masses differ" branch) so it demonstrates rather than lets pupils test the idea.
subject: Science
name: Reaction Balancer

rationale: A well-built, accurate KS2 map that morphs across the four NC eras (Stone/Iron Age to Vikings) with era-specific overlays (Hadrian's Wall, Danelaw), tappable settlements giving solid age-appropriate facts, and a randomised clue-matching challenge with immediate feedback. Strong curriculum fit and good keyboard/ARIA accessibility; held back slightly by a crude, geographically loose schematic outline and content that is not deliberately misconception-aware.
subject: History
name: Britain Through the Ages: Invaders Map

rationale: A historically accurate, misconception-aware KS3 World History app that maps Mughal/Qing trade, idea and conflict links to global regions with per-card explanatory feedback and a synthesis directly tackling the "isolated then British" misconception; strong accessibility (keyboard placement, ARIA, live regions). It maps well onto the NC's "non-European society contrasting with British history" and East India Company strands, with only a slightly forced "Ottoman & Persian lands" catch-all region (the Qing-Russia Nerchinsk card sits there awkwardly).
subject: History
name: Empires Connected

rationale: A genuinely strong EYFS Geography activity: choose two contrasting homes on a globe, view a side-by-side scene comparison, then drag-sort four attributes into same/different bins with specific, encouraging feedback, full keyboard support and ARIA live regions. It maps cleanly to EYFS Understanding the World (people, culture and communities) at the right pitch, with robust pointer-drag, a sensible keyboard fallback and clean child-friendly design.
subject: Geography
name: Around the World Storybox

rationale: A well-built KS2 locational-knowledge drag-and-drop with genuine pointer and keyboard interaction, shuffled labels, first-try scoring, hints and misconception-aware feedback that names the correct category. Minor accuracy slip: the UK tab classifies counties (Devon, Cornwall, Yorkshire) as "country", and the simplified outlines are quite crude.
subject: Geography
name: Map the Americas

rationale: A polished, accurate KS2 RE sorter: pupils place religious and Humanist answers to ultimate questions with instant, reason-giving feedback, then a comparison panel pushes genuine compare/contrast thinking that is core to RE rather than rote recall. Robust drag-and-drop with click and keyboard fallbacks, sound accessibility (aria-live, focus styles), and content that correctly handles worldviews; the binary religious/Humanist sort and small bank of four questions slightly cap the cognitive ceiling.
subject: Religious Education
name: Big Question Sorter

rationale: A genuinely strong KS2 French reading app: real sentence-level comprehension via drag/keyboard matching with immediate targeted feedback, plus a contextual mini-dictionary that scaffolds dictionary skills directly named in the MFL NC. Accessibility and design are well above average; the main flaw is stripped French accents (garcon, gateau) in an app where orthography matters.
subject: Modern Foreign Languages
name: Read and Match (KS2 French Reading)

rationale: A polished, accessible KS2 MFL app with three countries, contextual vocab, structured there-vs-UK cultural comparisons and per-option quiz feedback that genuinely teaches intercultural understanding; it loses a touch because the language strand is thin (one word per moment, phonetic respelling only, no actual audio despite the speaker button) so it pitches more as culture than language production.
subject: Modern Foreign Languages
name: A Day in the Life

rationale: A coherent, well-scaffolded KS4 MFL writing tool across three target-language festivals: a captioned offline clip, tone-sorting drag-drop, a live opinion builder and a self-marked GCSE draft, each giving immediate meaningful feedback and explicitly teaching the GCSE mark structure. Accurate and accessibly built; the draft checker is shallow keyword-matching rather than genuine assessment, which caps pedagogy.
subject: Modern Foreign Languages
name: Festival Explorer

rationale: A polished, exam-authentic A-level speaking trainer that genuinely rehearses the Paper 3 stimulus-card debate and IRP, with a sound four-step structure, banded timer feedback and concession-then-reframe model rebuttals across French, Spanish and German. Main weakness is that all target-language text is stripped of accents, which matters in an MFL context where diacritics are assessed and modelled.
subject: Modern Foreign Languages
name: Stimulus Debate Arena (MFL KS5)

rationale: A polished, accessibility-conscious EYFS class-vote app that maps cleanly onto the PSED "Making relationships" strand, with three social scenarios, per-card tallies for genuine class voting, animated consequences and re-vote prompts on unkind choices. Cognitive demand is appropriately gentle but a little predictable (always two kind options vs one obviously unkind one, with no real dilemma), and the SVG/speech-bubble animation reuses the Rosie/Finn characters even when later stories introduce new names.
subject: Behaviour & Pastoral
name: Sharing the Sandpit

rationale: A well-judged KS1 online safety game whose six scenarios are genuinely misconception-aware (prize pop-ups, strangers asking for personal data, scary content vs safe teacher apps and known friends), with differentiated per-scenario feedback, a rule-collection mechanic and a teacher-led think-pair-share framing that maps cleanly onto the Education for a Connected World / statutory RSHE online-safety strands. Technically clean and accessible (aria-live, focus-visible, keyboard shortcuts, responsive tablet UI) with no bugs found.
subject: Behaviour & Pastoral
name: Smart Tap Tablet

rationale: Four genuine interactive mechanics (drag/keyboard plate builder, week-activity toggles, sleep slider, DALT safety choices) feed a transparent scoring model and a reactive avatar, mapping closely to KS2 Health Education/PSHE on balanced diet, physical activity, sleep and drug/alcohol/tobacco safety with age-appropriate, misconception-aware feedback. Accessibility is strong (aria-live, keyboard add, focus styles) and the build is clean and robust, holding it just short of an exceptional 5 only by the scoring being a simplified composite rather than mirroring a true Eatwell proportion model.
subject: Behaviour & Pastoral
name: Fuel Your Body

rationale: A calm, accurate and misconception-aware treatment of the statutory KS2 "changing adolescent body" strand, with three genuinely interactive activities (age-slider timeline, drag/keyboard/tap myth sort with per-card explanations, and self-care matching) and notably strong accessibility including keyboard sorting, ARIA live regions and a tap fallback. It falls short of full marks because the self-care match is a unique one-to-one mapping solvable by elimination, the timeline is reveal-only with low cognitive demand, and a redundant no-op ternary (line 615) hints at light QA.
subject: Behaviour & Pastoral
name: Growing and Changing

rationale: A well-judged, misconception-aware KS4 RSE activity that accurately models consent, coercion tactics, age-vs-capacity and England's legal facts, with differentiated instant feedback and a sound safeguarding signpost. It is held just short of top marks because the "branching" scenario is effectively linear (all choices funnel to the same next node) and the true/false mythbuster reveals correctness on selection, making it lower-demand than the framing implies.
subject: Behaviour & Pastoral
name: Consent and Pressure Scenarios

rationale: A polished, genuinely interactive EYFS Art app with three distinct activities (live canvas-based colour mixing, a predict-the-colour wheel quiz, and a randomised texture-matching hunt), accurate colour chemistry, retry feedback, and strong accessibility (ARIA, keyboard tabs, focus outlines, touch handling). It maps cleanly onto EYFS Expressive Arts and Design at the right pitch and is well worth lesson time.
subject: Art and Design
name: Magic Mixing Pots (EYFS Art)

rationale: A well-built EYFS carpet-time tool: children tap emotion faces and colour swatches against three original SVG "paintings" and receive gentle, validating, talk-prompting feedback that models the right vocabulary for Expressive Arts and Design. Clean, responsive and genuinely accessible (aria-pressed, aria-live, focus-visible, keyboard support), with interactivity that is real if modest in depth.
subject: Art and Design
name: How Does It Make You Feel?

rationale: A genuinely interactive KS1 drawing lab that teaches the expressive qualities of line through real canvas drawing, a line-to-feeling matching game, and an applied creature task, all accurately pitched to the England KS1 Art curriculum with strong accessibility. Held back slightly by a tracking flaw where Step 1 exploration pre-fills the Step 3 creature checklist and by generic boilerplate in the demo-data modal.
subject: Art and Design
name: Line Lab

rationale: A polished, offline KS1 Art app that maps cleanly onto the National Curriculum requirement to study named artists and link their techniques to pupils' own work, with a progressive reveal mechanic, misconception-aware per-option feedback, and accurate fact cards naming real techniques (impasto, soft dabs, swirling strokes). It is held just short of top marks by a low cognitive ceiling (multiple-choice recognition), only three fixed cases, and a mismatched shared demo-data modal whose copy talks about "student records, grades and attendance" rather than artwork.
subject: Art and Design
name: Artist Detective

rationale: A genuinely manipulable colour-wheel (drag, keyboard, spin, click) with live scheme highlighting, swatch palette, scheme-specific feedback, a recolouring sample painting and a misconception-aware quiz, well pitched to KS2 visual-elements vocabulary and largely accessible. Held off top marks because the warm/cool temperature classifications are debatable simplifications (yellow-green tagged cool, red-violet warm) that a teacher would need to caveat.
subject: Art and Design
name: Colour Harmony Wheel

rationale: A genuinely interactive timeline scrubber that re-paints one procedurally-drawn scene live in six recognisable movement styles, paired with a directional, feature-aware curator quiz; it maps cleanly onto the KS2 "great artists" strand with age-appropriate language, real keyboard/ARIA accessibility and no obvious bugs. It loses a point on pedagogy and alignment because the styles are evocative caricatures aimed at recognition and ordering rather than developing actual technique, and the generic "no real child / grades / attendance" demo modal copy is a slight mismatch for an art tool.
subject: Art and Design
name: Movement Timeline

rationale: A genuinely well-designed KS2 critique tool built on the Describe-Analyse-Interpret-Judge frame, with evidence-gated steps and misconception-aware coaching that demands justification rather than a single right answer. The one real weakness is that colour-callout temperature is randomly assigned on click rather than read from the pixel under the cursor, so the analysis can contradict what the pupil actually clicked.
fixIt: Alignment: sample the actual colour under the click point (or constrain callouts to predefined warm/cool regions of each SVG painting) so the warm/cool label always matches the colour the pupil pointed at; the current random assignment undermines the evidence-to-claim link the whole critique frame depends on.
subject: Art and Design
name: Critique Lens (Art KS2)

rationale: Genuinely generative app: the same pupil-drawn strokes are re-rendered through four distinct, accurate procedural media (charcoal grain, ink bleed, lino relief with print reversal, torn-paper collage), with sound art vocabulary and a brief-matching task, well aligned to KS3 NC media proficiency. Strong accessibility (keyboard drawing, live region, aria-pressed, focus-visible) holds it back from full marks only because the "match the brief" quiz can never be answered wrongly and some drawing-input code is dead/sloppy.
subject: Art and Design
name: Media Mixer (KS3 Art)

rationale: A polished, fully offline EYFS music app with three genuinely distinct activities (synthesised sing-along with per-syllable pitch and beat plus a bouncing-ball word tracker, a real MediaRecorder class recorder with feature detection and fallbacks, and a beat-accurate Pat-a-Cake clap map with on-beat timing feedback), mapping cleanly onto EYFS Expressive Arts and the DfE Music Model Curriculum for singing rhymes and keeping a steady beat. Accessibility (aria-live, aria-pressed, focus-visible, keyboard handlers) and robustness (cleanup, suspended-context resume) are strong; minor dead code and slight ball-position drift on wrapped lyrics are cosmetic.
subject: Music
name: Sing-Along Story Time (Music, EYFS)

rationale: A genuinely strong EYFS listening game: it synthesises four distinct percussion timbres live via the Web Audio API and demands real discrimination between sounds, with timbre-specific feedback and a dynamics (loud/quiet) extension that maps cleanly onto the EYFS Expressive Arts and Design "listen attentively, move to and talk about sounds" goals. Interactivity is excellent (pointer drag with flying clone, tap-to-select, and full keyboard pickup/drop), held back only by reliance on synthesised approximations rather than real instrument recordings and a hard page reload on restart.
subject: Music
name: What Made That Sound?

rationale: A genuinely interactive EYFS composing tool with synthesised Web Audio sounds, drag-and-drop plus a full keyboard placement path, and differentiated "check pattern" feedback that nudges children from random sounds toward actual repeating patterns and contrast, well matched to EYFS Expressive Arts and Design. Slightly undercut by loose linkage between the picture-book pages and the composition, and a pattern detector that only catches strict ABAB sequences.
subject: Music
name: Sound Story Maker

rationale: A polished, robust KS1 Music app combining a real-time falling-colour-cue play-along, a WebAudio colour-coded glockenspiel, and a percussion pulse band, with timing-sensitive and misconception-aware feedback ("match the colour"), genuine multi-input interactivity (touch/mouse/number keys/space) and good accessibility. Accurately mapped to the KS1 programme of study (play tuned and untuned instruments musically; pulse); minor blemishes are harmless dead code in the band accounting and the slightly notional pitch link between abstract falling cues and real glockenspiels.
subject: Music
name: Glockenspiel Hero

rationale: A genuinely strong KS1 Music app: three coherent activities (pitch slider, high/low listening game, dynamics/tempo mixer) using real Web Audio synthesis with immediate, meaningful feedback, log-space pitch glide, clear NC vocabulary, and notably good accessibility (ARIA slider, keyboard control with note-snapping, live regions). Held just below top marks by a musically loose note-name labelling (G A B C D E G C is not a clean scale and the "note: G" readout adds little for 5-7 year olds).
subject: Music
name: High Low Slider

rationale: A genuinely strong KS1 listening app: live Web Audio synthesis, real-time tapping with a sensible hit window, streaks, precise-vs-near feedback, mission-specific miss messages, per-clue review and solid accessibility (radiogroups, focus management, aria-live, whiteboard spacebar). Pedagogy and alignment are slightly held back because the synthesised timbres (especially the 784Hz triangle "flute") are too crude to make instrument identification a sound musical task or to deliver the NC aim of listening to high-quality music.
subject: Music
name: Listening Detectives

rationale: A well-built, concept-strong KS2 singing app: staggered multi-group cursors, synthesised audio, count-in and a tap-the-beat steadiness check make the abstract idea of a round tangible and directly support the KS2 "sing in unison and parts with control" curriculum aim. The tap evaluation measures only tap-to-tap consistency rather than alignment to the actual beat, and the round plays a single pass rather than looping, which slightly weakens the rigour, but overall it is genuinely excellent and lesson-worthy.
subject: Music
name: Round Robin Rounds

rationale: A genuinely interactive listening tool: synthesised audio for each tradition is thoughtfully constructed (inharmonic gong partials, drone plus meend slides, layered polyrhythms), and the quiz gives diagnostic feedback naming the telltale aural clue, mapping well onto the KS3 "history, traditions and world musics" strand. Held back from top marks by audio being an acknowledged approximation, a purely decorative abstract "map" that adds little geographic value, and an off-topic demo-data modal about fabricated child records.
subject: Music
name: World Music Atlas

rationale: A genuinely well-built KS4 performance tool: a WebAudio click track with count-in, real tap-timing analysis (signed drift, SD-based steadiness, beat-matching) feeding clear coaching feedback, then self-marking against GCSE-style strands with a multi-take progress chart. It pitches correctly to the GCSE performing component and is careful to treat the timing measure as only a suggestion rather than the mark, which is misconception-aware; its ceiling is that two of the three marked strands (technical control, expression) rely on unscaffolded self-report since the app measures tapping to a click, not actual playing.
subject: Music
name: Performance Logbook

rationale: A well-built, accurate explorer pairing a reference panel (defining features, signature works, described listening walkthroughs) with a Style Detective quiz whose "why" and "distinguish" feedback directly targets the classic confusions (fusion vs world, classical vs popular), giving real cognitive demand and misconception-awareness. The honest limitation is that all listening is described rather than streamed, so it trains terminology and stylistic reasoning rather than true aural identification, which is the core GCSE skill.
subject: Music
name: Areas of Study Explorer (Music KS4)

rationale: A well-built, accurate KS2 networks app: animated request/response packet journey with keyboard-accessible hops, clear narrated status, and a misconception-aware self-marking quiz that nails internet-vs-WWW, packets and ISP. Genuine interactivity and strong accessibility lift it, though the page selector only alters a text label rather than the visual, so manipulation is fairly shallow.
subject: Computing
name: Journey of a Web Request

rationale: A genuinely well-designed KS2 app that maps precisely onto the "select, use and combine software on a range of digital devices to accomplish given goals" objective, with real drag/keyboard manipulation, a smart device-preview overflow check that makes the "range of devices" strand concrete, and a clear 6-point brief checklist with meaningful feedback. Held just below top marks because the checks are name-match mechanical (gameable), sounds are static labels that never play, and the device preview alters width only.
subject: Computing
name: Multimedia Maker

rationale: A polished, genuinely manipulable EYFS construction tool with real drag/rotate/join mechanics, contextual feedback, a sensibly pitched "Creating with Materials" framing, and a Tool Tap mini-game that targets the real misconception of matching joining method to purpose. Held back from full marks because the free-build join detection accepts any tool regardless of suitability (so it never corrects a poor choice in the building space itself), rotation labelled as "shaping" is a slight stretch, and the inherited demo-data modal text about "student records, grades and attendance" is irrelevant boilerplate for this app.
subject: Design and Technology
name: Junk Model Maker

rationale: A well-built KS1 Cooking and Nutrition app that maps tightly to the National Curriculum: tap-to-reveal food trails with a plant/animal sort (including thoughtful processed-food trails like cheese back to milk to cow), order-enforced hygiene steps, and an accessible drag-or-keyboard snack builder with immediate, misconception-aware feedback. Strong accessibility and clean design; held back only by modest cognitive demand and a non-functional Stage 1 gate (the "finish tracing first" lock never actually engages, so Stage 2 is always usable).
subject: Design and Technology
name: Farm to Plate

rationale: A purposeful KS2 D&T making app that genuinely teaches two distinct skills (selecting a component by functional property, then measuring/marking to a target within tolerance) with real drag/nudge/keyboard manipulation, immediate colour-coded difference feedback, and consequence visuals (offcut wasted vs accepted). Solid accessibility and accurate curriculum pitch; held back from a perfect score by the try-square being decorative (squareness is taught by label, never by manipulation) and a feedback string that mislabels a length error as "not square".
subject: Design and Technology
name: Mark It Out

rationale: A genuinely well-targeted EYFS Physical Development (fine motor) warm-up: three distinct manipulative tasks (ordered threading, pinch-and-drop, on-path tracing) with real drag physics, misconception-aware feedback (whole-hand grab returns the bead, wrong colour order, straying off the line), full pointer/mouse/keyboard support, ARIA live regions and a teacher panel that correctly frames it as model-then-do before active play. Pedagogy is one shy of a 5 because it is a brief screen-based warm-up that, by its own admission, only models grip rather than developing it (real development needs the physical equivalent), and on-screen dragging does not actually train a physical pinch.
subject: Physical Education
name: Grip and Grab

rationale: A well-judged KS1 PE coaching and assessment tool: each fundamental movement skill (run, jump, throw, catch) is broken into sound, child-friendly technique steps with explicit "look for" checkpoints, backed by genuine interactivity (play/pause slow-motion, scrubber, spinner, live thumbs self-assessment) and tight National Curriculum alignment. The animation is really a 4-frame step-through and the spinner's visual landing is approximate, but it is robust, accessible and clearly worth lesson time.
subject: Physical Education
name: Throw and Catch Coach

rationale: A genuinely interactive SVG tactics board with real geometric scoring (point-to-segment pass lanes, goal-side marking, shot-line blocking) and graded, misconception-aware feedback that maps cleanly onto the KS1 NC tactics aim. Slightly held back by abstract numeric "units" language that is over-pitched for 5-7 year-olds and by static (non-dynamic) opposition.
subject: Physical Education
name: Space Invaders PE

rationale: A genuinely well-built KS1 dance tool: children build a four-beat phrase, play it against an adjustable beat with a 5-6-7-8 count-in and per-slot highlighting, then rebuild it from memory, all with specific, dance-aware feedback and strong accessibility (ARIA, keyboard, reduced-motion, touch/mouse/IWB drag plus tap fallback). It accurately matches the KS1 PE "simple movement patterns to a steady beat" objective and is worth whole-class lesson time.
subject: Physical Education
name: Move to the Beat (KS1 Dance)

rationale: A polished, genuinely interactive KS2 athletics app: phase-stepped technique demos with sound coaching cues, a validated results board that auto-detects PBs and correctly distinguishes timed (lower-better) from field (higher-better) events, and a clear hand-drawn progress chart with plain-English summaries. Strong NC alignment and good accessibility; only let down by fairly generic stick-figure animation and session-only data.
subject: Physical Education
name: Athletics Arena

rationale: A genuinely interactive route-planning tool with a brute-force optimal-path solver, live distance feedback, adjacent-swap coaching tips and an accessible keyboard/SVG interface, mapping well onto KS2 OAA problem-solving and route choice. It abstracts orienteering to a maths-style travelling-salesman puzzle, so it teaches route optimisation more than authentic map-and-compass/terrain reading, which slightly limits PE-specific pedagogy and curriculum fit.
subject: Physical Education
name: Orienteering Quest

rationale: Four genuine, well-integrated tools (stroke animator, two-step diagnose-then-fix fault finder, water safety scenarios with specific feedback, statutory 25m tracker) that map precisely onto the only sport-specific KS2 NC content (25m, range of strokes, safe self-rescue, Float to Live), with strong ARIA accessibility. The headline SVG stick-figure animator is crude and abstract (breaststroke read from a side view where its key features are not visible, and an odd recovery-arm geometry), keeping it short of excellent.
subject: Physical Education
name: Stroke Lab

rationale: A genuinely well-engineered KS2 PE peer-assessment tool with zone-aware feedback, real biomechanical coaching cues, and full keyboard/ARIA accessibility, mapping near-verbatim onto the NC "compare performances and achieve personal best" strand. Cognitive demand is solid-but-moderate and the crude stick figures plus character-count-only text checks keep it just short of exceptional.
subject: Physical Education
name: Coach's Eye

rationale: A polished, accessible multi-sport tactics board with genuine drag/keyboard manipulation, animated scenarios and specific positional feedback that maps well onto the KS3 PE "use tactics and strategies to overcome opponents / analyse to improve" aims across invasion, net and striking games. Held back from top marks by a crude scoring model where opponent markers are decorative (unlinked to the attack path) and the "Space" reward for spread can wrongly penalise a correctly compact defence.
subject: Physical Education
name: Game Plan Studio

rationale: A coherent three-step KS1 Citizenship app (drag good-vs-distractor rule cards with targeted feedback, a live vote tally with winner/tie handling, and an SVG playground sim contrasting orderly turn-taking with rules-off chaos) that maps precisely onto the PSHE/Citizenship rules-fairness-taking-part strand at the right infant pitch, with genuinely good accessibility (keyboard drag path, aria-live narration, focus-visible). It falls short of full marks because the voting and simulation outcomes are deterministic and single-user rather than emergent or truly class-driven.
subject: Citizenship
name: Build Our Class Rules

rationale: A polished, age-appropriate KS1 Citizenship sorting game with genuine multi-modal interaction (drag, tap-then-tap, and full keyboard), live happiness meter, and misconception-aware per-item feedback distinguishing recyclables from true rubbish. Robust and accessible (ARIA labels, live regions, focus styles, whiteboard keyboard path), with sound but modest binary-sort cognitive demand.
subject: Citizenship
name: Tidy Town

rationale: A genuinely strong KS3 Citizenship tool: real AV elimination/transfer and correct largest-remainder PR let pupils see identical ballots crown different winners, with presets that deliberately stage misconceptions (spoiler effect, dead heat) and an insight engine that names the FPTP-vs-AV divergence. The FPTP and AV seat-block models are invented heuristics (an arbitrary power-law amplification and a forced proportional "winner block") that misrepresent how those systems actually allocate seats, slightly denting accuracy.
subject: Citizenship
name: Election Lab

rationale: A polished, accurate KS4 Citizenship sandbox where pupils join/leave real UK treaties and memberships and see live trade/security/influence meters, scenario briefs and explanatory feedback; strong NC alignment and accessibility. Loses marks because the "live trade-offs" framing overstates a purely additive model with little genuine opportunity cost beyond two mutually-exclusive sovereignty constraints.
subject: Citizenship
name: Britain in the World

rationale: A polished, robust and genuinely interactive staff data tool: editing any grade instantly recomputes entry rate, APS, standard/strong pass and a "one subject short" coaching prompt, with strong accessibility (aria-live, focus-visible, sr-only announcer). It loses ground on fidelity to the official EBacc APS, which sums the best two sciences across six qualifications and divides by six; here Sciences is one pillar using only the single best science grade and the cohort APS divides by five, so the modelled APS will not match real Performance Tables figures.
fixIt: Alignment: align the APS engine with the DfE method, sum the best two science grades into the science contribution and divide each pupil's total by six (or clearly relabel it as a simplified five-pillar proxy) so the displayed APS matches an actual Performance Tables calculation rather than understating science.
subject: Data & Analytics
name: EBacc Entry and APS Dashboard

rationale: A polished, accurate data-literacy tool that teaches the genuinely valuable distinction between total absence and unauthorised absence, with interactive year-group exploration and five data-driven challenge questions carrying specific, misconception-aware feedback. Technically robust and accessible; loses marks only because cognitive demand is moderate and the underlying datasets are fixed rather than user-adjustable.
subject: Data & Analytics
name: Attendance Code Breakdown

rationale: A polished, genuinely interactive leadership data tool: cohort and two policy levers drive live metrics, disproportionality bars with parity markers, RAG flags, a pupil table and a prose judgement that explicitly teaches that longer suspensions raise lost days without closing the disadvantage/SEND gap while only early help narrows it, all using accurate DfE framing (episodes per 100, PEX rate, parity ratios). Strong accessibility and clean design with no breaking bugs, though the "pupils suspended" count rests on arbitrary magic-number heuristics that can yield slightly implausible distinct-pupil figures.
subject: Data & Analytics
name: Suspensions and Exclusions Monitor

rationale: A genuinely interactive, configurable behaviour-data heatmap with a ratio-to-mean cell inspector, peak highlighting, full keyboard grid navigation, and a self-checking quiz whose answers are computed live from the dataset, making it a fit-for-purpose pastoral triage trainer for staff. Built on a deterministic seeded dataset with believable baked-in hotspots; minor ARIA-grid incompleteness and no real-data import keep it just short of perfect.
subject: Data & Analytics
name: Behaviour Referral Analyser

rationale: A well-targeted KS4 MFL minimal-pair discrimination trainer with linguistically accurate phonetic content across French, German and Spanish, real TTS playback, and three escalating modes (Explore, Drag-and-Match, Quiz) with live accuracy stats. Interactivity is excellent, including keyboard and touch alternatives, an honest fallback warning when no native voice is installed, and ARIA accessibility; the only soft spot is that the SVG waveforms are decorative pseudo-random shapes rather than real spectrograms.
subject: Modern Foreign Languages
name: Sound Discrimination Trainer

rationale: A thoughtfully built EYFS self-regulation tool: procedurally drawn SVG faces, three escalating modes (colour-coding emotions, matching calming actions, and a Calm Corner with good/distractor discrimination), genuine pointer drag-and-drop with a keyboard fallback, ARIA and TTS audio prompts. It maps well onto the EYFS PSED self-regulation framework (EYFS has no formal subject NC, so judged against that) and is robust with no significant bugs.
subject: Behaviour & Pastoral
name: Feelings Faces Match

rationale: A well-built EYFS carpet-time tool that deliberately decouples effort from success, growing an animated SVG leaf with rotating praise, TTS, milestones and a progress bar to a 12-leaf goal; it maps cleanly to EYFS PSED self-regulation and the Characteristics of Effective Learning, and is technically robust (safety caps, reduced-motion handling, ARIA live region, focus-visible, keyboard/touch parity, graceful TTS fallback). Manipulation is a single repeated button so depth is modest, but it is genuinely fit for adult-led whole-class use.
subject: Behaviour & Pastoral
name: The Try-Again Tree

rationale: A genuinely strong KS4 pastoral tool with a coherent build-test-reflect arc, a misconception-aware toolkit-balance mechanic (avoid habits, over-reliance, packed-vs-unpacked), and rich state-driven feedback, TTS, keyboard and drag/drop accessibility. Marked down slightly because the pressure-week scoring lets any all-healthy toolkit win comfortably and unpacked healthy choices are still rewarded, softening the intended consequence.
subject: Behaviour & Pastoral
name: Stress and Resilience Studio

rationale: A genuinely useful CPD tool for assessment leads: the DfE point-score conversions (reformed 9-1 = grade number, legacy A*=8.5/C=4, approved Level 2 vocational equivalents) are accurate and the cohort builder plus misconception-aware "convert it yourself" challenge with targeted direction hints make it a strong professional trainer. It loses a point on alignment because the "APS for a cohort" framing blends per-learner and per-subject averaging into a simple unweighted mean without flagging that distinction, which a real Attainment 8 calculation would not.
subject: Data & Analytics
name: Grade-to-Points Converter

rationale: A genuinely well-built EYFS Drama tool: tapping any of eight cards triggers a distinct hand-coded SVG movement animation, a spoken coaching cue, and a live class vote between four body shapes with tallies, bars and a winning highlight, all with strong aria/keyboard accessibility. Pitch and content suit EYFS expressive-arts movement work, though the "best shape" mapping is occasionally arbitrary (e.g. a light, twirling fairy keyed to "tall and stretched") and one helper function is a no-op, which keeps it from a perfect score.
subject: Drama
name: Move Like a... (Drama, EYFS)

rationale: A polished, accessible KS2 drama tool that genuinely supports characterisation, role and status work, with character selection, status toggling, probing questions, class voting, streaks and coaching feedback. It sits just below top marks on pedagogy because the vote and feedback are teacher-subjective and the feedback text is randomly canned rather than tied to the actual spoken answer.
subject: Drama
name: Hot Seat Generator

rationale: A genuinely well-built synthetic-phonics blending tool: real drag manipulation with pointer/keyboard support, order-aware feedback, digraph colour-coding treated correctly as one sound, and a sound-by-sound blend sequence. The chief compromise is reliance on device TTS fed schwa-laden approximations ("tuh", "cuh", "duh") that undercut the pure-sound goal a phonics tool exists to model.
subject: English
name: Sound Button Blender

rationale: A genuinely thoughtful KS4 creative-writing scaffold: the pan/zoom SVG camera is wired to four tiered prompts that model how strong description guides the reader's eye from wide shot to telling detail, with tier-specific keyword/figurative feedback, model sentences, and a composed-piece builder accurately pitched to AO5/AO6. Marks held off 5s because the regex marking is shallow and gameable and the bolted-on demo-data modal is incongruous for a tool that handles no student data.
subject: English
name: Descriptive Zoom

rationale: A polished, well-structured KS2 physical-geography app with a clear explore-then-consolidate arc, accurate child-friendly content that explicitly tackles the magma-vs-lava misconception, and genuinely strong multi-modal interactivity (hotspots, eruption animation, drag/tap/keyboard placement) with immediate specific feedback. It loses marks only because the labelling task is recall-level matching rather than deeper reasoning, and minor polish issues (boilerplate demo-modal text about "student records/grades" irrelevant to this app, possible slot-text overflow on very narrow screens).
subject: Geography
name: Volcano Cross-Section

rationale: A polished, accessible EYFS app that maps precisely onto the Past and Present ELG, with genuine flip/read-aloud/sorting interactivity, immediate spoken and visual feedback, and good change-and-continuity framing. Cognitive demand is modest and the geometric SVG scenes are crude, so it leans on adult mediation, but it is robust and clearly worth lesson time.
subject: History
name: Who Helps Us, Then and Now

rationale: A historically accurate, well-pitched KS2 depth study of Baghdad c.900 with three genuinely distinct interactions (knowledge-journey matching, a draggable/keyboard astrolabe dial with tolerance-banded feedback, and a quiz), strong accessibility, and direct alignment to the NC "early Islamic civilization" unit. Marked down slightly because cognitive demand leans towards matching/recall rather than deeper enquiry, and the standout astrolabe activity sits beside lighter quiz content.
subject: History
name: House of Wisdom: Baghdad c.900

rationale: A genuinely strong KS2 French listening tool: gist-and-detail comprehension across three contexts, semantically and phonetically plausible distractors, replay/slow/transcript scaffolds, and per-spot diagnostic feedback, all with excellent keyboard, touch and ARIA accessibility and accurate French mapped well to the KS2 MFL programme of study. Held off top marks because audio depends on browser SpeechSynthesis voice quality/availability and the "scene" is abstract numbered slots rather than a depicted picture, weakening the visual comprehension claim.
subject: Modern Foreign Languages
name: Listen and Show

rationale: A polished, accessible KS1 RE matching game with genuine dual-input interaction (pointer drag plus full keyboard pick-up/drop), TTS read-aloud, ARIA-labelled wheel, and diagnostic feedback that distinguishes wrong-kind from wrong-festival errors. Festival content is age-appropriate and factually sound across Christianity, Islam, Judaism and Hinduism, mapping cleanly to the KS1 RE focus on festivals and special times.
subject: Religious Education
name: Festival Calendar Wheel

rationale: Strong KS2 RE coverage of six world faiths across pilgrimage and festival, with age-appropriate questions, retry-on-error feedback, gated reveal of meaning and practice, TTS support and a thoughtful synthesis prompt; well-built, accessible (keyboard/ARIA markers, live regions) and self-contained. Held back from top marks because the quiz layer is recall-only single-MCQ with the correct distractor never penalised beyond a retry, and the map shapes are crude, limiting deeper conceptual challenge.
subject: Religious Education
name: Journey and Festival Map

rationale: Three genuinely interactive, well-built activities (drag/keyboard labelling, a staged pollination-to-seed animation, and a prediction-then-test water slider) with strong KS2 Plants coverage, clean accessibility, and embedded retrieval-style quizzes. Held back by a scientific misconception in the water activity, which teaches that more water in the jar makes water rise higher up the stem (conflating jar level with transpiration-driven transport).
fixIt: Water activity reinforces a misconception: amount of water in the jar does not determine how high water rises in the stem. Recast the variable as transpiration/heat/light or simply show the rising dye effect over time, and fix the prediction quiz so the correct answer is not "more water means it rises higher".
subject: Science
name: Inside a Flower

rationale: A polished, accurate tool whose four hand-written scenes show genuine craft: the omniscient versions correctly model dramatic irony and authorial intrusion, and the consistent colour-coded highlighting (pronouns/thoughts/distance) maps perfectly onto KS3 narrative viewpoint study. It sits at the top of tier B because the interaction is essentially curated reading, with no task that asks students to apply, predict or produce, capping its cognitive demand.
subject: English
name: Narrative Perspective Shifter

rationale: A genuinely strong KS3 figurative-language tool: drag-or-click pairing of tenor and vehicle yields rich, accurate ground/effect/literary-connection analysis using Richards' terminology, with a metaphor/simile toggle, a thoughtful Theory tab and a "Create Your Own" scaffold. It falls short of tier A because the animated canvas is decorative rather than conceptually meaningful, several anger/knowledge pairings hit a "no analysis yet" dead-end (plus a duplicate hope-stone key), and the custom-metaphor gallery neither persists nor gives the writer any feedback.
subject: English
name: Metaphor & Simile Machine

rationale: A polished, well-pitched KS4 tool that annotates four canonical persuasive speeches with device-specific "why it works here" analysis, an Aristotelian ethos/pathos/logos taxonomy, live statistics and a hidden-highlight "Device Spotter" challenge, all robust and responsive. It falls short of top marks because the challenge mode is really an answer-reveal with no wrong-answer feedback, there is no student-authored analysis, and a few labels are inaccurate ("We shall go on to the end" tagged imperative when it is declarative; "subjugated and starving" called alliteration when it is sibilance).
subject: English
name: Rhetoric Autopsy

rationale: A polished, age-appropriate EYFS pattern-continuation game with a sensible AB to AABB difficulty ramp, genuine tap-to-place interaction, immediate feedback and distractor beads that probe misconceptions. It is held back from top marks by only ever asking "what comes next" (never internal gap-fill or error-spotting, and no actual pattern creation despite the title) and by div-based choice buttons with no keyboard or ARIA support.
subject: Maths
name: Pattern Maker

rationale: A genuinely interactive, mathematically accurate transformations tool: draggable vertices, sliders, animation, live coordinate table and a change-highlighting 2x2 matrix, plus a misconception-aware GCSE guide (congruence vs similarity, area scale factor squared, negative SF, matrix only valid about the origin). Pitched slightly above pure KS3 (matrices, negative scale factors) but this gives useful stretch and the core covers the KS3 NC well; clean, responsive and accessibly built with no obvious bugs.
subject: Maths
name: Geometric Transformations Explorer

rationale: A polished, genuinely interactive visualiser linking the algebra to the graphical intersection, with live elimination and substitution working, all three solution types covered, and strong accessibility (aria-live banner, updating canvas description). It is pitched at GCSE/A-Level despite being catalogued as KS3, and the "simplify and solve" steps jump to the answer rather than showing the single-variable algebra, with exact fractional solutions shown as rounded decimals.
fixIt: Alignment: simultaneous equations is a KS4/GCSE topic, but the catalogue tags it KS3 while the app itself says GCSE/A-Level. Either re-tag it KS4 in the catalogue/menus or add a clearly scaffolded KS3-appropriate entry mode; ensure the stated key stage and the app's own framing match.
subject: Maths
name: Simultaneous Equations Visualiser

rationale: A well-constructed A-Level indefinite integration tool whose family-of-curves graph genuinely visualises the +C concept, paired with accurate step-by-step worked examples and a practice engine with immediate feedback. It falls just short of tier A because the answer checker relies on exact string matching against a hand-listed array, so mathematically valid equivalents (decimals, reordered terms, unlisted spacing) are marked wrong.
subject: Maths
name: Indefinite Integration

rationale: A polished, scientifically accurate enzyme-kinetics simulator with four live parameters, correct Michaelis-Menten/inhibitor maths, a Lineweaver-Burk toggle and a genuinely illustrative binding/denaturation animation. The content (Vmax, Km, Lineweaver-Burk, inhibition formalism) is really A-level Biology rather than KS4, so it overshoots the stated key stage even though everything it teaches is correct.
subject: Science
name: Enzyme Kinetics Simulator

rationale: A polished, accessible canvas simulator that animates the canonical GCSE crack-cave-arch-stack-stump sequence on a discordant coastline with genuine controls (editable rock bands, wave power, erosion focus) and accurate UK examples. It demonstrates well but stops short of A-tier because it conflates the plan-view headland/bay model with the cross-section feature sequence, the erosion-type "focus" only nudges an abstract rate rather than showing a distinct process, and there is no questioning, prediction or assessment to deepen cognitive demand.
subject: Geography
name: Coastal Erosion Simulator

rationale: A substantive, historically accurate KS3 tool on the change/continuity second-order concept with two well-researched period datasets, four genuine modes, per-aspect change meters, and an excellent misconception-aware Key Concepts panel. Held back from tier A because the Compare mode contrasts rate-of-change *ratings* rather than actual life conditions, which can mislabel genuine differences as "continuity", and the promised animated transitions are minimal.
subject: History
name: Change & Continuity Analyser

rationale: A polished, accurate KS4 source-enquiry tool with four well-researched topics, real drag-drop manipulation, a star-driven reliability model, and an OPCVL explainer; pitched correctly for GCSE history. It falls short of excellence because the weighting model conflates reliability with evidential weight (the very nuance its own info panel cautions against) and the numeric "verdict" risks reinforcing the misconception that historical judgement is a matter of tallying weights.
subject: History
name: Evidence Weighing Scales

rationale: A polished, genuinely useful KS3 French tool that teaches noun gender through ending patterns with filterable cards, a distribution chart, an exceptions panel, and a quiz with difficulty tiers, streaks and rule-based feedback that surfaces misconceptions (l' elision, livre vs livre). Held back from top tier by a couple of lexical/accuracy points and chart percentages that reflect the curated sample rather than real frequency.
subject: Modern Foreign Languages
name: Gender & Article Patterns

rationale: An accurate, nuanced node-link map of nine afterlife concepts with genuine two-concept comparison (similarities/differences/theological points across 16 curated pairings), well matched to the KS4 RE skill of comparing and contrasting diverse beliefs. Held back from top tier by being a read-only explorer with no retrieval, self-testing or written-response element, and by surveying six faiths in breadth where GCSE specs go deep on two.
subject: Religious Education
name: Afterlife Beliefs Explorer

rationale: A faithful, well-populated implementation of the Zones of Regulation framework with five genuinely distinct modes (zones, scenarios, journey plotting, body signals, adult guidance), accurate non-judgemental SEL content, and strong RSHE/PSHE "mental wellbeing" alignment for KS1-2. Held off top marks by softness in the scenario logic (green always short-circuits to praise even when inappropriate, e.g. fire alarm), a "My Journey" tracker that has no persistence, and clickable divs lacking keyboard/ARIA support.
subject: Behaviour & Pastoral
name: Zones of Regulation

rationale: A well-built branching decision tree with four realistic, age-appropriate conflict scenarios, RAG-rated outcomes, a full path review and reflective commentary that surfaces misconceptions (favouring the louder voice, retaliation, going behind backs). It maps soundly onto KS3 PSHE/RSHE relationships content; the main ceiling is that outcomes are predetermined branches rather than open input, and the sidebar skills are not directly tied to the path taken.
subject: Behaviour & Pastoral
name: Conflict Resolution Flowchart

rationale: A polished, multi-mode PSHE/pastoral tool (learn pairs, free-text reframing practice, a reverse-scored mindset quiz, an interactive neuroplasticity canvas, and a Dweck-grounded research panel) with genuine interaction and misconception-aware content. Held off top marks because the practice scorer is a shallow, gameable substring matcher and several neuroscience claims (myelin, mirror neurons) are popular-science overstatements rather than precise.
subject: Behaviour & Pastoral
name: Growth Mindset Reframer

rationale: A polished, misconception-aware anti-bullying training app with three role perspectives, scored choices, meaningful immediate feedback, a best-response review, takeaways and real support signposting; fit for PSHE/pastoral purpose. Falls short of excellent because the "branching" is actually a scored linear path (later stages ignore earlier choices) and the correct answer is always the first option, which is predictable and gameable.
subject: Behaviour & Pastoral
name: Bullying Scenario Navigator

rationale: A genuinely useful, pedagogically literate CPD tool that turns teacher-entered facts/terms into six evidence-based retrieval activities (flashcards, fill-gap, MCQ, match-up, sequencing, brain dump) with interleaving, scoring, fullscreen, topic bank and print export; the weak link is the crude keyword-blanking and distractor algorithm, which often yields ambiguous gaps or implausible MCQ options that a teacher must heavily curate before classroom use.
fixIt: Alignment (3): the auto-question generation is too heuristic to be reliably classroom-ready. Let teachers explicitly mark the answer word(s) per fact (e.g. wrap in asterisks) rather than guessing via capitalisation/length, accept multiple acceptable answers per gap, and flag when sequencing relies on input order (currently it silently assumes entry order is the correct order). Improve MCQ distractors to be same-category (e.g. other dates for a date gap) instead of random keywords padded with "None of the above".
subject: CPD & Staff Tools
name: Retrieval Quiz Generator

rationale: A polished, genuinely useful classroom pacing tool: editable draggable segments, four well-judged templates, live allocation/over-run warnings, plan export and a robust countdown timer with dual progress tracking and phase-aware visuals, plus Rosenshine-grounded teaching tips. Clean code with HTML escaping and duration clamping; falls short of excellence mainly on accessibility (drag-and-drop has no keyboard alternative, audible alerts are visual-only) rather than function.
subject: CPD & Staff Tools
name: Lesson Plan Timer

rationale: A genuinely useful, research-informed CPD planning tool: an editable strategy x learner-group matrix with context-aware suggestion banks across 13 subjects, plus solid expandable guidance on ZPD, EEF adaptive teaching, Bloom's and UDL. Clean, responsive and well-built, held back from top tier by a fixed ability-group framing that the EEF itself cautions against (only partly mitigated by the guidance text), no save/persistence, and limited accessibility on the contenteditable cells.
subject: CPD & Staff Tools
name: Differentiation Planner

rationale: A genuinely useful, well-built CPD utility: subject-specific WWW/EBI/Next Steps phrase banks are specific and actionable, grounded in EEF and growth-mindset principles, with criteria filtering, sentence starters, live preview, copy and history all working cleanly. It falls short of excellent because the Key Stage selector is purely cosmetic (KS3/KS4/KS5 yield identical phrases) and the tool composes rather than evaluates feedback.
subject: CPD & Staff Tools
name: Marking Feedback Builder

rationale: A theoretically accurate, well-presented CPD self-audit against cognitive load theory, with genuine live feedback (gauge, category bars, targeted suggestions, exportable report) and strong accessibility. It remains a weighted self-report checklist rather than an analysis of a real lesson, lacks persistence, and its generic data-acknowledgement modal copy is mismatched to the tool, holding it just below excellent.
subject: CPD & Staff Tools
name: Cognitive Load Analyser

rationale: A well-grounded CPD planning tool (ZPD, Gradual Release, metacognition with proper citations) that lets teachers map scaffold intensity across a sequence, add notes, load subject presets and animate the fade, then print a summary. Solidly useful and accurate, but the fade slider maths is impressionistic rather than precise and there is no persistence, so a planned sequence is lost on refresh, keeping it short of exceptional.
subject: CPD & Staff Tools
name: Scaffolding & Fading Planner

rationale: A well-built, professionally framed disadvantaged-gap analysis tool for school leaders: six real DfE measures (Attainment 8, Progress 8, grade thresholds, EBacc), four linked Chart.js views, editable data with live recalculation, a generated narrative, and accurate EEF/Pupil Premium context. Solid rather than exceptional: four small static squares of figures across years risk over-reading noise as trend, and the gap-direction classification is coarse.
subject: Data & Analytics
name: Attainment Gap Visualiser

rationale: A polished, robust staff-training tool that teaches attendance pattern recognition well: interactive heatmap with click-to-cycle, compare mode, day-of-week/session charts, and pattern detection all wired to accurate UK PA framing (90% threshold, 19+ days, intervention triggers). Held off top tier because the AM/PM session and matrix data are partly fabricated via index arithmetic rather than derived, making the "Morning vs Afternoon" insight illustrative-only and potentially misleading if read as real analysis.
subject: Data & Analytics
name: Attendance Pattern Visualiser

rationale: A well-built, age-appropriate EYFS emotion-literacy tool: six clearly differentiated SVG faces with part-by-part breakdowns (eyes, mouth, eyebrows) and plain-language descriptions that teach how facial features combine to convey feeling, mapping soundly onto EYFS PSED "Self-Regulation" and Expressive Arts/drama. It is genuinely interactive with immediate visual and textual feedback, though cognitive demand stays at recognition/labelling rather than discrimination or production, and the celebratory confetti fires on every emotion pick (even "sad"/"angry"), which slightly muddies the emotional message.
subject: Drama
name: Emotion Masks

rationale: A genuinely useful, well-built KS2 playscript composer: pupils add dialogue, stage directions and scene breaks and see them reformatted live into script layout, with genre templates, character autocomplete, print and download. It teaches script conventions authentically and aligns well with KS2 English, but offers no guidance/feedback on writing quality and no work persistence, keeping it short of the top tier.
subject: Drama
name: Script Writer

rationale: A genuinely useful five-stage devising scaffold (stimulus, mind-map, structure, techniques, reflection) with real drag-and-drop manipulation, accurate technique definitions and evaluative reflection prompts well pitched at KS3 Drama. Held back from top tier by feedback that reflects state rather than judging quality, reliance on native prompt/confirm dialogues, and no persistence so a refresh loses all work.
subject: Drama
name: Devising Toolkit

rationale: A well-curated, factually accurate side-by-side comparison of nine theatre practitioners and companies (Stanislavski, Brecht, Artaud, Grotowski, Berkoff, Frantic Assembly, DV8, Complicite) that maps precisely onto A-Level Drama and Theatre specifications, with persistent study notes. Its weakness is interactivity: it functions as a reference lookup rather than a tool that demands active thinking, and the "Key Differences" panel is templated string-stitching rather than genuine analytical synthesis.
fixIt: Interactivity is the soft spot: it only retrieves and displays pre-written facts. Add active-recall elements (e.g. hide one practitioner's attributes and ask the student to predict them, or a "which practitioner would stage this scene?" challenge with feedback) so students manipulate and apply knowledge rather than just reading curated cards.
subject: Drama
name: Practitioner Comparison Tool

rationale: A well-structured three-mode tool (Learn, Sort, Write) that genuinely teaches KS2 paragraph cohesion with topic/detail/concluding sentences, immediate feedback in the sort task, and a live-preview writing scaffold. It stops short of excellence because the sort check demands one rigid ordering of detail sentences (marking valid alternative orders wrong) and the Write mode gives no quality feedback, only word counts.
subject: English
name: Paragraph Planner

rationale: A genuinely intellectual KS5 tool: three canonical passages read through five critical lenses with accurate, discipline-literate phrase annotations, correct theorists, and a side-by-side compare mode that powerfully demonstrates how theory reshapes meaning. It is exploratory rather than productive, however: all readings are pre-written, with no student input or formative feedback on their own critical thinking, which caps pedagogy and interactivity.
subject: English
name: Literary Theory Lens

rationale: A polished, age-appropriate EYFS seasons sorter with genuine tap interaction, sensible cross-season distractors that create real discrimination demand, and immediate correct/wrong feedback. It maps well onto Understanding the World, but stays a single mechanic with no scaffolded progression, thin accessibility (no keyboard sort, no audio for pre-readers), and no teaching on wrong answers.
subject: Science
name: Seasons Wheel

rationale: Accurate, curriculum-perfect coverage of KS2 map skills (4-figure grid references, 8-point compass, OS-style map, distance to scale) with genuinely misconception-aware feedback that injects and corrects the classic easting/northing reversal. Held back by multiple-choice answering rather than direct map manipulation, and a purely decorative compass with Q&A-only interaction.
subject: Geography
name: Map Skills

rationale: A clean, accurate KS2 biomes tool with genuine interactivity: a biome selector, read-aloud, and an 8-question quiz whose clues teach adaptation reasoning and give immediate, named, diagnostic feedback. Pitched well (UK temperate forest anchors it to pupils' own context) with strong accessibility, though "biomes" terminology leans slightly KS3 and the quiz is one fixed run rather than adaptive.
subject: Geography
name: Biomes Explorer

rationale: A genuinely useful A-Level Geography modeller: four adjustable fluxes plus four named scenarios drive live, colour-coded readings for net flux, atmospheric CO2, ppm and temperature anomaly, mapping cleanly onto the carbon cycle / human-impact spec. Held back from the top tier by a simplistic linear temperature proxy, unconstrained stores that can drift to implausible values, dead click affordances on stores/flux arrows, and a copy-paste demo modal that wrongly references "student records, names, grades, attendance".
subject: Geography
name: Carbon Cycle Modeller

rationale: A well-built KS1 MFL colours app with accurate vocabulary, pronunciation guides and locale-correct speech synthesis across French, Spanish and German, plus a genuine listen-then-choose recall task, strong accessibility (ARIA roles, keyboard support, screen-reader status) and robust Pointer Events painting. It falls short of excellent because the "Check my colour" task only verifies the currently selected swatch, not the colour actually painted on the canvas, so the painting and the assessment are loosely coupled.
subject: Modern Foreign Languages
name: Colours Around the World

rationale: A well-pitched EYFS RE explorer covering six faiths' places of worship with accurate, respectful terminology, text-to-speech for non-readers, a sensible explore-then-match progression with first-try tracking, and strong ARIA accessibility. It maps cleanly onto EYFS Understanding the World (People, Culture and Communities) and Agreed-Syllabus RE; the recognition-level matching and the out-of-place fabricated-child-data modal hold it just short of excellent.
subject: Religious Education
name: Special Places

rationale: A well-built KS2 RE app covering six religions with accurate practices, named prayers, reflection prompts, a clue-to-religion matching quiz with feedback, and a working guided breathing meditation with optional speech synthesis; content is age-appropriate and maps cleanly onto a non-statutory locally-agreed RE syllabus for KS2. It falls short of excellence because the quiz tests surface recall (icon/clue to label) rather than deeper conceptual understanding, and the demo-data modal text about fabricated child records is irrelevant boilerplate for this content type.
subject: Religious Education
name: Prayer and Meditation

rationale: A genuinely substantial five-tab Drama studio with a coherent knowledge-to-application pathway, accurate set-text content (Medea, Headlong 1984, Woyzeck, The Machinal) and properly scored interactive script analysis plus a shuffled retrieval quiz with banded feedback. It falls short of an A because two of its tabs (Performance Application and Exam Practice) accept written responses with no marking or feedback, the option/card interactions are click-only (limited keyboard accessibility), and the repeated "Woyzeck through Brecht" framing risks a chronology misconception.
subject: Drama
name: Mastery Studio

rationale: A genuinely well-designed KS3 multi-topic problem-solving app whose standout feature, the "What Maths Do I Need?" topic-identification phase, directly serves its stated purpose of teaching pupils to recognise which mathematics a problem demands, backed by scaffolded steps, flexible answer-matching, a scratchpad and full worked solutions across three accurately pitched difficulty tiers. It falls short of A because feedback on wrong answers is generic rather than misconception-diagnostic, the topic-identification step carries no consequence (you proceed regardless), and a few accept arrays are pedagogically loose (e.g. accepting an integer for a non-integer crossover in the phone-contract problem).
subject: Maths
name: Cross-Topic Challenges

rationale: A well-structured A-Level modelling workbook that follows the proper modelling cycle (formulate, solve, interpret/evaluate) across 18 accurate, key-stage-appropriate problems with strong attention to assumptions and limitations. It is held back by a weak feedback engine: the "Check" buttons for equations, working steps and the final answer do not validate input, they simply reveal the expected answer for self-comparison, so most interactivity is reveal-the-solution rather than genuine immediate marking.
fixIt: Make checkStep and the equation/final-answer checks actually validate input (normalised string/numeric comparison or accepted-answer arrays) and return correct/incorrect feedback, rather than always displaying the expected answer regardless of what the student typed.
subject: Maths
name: Mathematical Modelling

rationale: A well-built KS1 spelling app whose standout feature is the animated worked rule (doubling, dropping the silent e, adding -es) that makes the orthographic reasoning visible rather than just marking right/wrong, with distractor suffixes forcing genuine choice, misconception-aware hints, and solid keyboard/pointer accessibility. Held back from top tier by a thin bank of only 8 fixed words (limited rule coverage and no varied repetition for mastery) and minor accuracy gaps: it labels -ing/-ed as "doing word ending" loosely and the only "just add -ed" example (jump) sits alongside no example contrasting the "drop e + ed" or "double + ed" cases, narrowing the past-tense rule picture.
subject: English
name: Suffix Spinner

rationale: A well-built EYFS Understanding the World activity with genuine tool-to-target matching, animated SVG feedback, and notably misconception-aware mismatch messages (e.g. watering a weed helps it grow) that push children to think about what each living thing actually needs. Solid keyboard/ARIA support and clean child-friendly design keep it just short of excellent; the cognitive demand stays low (correct-match-only with no real challenge or variation), which is appropriate for the age but caps it at tier B.
subject: Science
name: Look After the Garden

rationale: A thoughtful KS3 enquiry that genuinely teaches continuity/change as a second-order concept via push/pull analysis, chronologically gated waves, and misconception-aware reasoning challenges with targeted feedback, all with strong keyboard and ARIA accessibility. Held back from tier A by a thin four-wave sample for a "2,000-year" claim (Vikings/Normans/Jewish/Irish migrations absent or non-interactive), a purely decorative non-geographic map, and a boilerplate demo modal referencing "grades and attendance" that does not fit this app.
subject: History
name: Migration to Britain: The Long View

rationale: A strong, genuinely interactive canvas-based OS map tool covering grid references, contours, scale/distance, live relief cross-sections and a GIS enquiry cycle, all with specific, misconception-aware feedback and real manipulation (draggable plus keyboard markers, live readouts). Pitched well for KS3 geographical skills, but undermined by a legend-versus-question contradiction on the contour interval (10 m vs 20 m) and faint, stippled contour rendering.
fixIt: Technical: fix the contour interval contradiction (legend says 10 m, the contour question says 20 m) and repair traceContour, which draws zero-length segments (points a and b are identical) so contours appear as faint dots rather than continuous lines.
subject: Geography
name: OS Map Detective

rationale: A polished, respectful KS1 RE matching game that accurately pairs kind teachings with four religious leaders, with supportive retry-and-clue feedback, story reveals, progress pips and a replayable summary. Strong build and pitch; cognitive demand is modest recognition-matching and the generic "no real child" demo-data modal is contextually wrong for an app about real religious figures.
subject: Religious Education
name: Who Taught This?
