rationale: A polished KS3 MFL intercultural app with a sound predict-then-reveal loop, immediate banded feedback, UK-vs-target comparison panels, and integrated target-language vocabulary across three languages; accurate (if necessarily simplified) cultural content and strong accessibility put it solidly in the upper-B band, just short of A because some scale items lean on dated stereotypes (Wednesday off, siesta) and the predictions are at times artificial.
subject: Modern Foreign Languages
name: Culture Compare

rationale: A polished, robust KS2 sculpture app with genuine canvas building, method-specific textures, multi-mode rotation and misconception-aware kiln-firing feedback (thin walls crack, thick walls trap air), well matched to NC clay/sculpture work. It falls short of top marks because the "3D form" is a 2.5D illusion: the pot silhouette does not actually turn in depth as you rotate, so the central claim of seeing how a 3D form is constructed is somewhat overstated.
subject: Art and Design
name: Clay Studio 3D

rationale: A well-built sight-size tool with genuine draggable plumb line and two-point angle stick over a ground-truth still life, giving specific, misconception-aware feedback with directional hints and solid keyboard/ARIA support. Held back by a single fixed scenario with no regeneration, a demanding angle tolerance that may frustrate via keyboard/touch, and a Task 3 that reduces real measuring to a multiple-choice dropdown.
subject: Art and Design
name: Sight-Size Drawing

rationale: A polished, accessible drag-match app that genuinely teaches GCSE Art contextual understanding (AO1) by linking artwork to movement, social context and creative career, with category-locked zones, distractors, targeted feedback and coursework annotation tips. Strong technically (pointer-event drag, full keyboard path, ARIA live regions) though the career-to-movement pairings are somewhat arbitrary and one feedback announce string is garbled.
subject: Art and Design
name: Context Connector (Art KS4)

rationale: A genuinely strong KS2 performing tool: real-time autocorrelation microphone tuner with cents needle plus flat/sharp/wrong-note coaching, fingering charts and a positional-feedback Riff Builder make it highly interactive and well pitched for whole-class recorder teaching. Marked down on alignment/accuracy because high C5 is given the wrong fingering (thumb + first two fingers, identical to A4) rather than the correct thumb + second-finger-only, which risks teaching a misconception in an app whose whole premise is fingering accuracy.
fixIt: Fix the C5 fingering in the NOTES table: high C on a baroque descant recorder is thumb (back) plus the second top-hand finger only, i.e. holes:[1,0,1,0,0,0,0,0], not [1,1,1,0,0,0,0,0] which duplicates A4 and shows pupils the wrong grip.
subject: Music
name: Recorder Trainer

rationale: A genuinely strong, self-contained KS3 theory drill: real SVG staff renderer, browser-synthesised audio that works offline, five well-pitched modes, sensible adaptive levelling and an excellent Scale and Chord Builder that spells, lights and plays patterns, with solid accessibility. Alignment is held back because the notation rendering is deliberately approximate: the scales drill draws fake spellings (no key-signature sharps, only a flattened 3rd for minors), the interval staff can be enharmonically misspelled, and the Builder spells everything with sharps, so flat keys read incorrectly for a tool whose core purpose is accurate staff notation.
fixIt: Alignment (3): make the rendered notation musically correct. In genScales, draw the actual scale degrees with the right key-signature accidentals (b3/b6/b7 for minors, correct sharps/flats for the chosen root) instead of plain diatonic steps with one flat; in genIntervals, spell the upper note from the lower note plus the interval's correct letter distance rather than clamping an accidental to plus or minus one; and in the Builder, spell flat-key roots and degrees with flats (context-aware enharmonics) rather than the sharp-only NOTE_NAMES_SHARP table.
subject: Music
name: Theory Trainer

rationale: A well-built, accurate KS5 Music tool with three genuinely interactive tabs (context, browser-synthesised comparative listening, drag/keyboard feature sorting) and strong targeted, misconception-aware feedback plus solid accessibility. It falls short of top tier because the audio engine only varies dynamics, so for Classical and Romantic the "broken convention" the feedback describes (Baroque counterpoint, square phrasing) is not actually audible, and the Feature Detective uses only 3 of the 4 periods.
subject: Music
name: Style Period Deep Dive

rationale: A well-pitched, accessible EYFS discovery activity that genuinely supports the "technology in the everyday world" strand with misconception-aware "how you know" cues, progress chips, and strong teacher prompts. Interactivity is solid but modest: it is tap-to-reveal with no distractor non-technology items to discriminate against and no audio despite the "hear what it does" framing.
fixIt: Add a few non-technology distractors (e.g. cushion, plant, book) into each scene that give gentle "that one does not use technology" feedback when tapped, turning passive reveal into real discrimination; and deliver the device descriptions via the Web Speech API so non-readers actually "hear" what each device does as promised.
subject: Computing
name: Spot the Technology

rationale: A polished, misconception-aware EYFS sequencing activity: children order five step cards and a literal robot narrates each action, with contextual error feedback pinpointing the first wrong box, plus genuine drag-and-drop and full keyboard/ARIA accessibility. It falls just short of excellent because the sandwich animation always builds the layers in correct stack positions even when the robot's speech says the step went wrong, so the visuals do not actually mirror the "hilariously wrong" outcomes that are the activity's whole point.
subject: Computing
name: Make a Jam Sandwich Robot

rationale: A well-pitched EYFS whole-class online-safety activity with a clear "stop and ask a trusted grown-up" purpose, distinct misconception-aware scenes (prize pop-up, unknown button, personal-data request, screen-time break) and a sensible class-voting mechanic with immediate, child-friendly feedback. Technically robust and accessible (keyboard, ARIA live region, reduced-motion, focus management), though the scenes are scripted and short, the boilerplate "grades/attendance" demo modal text is mismatched to this app, and the home-button scene's binary tap/grown framing is slightly muddled.
subject: Computing
name: Ask a Grown-Up (EYFS Online Safety)

rationale: A well-built predict-then-check app that maps precisely onto the KS1 Computing aim of using logical reasoning to predict simple-program behaviour, with differentiated, misconception-aware feedback and a clean, keyboard-accessible animated reveal. Its main limitation is that the robot's facing direction (essential for predicting turns) is conveyed only by a "faint" emoji rotation, weakening the very cue young pupils need.
subject: Computing
name: Predict the Path

rationale: A polished, accessible EYFS sequencing activity with clear purpose (food hygiene, ordering, where fruit comes from), genuine choice-based manipulation, differentiated order-aware feedback, hygiene modals, and robust focus management. Cognitive demand stays modest (one repeatable 4-step sequence, two distractors) and the "where food comes from" content is read-only rather than interactive, which caps it just short of excellent.
subject: Design and Technology
name: Wash, Chop, Mix

rationale: A genuinely interactive, age-appropriate EYFS app pairing a responsive heart/breathing avatar (effort accumulates, animation speeds, sweat/mouth change, banded feedback and rotating talk prompts) with a robust drag-or-tap healthy-day sorter that includes keyboard access, accessible labels and prefers-reduced-motion handling; it maps well to EYFS PE/Health and Self-Care and supports rich class talk. It falls short of excellence because the science is loosely modelled (resting BPM and the "racing" bands are illustrative rather than precise) and the cognitive demand is appropriately light, so it is a strong supporting tool rather than a standout.
subject: Physical Education
name: My Busy Body

rationale: A polished, projector-ready KS1 PE cueing tool that scaffolds footwork drills with colour-coded L/R feet, directional arrows, a next-step ghost and a live-retiming speed slider, mapping cleanly onto the KS1 NC emphasis on agility and coordination. It is technically robust with strong keyboard, pointer and ARIA support, but it remains a movement-cueing aid rather than a tool with deep cognitive demand or misconception-handling, which keeps it out of the top tier.
subject: Physical Education
name: Agility Ladder Lab

rationale:
A clean, accessible three-step KS4 PE tool that genuinely maps barriers to engagement strategies for socio-cultural participation gaps, with live chart and scoreboard feedback well-pitched to GCSE PE specifications. Cognitive demand is slightly softened because matching strategies are pre-highlighted, doing some of the diagnostic reasoning for the pupil rather than requiring them to justify the link.</parameter>
<parameter name="fixIt">
subject: Physical Education
name: Sport and Society Explorer

rationale: A well-built KS1 Citizenship app with a clear purpose (meet community helpers, then design gratitude messages), genuine tap-and-drag interactivity, conditional four-state feedback, a progress trail, and strong keyboard/ARIA accessibility. It falls short of excellent because the "gratitude marking" is shallow keyword presence-checking (any order passes, no sentence modelling) and the demo-modal boilerplate about "grades and attendance figures" is mismatched to a young-children app.
subject: Citizenship
name: Who Helps Us?

rationale: A polished, well-structured KS1 Citizenship app: three right/wrong scenarios drive a vote-watch-reflect-then-kinder-choice cycle with clear feeling-focused discussion prompts, animated SVG consequences, a class-vote tally, and genuinely strong accessibility (aria-live, focus management, focus-visible). It pitches accurately to the PSHE/Citizenship "simple choices between right and wrong" strand; held off top tier because the vote tally is decorative (the teacher manually triggers the outcome rather than the winning vote driving it) and the boilerplate demo-data modal mis-describes the app as showing fabricated grades/attendance records that do not exist here.
subject: Citizenship
name: What Happens Next?

rationale: A polished, genuinely interactive (drag, tap and full keyboard) and accessible KS2 Citizenship app whose well-written mini-stories and diversity meters reinforce community, respect and inclusion; the matching task itself is deterministic and low-demand, so the learning is delivered largely through reading rather than pupil reasoning.
fixIt: Pedagogy: each person has one obvious correct slot, so it's a closed matching exercise. Add some cognitive demand: e.g. plausible places where two people could fit and pupils must justify a choice, a sorting/classifying task on faith/culture/job, or reflective prompts (predict a story, identify what each person uniquely contributes) so children reason about diversity rather than just unlocking pre-written text.
subject: Citizenship
name: Our Diverse Community

rationale: An accurate, well-sequenced KS3 Citizenship walkthrough of a Bill's passage with misconception-aware MCQs, animated division votes, amendment notes and an SVG diagram with a moving token; alignment to the legislative process is excellent. Interactivity is the weak point: it is essentially a linear quiz with decorative votes that always pass, so pupil choices never genuinely branch or change the outcome.
subject: Citizenship
name: How Parliament Works

rationale: A genuinely useful, well-structured CPD tool whose four-stage flow (classify framing, rewrite negatives, co-construct a five-norm charter, plan recognition) is grounded in sound behaviour-management practice and gives meaningful, targeted feedback at each step. It falls short of excellence because the audit and rewrite checks are scripted/heuristic rather than adaptive, and the charter is built from a fixed exemplar bank rather than the teacher's own audited language, limiting depth.
subject: CPD & Staff Tools
name: Expectations and Norms Builder

rationale: A genuinely useful three-stage CPD tool with live SMART-goal analysis, a confidence-trend SVG chart fed by practice logs, status logic, and a reflection-depth scorer, all soundly mapped to the DfE Teachers' Standards with strong ARIA support. It falls short of excellent because scoring is gameable keyword matching and, critically, there is no persistence, so a journal designed to span the appraisal cycle is wiped on refresh.
subject: CPD & Staff Tools
name: CPD Goal and Reflection Journal

rationale: A polished, professionally useful CPD/data tool that correctly models the DfE Basics combined English-and-maths measures (9-4 standard, 9-5 strong as a subset) with live disadvantage/SEND splits, national-gap commentary, and small-number cautions, all delivered through genuinely manipulable sliders/tabs with diagnostic feedback and a target challenge. Accurate and accessible, with only minor blemishes (redundant challenge-logic computation, a cryptic "Nat" benchmark label) keeping it just short of exceptional.
subject: Data & Analytics
name: Basics Pass-Rate Tracker

rationale: A genuinely useful CPD/data-literacy tool that correctly models within-pupil residuals and aggregates them per subject to control for cohort strength, with a self-check quiz, keyboard navigation and solid ARIA labelling. It explicitly teaches the misconception it targets (a high raw grade can still be a relative concern), though it stops short of nuance like statistical significance, small-n caveats, or the difference between a residual and true value-added against prior attainment.
subject: Data & Analytics
name: Subject Residual Heatmap

rationale: A genuinely useful school-leadership planning tool: it operationalises an EEF-style cost-per-month-of-progress lens with evidence discounting, live readouts, a sorted value ranking and a clear budget-allocation verdict, all mathematically sound and safeguarded with the demo-data modal. Falls just short of A because added interventions cannot be edited or removed and nothing persists, and the value bands are fixed thresholds rather than configurable, slightly limiting real planning use.
subject: Data & Analytics
name: Intervention Cost-Benefit Planner

rationale: A well-built KS1 PSHE/pastoral tool with clear purpose: it grows a spoken class feelings word bank via tapped faces (TTS, tap counts, live word bank) then a coping-strategy matching game that sensibly draws from the wall, with kind explanatory feedback and good accessibility (aria-labels, focus-visible, aria-live). It pitches well onto KS1 emotional literacy, and the game honestly acknowledges there is no single right answer, though forcing one "best" coping card (e.g. jealous to water, calm to smile) is a slightly arbitrary simplification that a teacher must mediate.
subject: Behaviour & Pastoral
name: Feelings Word Wall

rationale: A focused, well-built P8/A8 aggregation tool whose double-weighting, /10 divisor, tenths-of-a-grade Progress 8 logic and -0.5 floor threshold are conceptually accurate and honestly caveated as simplified demo figures; live sliders, presets, scenario bands and a clear verdict give genuine immediate feedback. It loses the top mark because the scenario-band "boundary risk" model is a pedagogical invention rather than real statistics, A8 is shown only as a single fixed per-pupil score with no prior-attainment context, and the verdict text repeats rather than deepening the maths.
subject: Data & Analytics
name: Cohort Outcome Forecaster

rationale: A well-conceived KS1 Drama app that teaches the real skill of matching volume, speed and mood to meaning, with custom drag/keyboard SVG dials, dial-mapped TTS playback, and a scored matching game giving precise per-dial diagnostic feedback. Strong accessibility (slider roles, aria-valuetext, live regions, keyboard control) and graceful audio fallback land it solidly in tier B; the only soft spots are the loose pitch-as-emotion mapping and a TTS volume setting applied after speak() that may not take effect.
subject: Drama
name: Voice Dial

rationale: A thoughtfully engineered KS3 Drama critique tool whose layered feedback engine genuinely scaffolds "technique plus effect plus correct tag" responses that map well onto evaluating live and recorded theatre. Held back from A-tier because the abstract stick-figure animation cannot actually show the specific acting details (facial expression, gripping the door frame) the prompts ask pupils to observe, so the stimulus underdelivers on the critique it demands.
subject: Drama
name: Watch and Critique (KS3 Drama)

rationale: A genuinely interactive, misconception-aware KS3 globalisation tool: clicking a TNC animates supply and market flows across a world map, with colour-coded role nodes, benefit/drawback trade-off cards and targeted quiz feedback, all with solid keyboard and ARIA accessibility. Held back from tier A by a crudely blocky, not-to-scale map with approximate place anchors (e.g. Bangladesh overlapping India), a single-question quiz per company and a boilerplate demo-data modal that talks about grades and attendance irrelevant to this app.
subject: Geography
name: Globalisation Web

rationale: A focused, well-built KS2 chronology tool: proportionally scaled BC/AD bars make relative duration and "how long ago" concrete, with accurate, sensibly rounded dates for the named NC units (Egypt, Roman Britain, Vikings) and specific, year-anchored feedback on both placement and a read-off quiz. It falls just short of excellent because lanes are pre-assigned so pupils follow a prescribed order rather than genuinely discovering chronology, and the generic safeguarding modal text ("grades, attendance") is irrelevant boilerplate for a history app.
subject: History
name: The Big Picture Timeline

rationale: A polished, accessible EYFS carpet-time app with genuine spoken-greeting interactivity, correct inline-SVG flags, passport gamification and good keyboard/ARIA support, well pitched to EYFS Understanding the World and Communication and Language. Held back from top marks by low cognitive ceiling (tap-and-listen) and a content slip (Japan's "Crane" card uses a frog emoji, and the generic demo-data modal mentions irrelevant student grades/attendance).
subject: Modern Foreign Languages
name: Around the World Story Carpet

rationale: A genuinely thoughtful GCSE-style translation trainer that marks chunk by chunk, distinguishes tense/agreement/word-order/vocab points, awards banded feedback and supports bidirectional practice with TTS and good accessibility; held back from tier A by a tiny sentence bank (only 4 items per language, 2 per tier) and a crude stem-stripping match heuristic that can misjudge partial credit.
subject: Modern Foreign Languages
name: Translation Trainer (KS4 MFL)

rationale: A genuinely engaging KS4 ecology tool with excellent multi-modal interactivity (drag, tap, keyboard, random throw), clumped distributions that authentically teach why sample size affects reliability, error-aware feedback and a reveal-truth comparison. It is held back by a real scientific inconsistency in its core scaling maths: the 100m x 100m field shown as a 10x10 grid implies 10m cells, yet the help text claims 5m x 5m quadrats and uses a 400x multiplier that matches neither, undermining the population-estimation skill it sets out to teach.
fixIt: Resolve the area/quadrat/multiplier contradiction: pick one coherent geometry (e.g. 100m x 100m field, 5m x 5m quadrats needing a 20x20 grid, multiplier 400; or keep the 10x10 grid with 10m cells and use multiplier 100). Fix the intro line that says cells are "1 m by 1 m ... representing a 100 m²" and update the code comment so the displayed grid, stated quadrat size and FIELD_QUADRATS all agree.
subject: Science
name: Quadrat Sampler

rationale: A genuinely useful KS1 money manipulative with three purposeful modes, all eight UK coins (including correctly shaped heptagonal 20p/50p), a sensible difficulty ramp and accurate pounds/pence formatting, giving real practice in recognising coins and composing amounts. It is held back by technical rough edges: the click-to-remove handler closes over a stale index so removing a non-last coin can delete the wrong one, no keyboard/screen-reader access for a Reception/Year 1 audience, and a generic demo-data modal referencing "grades and attendance" that does not fit a coin game.
subject: Maths
name: Coin Counter

rationale: A genuinely interactive, well-pitched KS1 fractions tool with four real modes (split, shade, name, equivalence), live SVG manipulation, tap-to-shade and immediate meaningful feedback that maps accurately onto the England NC (halves, quarters, thirds, simple equivalence). It is held back by Split mode auto-rewarding any choice without addressing the central equal-vs-unequal-parts misconception, plus latent marking bugs and messy double monkey-patching in the JS.
subject: Maths
name: Fraction Explorers

rationale: A polished, well-built three-mode KS1 measurement tool with genuine drag-drop balance interaction and good NC alignment, but the headline "measure with a ruler" mode is pedagogically hollow: the draggable ruler's position is never used, so children match widths to multiple-choice options rather than actually reading a scale from zero.
fixIt: In Measure mode, make the ruler load-bearing: snap or read its offset so the child must align the ruler's zero with the object's start, then derive the answer (or only reveal options) from correct alignment, teaching the real misconception that you measure from 0 not from the end.
subject: Maths
name: Measurement Explorer

rationale: A genuinely useful KS2 FDP tool: a linked slider drives synchronised fraction/decimal/percentage values with bar and pie visuals, plus a tiered conversion challenge with scoring and a curated equivalences table that maps well to Year 5/6 expectations. It loses marks for accessibility (the slider is a div with pointer/mouse handlers only, no keyboard/ARIA role or focusability), the absence of any taught conversion method (it reveals answers but no "how"), and challenges that can present awkward repeating decimals such as 0.333333 to convert to a fraction, which is harsh at KS2.
fixIt: Technical/accessibility: make the slider keyboard-operable and screen-reader friendly. Replace the div-based track with a focusable control (tabindex, role="slider", aria-valuenow/min/max and arrow-key handling) or back it with a native range input, so it works without a mouse/touch and on assistive tech. Also cap challenge decimals to clean values (avoid displaying 0.333333 to convert to a fraction).
subject: Maths
name: Fractions, Decimals & Percentages

rationale: A genuinely interactive free-body-diagram and F=ma tool with live resultant-force vectors, animated motion, distance/velocity graphs, sensible presets and strong accessibility, well pitched for KS3 forces. Marred by flawed physics modelling: friction reduces the static net force rather than opposing actual motion (so the "Car Braking" preset never decelerates), gravity makes the object fall through the ground with no normal-force collision during simulation, the "slope" preset has no inclined-plane physics, and the info panel wrongly presents the normal force as gravity's Newton's-third-law reaction pair.
fixIt: Technical/physics: rework getNetForce so friction opposes the object's actual velocity each frame (capping at the value needed to bring v to zero) so braking truly decelerates; add ground-collision/normal-force handling during the falling simulation; either build real inclined-plane resolution for the "slope" preset or rename it; and correct the 3rd-law text so weight and normal force are not described as an action-reaction pair.
subject: Science
name: Forces & Motion Simulator

rationale: A well-built three-mode app (flip cards, drag-sort, contextual quiz with explanatory feedback) covering genuinely accurate and well-chosen false friends across French, Spanish and German, with strong "what it actually means" notes that target the core KS4 misconception. Marred by accents stripped from every foreign word, which is a real accuracy problem for an MFL tool and ironically turns some examples (exito, estrenido) into spelling traps.
fixIt: Alignment: restore correct diacritics throughout the DATA object (cassé, journée, éxito, estreñido, Küche, etc.); for an MFL app the accents are part of the language and their absence both misteaches spelling and undercuts the lesson on near-identical forms.
subject: Modern Foreign Languages
name: False Friends Explorer

rationale: A well-researched, misconception-aware comparative RE browser with accurate scripture, terminology and explicit respectful-comparison and emic/etic guidance, plus theme tabs, religion toggles and reveal-on-demand similarity/difference banners. Interactivity is real but shallow (curated content reveal rather than student construction), and the analysis prompts are display-only with no answering or feedback mechanism.
fixIt: Lift interactivity above a content browser: make the analysis prompts answerable (text boxes with model-answer reveals or self-check), or add a drag-to-match / "which religion said this?" quiz so students manipulate and get immediate meaningful feedback rather than only toggling visibility.
subject: Religious Education
name: Sacred Texts Comparator

rationale: A polished, content-rich RE explorer covering pilgrimage across six religions with a custom canvas world map, religion filters, detail panels, a strong side-by-side compare tool, and genuine accessibility work (keyboard site list, aria-live, pointer/touch support); content is accurate and well-pitched for KS3. It loses marks because interactivity is largely explore-and-reveal with no retrieval, checking of understanding, or responsive feedback, so it stays at exploratory rather than diagnostic depth.
subject: Religious Education
name: Pilgrimage Explorer

rationale: A clean, accurate attendance-to-lost-learning calculator that serves its pastoral/parental-engagement purpose well: a single slider drives live stats, three coherent charts, RAG banding and good accessibility (aria-live summary, labelled canvases). Interactivity is real but narrow (one input recomputing everything), and some research claims are stated assertively without sourcing.
subject: Behaviour & Pastoral
name: Attendance Impact Calculator

rationale: A genuinely interactive KS3/KS4 PSHE decision-tree with four well-written scenarios (peer pressure, online safety, anger, substances), animated SVG branching, immediate short/long-term consequence feedback, rewind, and a scored summary that fits statutory RSHE well. It is held back by a shallow colour-to-points scoring model that nudges towards a single "right answer" rather than genuine deliberation, plus minor defects (invalid CSS text-align: centre, and brittle treeNodes indexing in rewind).
fixIt: Pedagogy: replace the simplistic green=3/amber=2/red=1 score with reflective feedback that probes reasoning (e.g. ask the pupil to justify a choice before revealing consequences, and surface trade-offs rather than a percentage), so it builds decision-making skill instead of reward-seeking.
subject: Behaviour & Pastoral
name: Consequence Decision Tree

rationale: A clean, genuinely interactive teaching/CPD tool that correctly models the Attainment 8 slot structure (10 slots, English and Maths double-weighted), correct P8 banding, and a clear info panel, making it a solid explainer of how P8 works. It loses ground on accuracy: the expected-A8 lookup is a fabricated linear ramp rather than real DfE estimates by KS2 group, and the element scores divide by 2/2/3/3 instead of the true /10 denominator, so element figures are inflated and do not sum to the headline P8.
fixIt: Alignment: replace the made-up linear expectedA8Lookup with published DfE estimates of average Attainment 8 by KS2 prior-attainment band, and compute all four element subscores using the standard /10 denominator (not /2 and /3) so they correctly sum to the overall Progress 8 score.
subject: Data & Analytics
name: Progress 8 Calculator

rationale: A polished, genuinely functional intervention-monitoring tool: real CRUD, live before/after score entry, correctly computed Cohen's d (pooled SD), hand-rolled canvas charts, cost framing, CSV/print export and notably strong accessibility (ARIA live descriptions per chart). Fit for SLT/data-lead professional use, but accuracy is dented by a fabricated linear "EEF months of progress" mapping and an effect-size interpreter that uses absolute value, so a declining intervention can be labelled "Large/Medium".
fixIt: Alignment: the EEF months figure is invented (Math.round(d * 10/12/14)); EEF does not convert Cohen's d to months linearly. Either use the EEF's actual published banding or relabel it clearly as a rough heuristic, and fix interpretEffect so negative d is reported as a negative/declining effect rather than a positive "Medium/Large" via Math.abs.
subject: Data & Analytics
name: Intervention Impact Tracker

rationale: A genuinely useful staff CPD/data-literacy tool for exploring achievement gaps by gender, PP, EAL, SEN and ethnicity, with accurate gap maths, click-to-drill-down, an intersectionality heat matrix, and sound framing (correct protected-characteristics list, EEF Toolkit impacts). It loses marks for a dead "Stacked" chart toggle that has no effect, an additive (non-switching) trend view, and a "Score distribution" column that is really just a raw percentage bar.
subject: Data & Analytics
name: Demographic Performance Breakdown

rationale: A polished, robust KS1 imaginative-play tool with genuine drag/place/speak manipulation, pointer-capture handling for whiteboards and tablets, and warm child-friendly visuals that map well to the KS1 drama strand of "participate in role play and improvise". Pedagogically it stays at low cognitive demand: children pick from canned speech lines rather than generating or sequencing their own dialogue, so it supports free play more than structured storytelling or character development.
subject: Drama
name: Puppet Theatre

rationale: A polished, accurate KS2 word-class drill: words are shown in context sentences with immediate, colour-coded feedback and clean responsive design. It stays at recognition level, every word has a single unambiguous class, and it never tackles the core KS2 misconception that words shift class by context, so cognitive demand is modest.
fixIt: Pedagogy: add words whose class changes with context (e.g. "run", "light") so pupils must reason from the sentence rather than memorise the word, and make wrong-answer feedback explain the word's function rather than just "try again".
subject: English
name: Word Class Sorter

rationale: A well-built KS5 comparative-essay scaffold whose paired evidence plus dedicated comparative-analysis box directly targets the genuine comparison the boards reward, countering the common "two texts side by side" error. It is a structured workspace rather than a feedback-giving tutor, and a grab-cursor on paragraph headers implies drag-reorder that is never wired up.
fixIt: Interactivity: feedback is organisational only; the tool never evaluates content. Add lightweight formative checks (e.g. flag when a paragraph has evidence but no comparative analysis, or no comparative connective in the analysis), and either implement the implied drag-to-reorder or remove the misleading grab cursor.
subject: English
name: Comparative Essay Planner

rationale: A clean, attractive and genuinely interactive KS1 plant simulator with distinct per-stage SVG growth and a building diary, mapping well onto the Year 2 "plants need water and light" objectives. Pedagogy is held back because stage progression uses lifetime cumulative water/sun rather than ongoing care, so a child can splurge once then press Next Day repeatedly and still flower, weakening the cause-and-effect link and leaving no under/over-watering consequence.
fixIt: Pedagogy: make care ongoing rather than cumulative. Require each day's water/sun meters to be in a healthy band to progress, and add a visible wilt/droop state (and recovery) when the plant is neglected or over-watered, so the "plants need regular water and light" concept and its misconceptions are actually felt.
subject: Science
name: Plant Growth Diary

rationale: A polished, robust EYFS weather-and-clothing matcher with genuine selection mechanics, misconception-aware feedback (it names the implausible pick), and age-appropriate visuals; pitched correctly for EYFS Understanding the World as a precursor to KS1 seasonal geography. Cognitive demand is modest and some clothing rules are debatable (jumper for rain, trainers for wind), keeping pedagogy solid rather than exceptional.
subject: Geography
name: Weather Dressing

rationale: A well-pitched KS2 historical-enquiry quiz across four well-chosen sources, with accurate content, misconception-aware questions and immediate explanatory feedback that maps cleanly to the National Curriculum's "how we know about the past" aims. It is held back by being recognition-only multiple choice with several joke distractors and no genuine source-examination interaction beyond clicking, plus minor accessibility gaps.
fixIt: Interactivity (3): the "artefact" is only a static emoji and the magnifier is decorative, so it is just an MCQ. Add a real source-interrogation step (zoomable image/clickable hotspots, or a short typed/sorted "what does this tell us / what can't it tell us" task) and replace the joke distractors with plausible ones to raise cognitive demand.
subject: History
name: Primary Source Detective

rationale: A genuinely useful KS2 MFL sentence-builder: three accurate target languages, English glosses, pronunciation guides, drag-free tap-to-build with distractor words and immediate marking, which maps well onto KS2 MFL "speak in sentences" and "broaden vocabulary". Held back by a language-switch reuse bug (stale window.shuffledWords/lastIdx can mismatch the word bank) and some single-word phrases that make "building" trivial.
fixIt: Technical: reset window.lastIdx/shuffledWords on language change so the word bank always reflects the current language; also scope shuffledWords to app state rather than the global window object to avoid leakage.
subject: Modern Foreign Languages
name: Weather Reporter

rationale: A well-pitched KS1 RE matching activity covering six religions across symbols, clothing, artefacts, buildings and books, with accurate content, a consistent "belonging" framing, read-aloud support and two-attempt scaffolding that reveals teaching either way. It is competent but pedagogically modest: a single recognition/recall format with little progression in cognitive demand and no genuine drag-or-sort manipulation, so it sits solidly in the upper B band rather than reaching A.
fixIt: Interactivity is just multiple-choice recognition. Move beyond tap-to-pick: add a drag-to-sort mode (place several signs into the right community) or ask pupils to find the odd-one-out within a religion, so the task involves real manipulation and comparison rather than four-button selection each round.
subject: Religious Education
name: Signs of Belonging

rationale: A robust, well-structured tool whose 5-step guided flow mirrors the CPA mastery approach for bar models and whose Y3-6 progression (part-whole to fractions of amounts to ratio) is well pitched to the England NC. It is let down by forcing comparison problems into a part-whole structure (with "Same as Ava"/"More" labels) rather than a true two-bar comparison model, which misrepresents a canonical method, and the Free-mode answer check ignores the model itself.
subject: Maths
name: Bar Model Problem Solver

rationale: Targets a genuine, well-chosen GCSE weakness ("show that / prove that / explain why") with three distinct, working interaction modes, sentence frames, mark schemes and common-mistakes panels that are accurate and sensibly pitched across Foundation/Higher. Marred by a lenient, gameable free-text checker, an "explain the error" box that is never assessed, and mouse-only drag-and-drop that degrades on classroom tablets.
subject: Maths
name: Justify Your Answer

rationale: A polished, genuinely interactive EYFS RE game with drag-to-feel un-blur/zoom mechanics, reveal cards, sensory descriptions, progress stars and strong keyboard/ARIA support that fits the EYFS sensory-exploration pitch well. It is pulled below A-tier by a factual mislabel ("Diva lamp" instead of "Diya"), emoji stand-ins rather than true artefact images, and low cognitive demand with no misconception work.
subject: Religious Education
name: Mystery Artefact Box

rationale: A polished, accessible KS1 pulse-raiser with genuine manipulation (effort slider, move-specific exertion boosts, animated character, body-response checklist) and a misconception-aware predict-the-pulse task with tailored feedback. The heart-rate/oxygen science sits more squarely in KS2 science than the KS1 PE National Curriculum (which centres on movement and active lifestyles, not explaining cardiovascular response), so alignment is slightly above-pitch though accurate and cross-curricular.
fixIt: Reframe to the KS1 PE NC remit: foreground "master basic movements" and "be active", and either flag the heart-rate explanation as a KS2 science extension or simplify the oxygen/blood language to "heart works harder so we breathe faster" to keep it at the KS1 pitch.
subject: Physical Education
name: Warm-Up Wizard

rationale: A well-conceived KS2 comparative-region tool with genuine manipulation (region pickers, three sliders, live bars, plain-English verdict, TTS and an auto-MCQ challenge) and curated contrasting data that maps cleanly onto place-knowledge curriculum aims. It is held back by a misleading temperature bar that draws negative temps (e.g. Quebec -10C) at the same length as positive ones, a half-implemented "All land use" slider that silently just shows farmland, and a falsely advertised slider-question link (question type is in fact random).
fixIt: Technical: fix setTemp so sub-zero temperatures are visually distinguishable (offset the bar around a zero baseline or colour/flag negatives) rather than using Math.abs, which makes a freezing month look as long as a warm one; and make the "All land use" position render a genuine stacked/all-types view instead of silently falling back to farmland.
subject: Geography
name: Region Compare Lab

rationale: A polished, well-built KS3 Pythagoras tool with four genuinely interactive sections (dynamic squares, drag/keyboard rearrangement, missing-side detective challenge with the a+b misconception trap caught, and a converse/right-angle test). The headline "proof" is overstated: it is a fixed 3-4-5 area-decomposition (9 blue + two green rectangles tiling 25) showing the numbers sum, not a true geometric dissection of the squares, and the sliders in Part 1 are disconnected from it.
subject: Maths
name: Pythagoras Proof Squares

rationale: A genuinely interactive AFOREST annotation tool with five varied fictional passages, live tally/bar chart, and per-word correct/missed feedback that maps well to KS4 GCSE English Language persuasion skills. It is held back by being recognition-only (no analysis-of-effect, the skill GCSE actually rewards), brittle exact-span/exact-technique marking that penalises valid near-miss boundaries, and click-only annotation with no keyboard accessibility.
fixIt: Pedagogy and technical: accept overlapping/adjacent valid spans rather than exact word-index matches so pupils are not marked wrong for correctly identifying a device on slightly different boundaries, and add a short follow-up prompt asking pupils to explain the intended effect of one device they found (moving from spotting to analysis). Add keyboard support (tabindex/role/ARIA on word spans) for accessibility.
subject: English
name: Persuasive Techniques Spotter

rationale: Linguistically rich and accurate: ten well-chosen classic ambiguities correctly sorted into lexical/syntactic/scope/pragmatic with precise explanations (gerund vs adjective, wide/narrow scope, vocative comma), well pitched as KS4 language stretch. Interactivity is genuine but undermined by a broken reorder path (dropping a word clears all stress and commas) and fragile trigger-matching, so the "move clauses and commas" feedback loop only partly works.
fixIt: Fix the drag-drop so reordering remaps (not wipes) stress/comma state, and make interpretation highlighting respond to word order and commas, not only stress. Tighten matchesTrigger so exactly one reading lights up per configuration, and make tokens keyboard-operable with ARIA for accessibility.
subject: English
name: Ambiguity Explorer

rationale: A well-built, attractive drag-and-drop shape sorter that maps cleanly onto EYFS shape recognition and naming with sensible progressive difficulty and immediate, meaningful feedback; cognitive demand stays low and accessibility for pre-readers (no audio, drag-only, no keyboard route) holds it back from the top tier.
fixIt: Add spoken audio that names each shape on correct placement (and a tap-to-hear-the-name option) so genuinely pre-literate EYFS children get the naming reinforcement rather than relying on the written toast; also provide a click/keyboard alternative to drag-only input and ARIA labels on the shape divs.
subject: Maths
name: Shape Sorter

rationale: A polished, well-built KS3 chronology tool with accurate, curriculum-aligned event packs (WW1, Tudors, Civil Rights), parallel thematic tracks, zoom and a cause-effect arrow tool. Its pedagogy is undercut because events auto-snap to their correct dates and "Check Order" only validates category, never sequencing, so the headline skill the tool claims to teach is done by the software, not the student.
fixIt: Pedagogy/interactivity: stop auto-snapping events to their true dates. Let students place events at a position they choose along the timeline and have "Check Order" actually score chronological accuracy (the order-check loop at lines 1023-1027 is empty). Without genuine sequencing the core "Sequencing/turning points" learning aim is not exercised.
subject: History
name: Timeline Builder

rationale: A polished six-mode tool (compare, Venn, timeline, spectrum, drag-and-drop sort, quiz) with genuinely misconception-aware content built around the strong recurring concept that the revolution/rebellion label depends on outcome and who writes the history. Held back by US-centric examples and US spelling that fit England's KS4 spec only loosely, plus a lenient/buggy sort-game score and pins that overlap on narrow screens.
fixIt: Alignment: replace US spellings (labeled, localized, organized) with UK forms and add England-relevant KS4 case studies (e.g. Peasants' Revolt, 1745, Russian Revolution as already present) to anchor the GCSE pitch. Technical: fix the sort-game scoring so removing or re-placing a chip recalculates the score honestly, and add responsive handling to the spectrum pins so labels do not overlap on mobile.
subject: History
name: Revolution vs Rebellion

rationale: A polished, robust digital tableau builder with genuine manipulation (drag, pose-cycling, thought-tracking bubbles, freeze capture) that maps well onto KS1 drama conventions; however it is purely a creation sandbox with no guided prompts, modelling or feedback to drive the "analyse" half of its brief, so the cognitive demand and learning purpose stay shallow.
fixIt: Pedagogy/alignment: drama freeze frames are a physical, embodied technique and the description promises "guided prompts" plus "analyse" which this tool lacks. Add scene-specific structured prompts (e.g. "Who is the most important person? Show how this character feels through their body."), a reflection/discussion question on freeze, and ideally a contrast/before-and-after pair so children interpret meaning rather than just arrange stick figures.
subject: Drama
name: Freeze Frames

rationale: A polished, well-pitched EYFS sequencing activity with six varied stories, real drag-and-drop on both desktop and touch, a guaranteed non-trivial shuffle, and clear progress tracking that genuinely supports ordering and retelling objectives. It is held back by shallow failure feedback (a shake but no indication of which cards are misplaced or why), no read-aloud support for pre-readers, and no keyboard/ARIA accessibility on the draggable cards.
subject: English
name: Story Sequencing Cards

rationale: An attractive, age-appropriate EYFS senses explorer with genuine tap-and-filter interactivity, accurate multi-sensory mappings and pleasing feedback, well pitched to Understanding the World. It stays at reveal-and-read level, however: the sense filter poses questions but never checks the child's choice, so there is no formative loop or real cognitive demand.
fixIt: Pedagogy/technical: turn the filter into a graded task (e.g. "tap all the things you can hear" with right/wrong feedback and a self-check) to add cognitive demand; add real audio for hearing objects, and make object cards proper buttons with keyboard/ARIA support and alt text rather than emoji-only divs.
subject: Science
name: Senses Explorer

rationale: Accurate, well-pitched KS2 content with genuine click-to-explore nodes/processes and a meaningful self-marking quiz, making it worth lesson time. It loses marks because interactivity is reveal-text rather than manipulating the cycle, keyboard/ARIA accessibility is weak, and the generic "student records/grades" demo-data modal is irrelevant and confusing in a rock-cycle context.
subject: Science
name: Rock Cycle Explorer

rationale: A genuinely interactive two-mode app (Learn facts and a tap-to-place Label Game) with clear KS1 purpose, immediate feedback, shuffled labels and progress tracking that covers the seven continents and five oceans named in the NC. It is let down by a terminology slip ("Australasia" instead of the curriculum-standard Australia/Oceania), the non-semantic clickable divs being keyboard-inaccessible, and mismatched "student records/grades/attendance" demo-modal boilerplate.
subject: Geography
name: Continents and Oceans

rationale: A clean, well-pitched PECE scaffold that targets the highest A-Level AOs (counter-argument and substantiated judgement) and maps soundly onto exam-board assessment criteria. It is a structured worksheet rather than a tutor: it echoes input without any evaluative feedback, and unescaped template-literal injection makes the preview/inputs fragile to quote and markup characters.
fixIt: Interactivity/technical: add lightweight formative feedback (e.g. flag empty evaluation fields or counters shorter than the point), and HTML-escape para.topic and all field values before inserting into template literals so quotes and angle brackets don't break the input/preview. Add real <label> elements to the textareas for accessibility.
subject: History
name: Essay Argument Builder

rationale: A polished, age-appropriate listening app: five languages with TTS playback, tap-to-repeat greetings, a four-option listening game with reveal-the-answer feedback, plus graceful no-audio fallback, keyboard support and ARIA labels. Pedagogy and alignment are capped because it is essentially aural exposure and matching rather than building toward NC language objectives, and MFL is non-statutory at EYFS so it sits as enrichment rather than a curriculum-mapped unit; cognitive demand stays low (recognition only, no production or progression).
subject: Modern Foreign Languages
name: Hello World

rationale: A clean, robust multiple-choice matching quiz across French, Spanish and German with accurate vocabulary, gendered articles, pronunciation hints and immediate feedback. Cognitive demand stays low (pure visual recognition, the emoji plus English label hands over the meaning) and there is no audio, which is a notable gap for early MFL phonics; alignment is softened by MFL being statutory only at KS2 in England.
subject: Modern Foreign Languages
name: Animal Names

rationale: A well-built, misconception-aware EYFS number app covering addition, subtraction, sharing and comparison within 10, with genuine feedback, supportive emoji scenes and a hint feature. However it is entirely text-dependent despite a catalogue promise to "listen" to stories, so it cannot work for EYFS non-readers without an adult reading aloud.
fixIt: Add text-to-speech (Web Speech API) so each story and the "Help me" hint can be read aloud on a tap; this is essential for the EYFS pre-reader audience and the only way the "listen to the story" premise and true independent age-appropriateness are met.
subject: Maths
name: Story Problems

rationale: A genuinely interactive, well-structured tool: six Polya/Schoenfeld heuristics, worked examples with step reveal and auto-play, then strategy-choice plus guided input-checked practice across 31 bronze/silver/gold problems mapped to KS3 topics. It is dragged down by content-accuracy defects, most damagingly problem #8 whose constraints have no solution and whose "Review" step is a 600-word record of the author failing to solve it then saying "let me just fix the problem" (never fixed), with the practice path falsely marking an answer complete.
fixIt: Alignment: fix or remove problem #8 (mean/median/mode/range constraints are inconsistent, no valid solution) and redesign problem #9 so the sweets answer is a whole number rather than 4.5/7.5; replace the rambling failed "Review" narration with a clean correct one. Technical: make the Step 1 strategy choice carry real consequence (currently any strategy is accepted as "this could work"), and audit all worked-step text for the stream-of-consciousness left in #8.
subject: Maths
name: Problem-Solving Strategies

rationale: A well-structured Plan/Solve/Review tool with 40 grade-banded GCSE problems, genuine M/A/B mark-scheme coding and misconception-aware insights, but it only checks the final numeric answer per step (the plan, topic tags and method-description boxes are never assessed) and its digit-only parser mis-marks legitimate fraction answers like 4/15 or 2/3 while rewarding bare stored tokens.
fixIt: Technical/interactivity: replace the strip-to-digits parseNum with proper fraction and equivalent-form handling (accept 4/15, 2/3, etc. and compare numerically), store the true final answer rather than a numerator/constant token for ids 10, 16, 38, 40, and actually assess the plan steps and "what are you doing?" descriptions so the method scaffold the app teaches is marked rather than merely displayed.
subject: Maths
name: GCSE Problem Lab

rationale: A genuinely strong synthesis bank of ~20 multi-topic A-level problems with topic identification, strategic planning, step mark schemes, commentary and alternative methods, pitched accurately for KS5. It is held back because the per-step "Check" only reveals the expected answer rather than marking the student's input, "Mark as Complete" awards full marks unconditionally, and a couple of worked solutions contain blemishes (left-in "Wait, let me redo" scratch text in the partial-fractions step; an alt-method root that contradicts the main solution).
fixIt: Interactivity/technical: make per-step "Check" actually compare the student's answer (numeric/symbolic tolerance) and give correct/incorrect feedback instead of unconditionally revealing the model answer, and gate "Mark as Complete" on real progress rather than auto-awarding all marks. Also clean the t1_3 answer string (remove the "Wait, let me redo" working) and reconcile the t1_1 alt-method root with the main solution.
subject: Maths
name: A-Level Synthesis Problems

rationale: A polished, well-built EYFS whole-class performance prompt that lights up rhyme lines with bobbing action icons and clear singing/movement cues, accessible (ARIA labels, focus management, keyboard support) and pitched well for Expressive Arts and Design / Communication. However cognitive demand is low: tapping a line simply reveals scripted praise and marks it done with no judgement of pupil action, so it is closer to a guided slideshow than a task with genuine feedback.
subject: Drama
name: Action Song Stage

rationale: A clean, well-pitched EYFS comparison game with correct UK vocabulary ("fewer"), an equal option, two modes and immediate playful feedback, making it genuinely usable for early-number teaching. It stops short of excellence because it is select-an-answer rather than manipulate-the-quantity, offers no counting scaffolds or "how do you know" reasoning, and does not probe the classic EYFS misconception that wider spacing means more.
fixIt: Deepen pedagogy and interactivity: let children build/adjust each group (add or remove objects to make it equal), and at least sometimes vary object size or spacing so the "more space = more" misconception is challenged rather than always using identical emoji in tidy rows. Add optional number labels or a count-with-me reveal so it teaches one-to-one correspondence, not just visual guessing.
subject: Maths
name: More or Fewer?

rationale: A clean, responsive tap-to-build food-chain tool across three habitats with colour-coded trophic roles, energy-flow arrows, immediate feedback and a misconception prompt about ordering, but its acceptance logic passes any producer-start chain with increasing trophic order regardless of whether each predator actually eats its prey (e.g. Seaweed -> Crab -> Seal -> Shark), which can reinforce false feeding links. Solid Year 4/6 fit, though vocabulary (apex predator, secondary consumer) leans toward KS3 and there are accuracy slips (acorn labelled a producer; phytoplankton/zooplankton conflated as "Plankton").
fixIt: Tighten validation so non-whitelisted chains are not auto-accepted on trophic order alone: require each adjacent pair to be a real predator-prey link (define an eats-map per habitat) rather than just strictly increasing order. Also reclassify "Acorn" as part of the oak/producer tier rather than a standalone producer, and give Small Fish vs Tuna distinct icons. The catalogue promises drag-and-drop and food "webs", but the app is tap-to-add single linear chains only: either add branching web-building or correct the description. For technical, make organism cards keyboard-focusable buttons with ARIA labels so the tool is operable without a pointer.
subject: Science
name: Food Chain Builder

rationale: A clean, age-appropriate EYFS "Past and Present" tap-to-flip explorer that maps accurately onto the Understanding the World ELG and bridges to KS1 changes within living memory, with immediate per-side explanations and reward feedback. It stays at the level of guided exploration rather than requiring any judgement or sorting, and one card has a genuine bug.
fixIt: The "Keep Food Cold" card uses the same ice-cube emoji for both old (Ice Box) and new (Fridge Freezer), so the flip shows an identical icon: swap the "Today" side to a fridge glyph. Pedagogy/interactivity would also rise above guided exploration if a child had to make a choice (e.g. drag/tap to decide which is "then" vs "now") rather than just flipping. Accessibility: make the flip element a real button with keyboard support and aria-label, as it is currently a non-focusable div.
subject: History
name: Then and Now

rationale: A clean, responsive tap-to-sort game with immediate, meaningful feedback, correct-answer reveal and a satisfying loop that genuinely engages KS1 pupils on past-versus-present. It is held back by a fuzzy "long ago vs today" binary that risks the misconception that old things (castles, candles, swords) no longer exist, drifting from the NC's "within/beyond living memory" framing, plus a heavy reliance on reading and emoji-only icons with no audio or aria support for non-readers.
subject: History
name: Old and New Sort

rationale: A clean, accurate three-language classroom-phrases tool with pronunciation guides and a working scored multiple-choice quiz that fits the KS2 languages curriculum well. It is held back by shallow cognitive demand (recognition-only quiz, no production or audio), a dead "tap a phrase to explore" affordance in Learn mode, and a mismatched demo-data modal that talks about student records and grades.
fixIt: Learn mode: make the phrase cards actually do something (the modeLabel promises "tap to explore" but tapping does nothing) by adding Web Speech API audio playback so pronunciation can be heard, since this is a pronunciation tool with no sound. Quiz: add an English-to-target recall direction so it tests more than passive recognition. Technical: replace the irrelevant "student records/grades/attendance" demo-modal text with wording suited to an MFL app, and add an aria-live region for quiz feedback.
subject: Modern Foreign Languages
name: Classroom Phrases

rationale: A polished, accessible KS1 MFL listen-and-echo tool with quality native TTS, syllable-beat modelling and a well-judged whole-class call-and-response mode, but its headline "sound meter" feedback is simulated via Math.random rather than listening to the child, so the central pronunciation loop is theatre. Sound enrichment value with clean engineering; the faked accuracy feedback caps its pedagogical and interactive ceiling.
subject: Modern Foreign Languages
name: Say It Like a Native
